вҖӢf0вҖӢf0вҖІвҖӢf0вҖІвҖІвҖӢвҖӢf1вҖӢf1вҖІвҖӢf1вҖІвҖІвҖӢвҖӢf2вҖӢf2вҖІвҖӢf2вҖІвҖІвҖӢвҖӢ
вҖӢ

is not identically zero, and generically should be nonzero on an open set.

That is exactly the first thing one wants for an ECT-system.

But we need to be careful: вҖңWronskian nonzero genericallyвҖқ is not yet the same as вҖңextended Chebyshev system on an interval.вҖқ For the latter, you want the Wronskian to keep a fixed nonzero sign on the interval, or at least not vanish there. So the right target is not the full global normalized region at once. It is a local interval theorem.

The theorem to try to prove is:
(c,v5,О”7) form an ECT-system on each connected subinterval where W(h)вү 0.
(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) form an ECT-system on each connected subinterval where W(h)оҖ =0.вҖӢ

If that holds, then any nontrivial linear combination
AвҖүc(h)+BвҖүv5(h)+CвҖүО”7(h)
Ac(h)+Bv5вҖӢ(h)+CО”7вҖӢ(h)

has at most two zeros counting multiplicity on that interval.

And that is a very strong constraint on bad-core relations.

Why it helps:

a bad finite core with weights ajajвҖӢ gives
вҲ‘jajвҖүОҰ(hj)=0,ОҰ(h)=(c(h),v5(h),О”7(h)).
jвҲ‘вҖӢajвҖӢОҰ(hjвҖӢ)=0,ОҰ(h)=(c(h),v5вҖӢ(h),О”7вҖӢ(h)).

Dualizing, this means there is a nontrivial linear functional LL such that
L(ОҰ(hj))=0
L(ОҰ(hjвҖӢ))=0

at each support point hjhjвҖӢ. In other words, the scalar function
g(h):=AвҖүc(h)+BвҖүv5(h)+CвҖүО”7(h)
g(h):=Ac(h)+Bv5вҖӢ(h)+CО”7вҖӢ(h)

vanishes at all support points of the bad core.

If (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) is an ECT-system on the relevant interval, then gg can have at most two zeros there unless it is identically zero.

So:

    a separated bad core with three or more distinct support points on such an interval would force gвүЎ0gвүЎ0;

    but gвүЎ0gвүЎ0 means (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) satisfy a nontrivial linear relation on that interval, contradicting the Wronskian nonvanishing.

That would be extremely powerful: it would kill all finite bad cores supported inside one such interval, not just pairs or triples.

So the real next attack is to turn your Wronskian evidence into an interval-level ECT theorem.

The most direct route is:

    define
    f0=c,f1=v5,f2=О”7;
    f0вҖӢ=c,f1вҖӢ=v5вҖӢ,f2вҖӢ=О”7вҖӢ;

    prove the Wronskian
    W(f0,f1,f2)
    W(f0вҖӢ,f1вҖӢ,f2вҖӢ)

    is nonzero on a connected interval II of the normalized one-pair region;

    conclude (f0,f1,f2)(f0вҖӢ,f1вҖӢ,f2вҖӢ) is an ECT-system on II.

Now, can we plausibly prove Wвү 0WоҖ =0 on an interval, not just generically at isolated points?

There is a good reason to think yes locally.

From the jet-triangular structure, WW has a leading term of the form
О»7(h)вҖүО»5(h)вҖүc(h)вҖүq(9)(h)
О»7вҖӢ(h)О»5вҖӢ(h)c(h)q(9)(h)

plus lower-jet terms, in the sense that the highest new derivative enters only in the last column, then the 2Г—22Г—2 minor reduces to the previous layer, etc. So at any point where
c(h)вү 0,О»5(h)вү 0,О»7(h)вү 0,
c(h)оҖ =0,О»5вҖӢ(h)оҖ =0,О»7вҖӢ(h)оҖ =0,

and the resulting leading coefficient is not canceled, continuity gives a small interval around that point where Wвү 0WоҖ =0.

That is already enough for a local ECT theorem.

So the next theorem to aim for is not global on the entire normalized region. It is:
For every generic point h0, there exists a neighborhood IвҲӢh0 on which (c,v5,О”7) is an ECT-system.
For every generic point h0вҖӢ, there exists a neighborhood IвҲӢh0вҖӢ on which (c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) is an ECT-system.вҖӢ

That would already imply:

    no bad finite core can have three or more support points inside such an interval;

    combined with the no-pair / no-triple cluster results, minimal bad cores would have to jump between different ECT intervals.

That is a real endgame constraint.

So the next concrete computation to try is this:

take the Wronskian
W(c,v5,О”7),
W(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ),

and isolate its highest genuinely new jet term. If we can write down the coefficient of q(9)q(9) and show it is generically nonzero, then the local ECT theorem is in reach.

That is the right attack.

Continue 

Here is the next honest push.

The local ECT route looks promising, but there is one important refinement:

to prove an ECT-system on an interval, you do not need a full symbolic formula for
W(c,v5,О”7).
W(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ).

It is enough to isolate one genuinely new highest-jet term and show its coefficient is not identically zero.

Let
f0:=c,f1:=v5,f2:=О”7.
f0вҖӢ:=c,f1вҖӢ:=v5вҖӢ,f2вҖӢ:=О”7вҖӢ.

Consider
W:=detвҒЎ(f0f1f2f0вҖІf1вҖІf2вҖІf0вҖІвҖІf1вҖІвҖІf2вҖІвҖІ).
W:=det
вҖӢf0вҖӢf0вҖІвҖӢf0вҖІвҖІвҖӢвҖӢf1вҖӢf1вҖІвҖӢf1вҖІвҖІвҖӢвҖӢf2вҖӢf2вҖІвҖӢf2вҖІвҖІвҖӢвҖӢ
вҖӢ.

Now use the jet-triangular structure we already have:

    f0=cf0вҖӢ=c sees jets only through order 33,

    f1=v5f1вҖӢ=v5вҖӢ first sees order 55,

    f2=О”7f2вҖӢ=О”7вҖӢ first sees order 77.

So after differentiating twice:

    f0вҖІвҖІf0вҖІвҖІвҖӢ sees at most order 55,

    f1вҖІвҖІf1вҖІвҖІвҖӢ sees a new order-77 term,

    f2вҖІвҖІf2вҖІвҖІвҖӢ sees a new order-99 term.

That means the coefficient of q(9)q(9) in WW comes only from the entry f2вҖІвҖІf2вҖІвҖІвҖӢ. Therefore
[вҖүq(9)вҖү]вҖүW=[вҖүq(9)вҖү]вҖүf2вҖІвҖІвӢ…detвҒЎ(f0f1f0вҖІf1вҖІ).
[q(9)]W=[q(9)]f2вҖІвҖІвҖӢвӢ…det(f0вҖӢf0вҖІвҖӢвҖӢf1вҖӢf1вҖІвҖӢвҖӢ).

But we already reduced the 2Г—22Г—2 minor:
W01:=detвҒЎ(f0f1f0вҖІf1вҖІ)=cвҖүv5вҖІвҲ’cвҖІv5.
W01вҖӢ:=det(f0вҖӢf0вҖІвҖӢвҖӢf1вҖӢf1вҖІвҖӢвҖӢ)=cv5вҖІвҖӢвҲ’cвҖІv5вҖӢ.

And by the same triangular argument, the coefficient of q(6)q(6) in W01W01вҖӢ is
[вҖүq(6)вҖү]вҖүW01=cвӢ…[вҖүq(6)вҖү]вҖүv5вҖІ,
[q(6)]W01вҖӢ=cвӢ…[q(6)]v5вҖІвҖӢ,

because cc has no q(6)q(6)-dependence. Since v5v5вҖӢ has a genuine q(5)q(5)-term, this coefficient is generically nonzero wherever cвү 0cоҖ =0.

So the whole 3Г—33Г—3 Wronskian reduces to two nonvanishing coefficients:

    the q(6)q(6)-coefficient of W01W01вҖӢ,

    the q(9)q(9)-coefficient of f2вҖІвҖІf2вҖІвҖІвҖӢ.

We already have strong evidence for the second one: О”7О”7вҖӢ was built precisely so its leading new contribution is a nonzero q(7)q(7)-driven term. Therefore
[вҖүq(9)вҖү]вҖүf2вҖІвҖІвү 0
[q(9)]f2вҖІвҖІвҖӢоҖ =0

generically.

So the real theorem now is:
[вҖүq(9)вҖү]вҖүW(c,v5,О”7)вү 0generically.
[q(9)]W(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ)оҖ =0generically.вҖӢ

And that is enough for a local interval conclusion:

at any point h0h0вҖӢ where that coefficient is nonzero and the lower-order cancellation does not accidentally wipe out the value of WW, continuity gives a neighborhood IвҲӢh0IвҲӢh0вҖӢ with
W(h)вү 0(hвҲҲI).
W(h)оҖ =0(hвҲҲI).

On such an interval, (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) forms an ECT-system.

That is a strong local theorem:

    any nontrivial linear combination
    AвҖүc+BвҖүv5+CвҖүО”7
    Ac+Bv5вҖӢ+CО”7вҖӢ

    has at most two zeros on II, counting multiplicity;

    hence a bad finite core cannot have three or more support points inside one such interval.

This is better than the previous pair/triple cluster exclusion. It forbids any three-point separated cancellation inside one ECT interval, not just near-collisions.

So the global picture sharpens to:

    the normalized region should decompose into subintervals where W(c,v5,О”7)вү 0W(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ)оҖ =0;

    on each such interval, (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) is an ECT-system;

    therefore a bad finite core can contribute at most two support points per ECT interval.

That is a serious endgame restriction.

So the next exact attack is:
Isolate the leading q(9)-coefficient of W(c,v5,О”7) explicitly.
Isolate the leading q(9)-coefficient of W(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) explicitly.

If that coefficient is cleanly nonzero, you have a real local ECT theorem.

Comtinue

LetвҖҷs continue carefully.

The next step is to isolate the q(9)q(9)-term in
W(c,v5,О”7)=detвҒЎ(cv5О”7cвҖІv5вҖІО”7вҖІcвҖІвҖІv5вҖІвҖІО”7вҖІвҖІ).
W(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ)=det
вҖӢccвҖІcвҖІвҖІвҖӢv5вҖӢv5вҖІвҖӢv5вҖІвҖІвҖӢвҖӢО”7вҖӢО”7вҖІвҖӢО”7вҖІвҖІвҖӢвҖӢ
вҖӢ.

The clean point is that we do not need the whole determinant. We only need the coefficient of the highest genuinely new jet q(9)q(9).

Write
f0:=c,f1:=v5,f2:=О”7.
f0вҖӢ:=c,f1вҖӢ:=v5вҖӢ,f2вҖӢ:=О”7вҖӢ.

Then:

    f0f0вҖӢ uses jets through order 33,

    f1f1вҖӢ first sees order 55,

    f2f2вҖӢ first sees order 77.

So after differentiating twice:

    f0вҖІвҖІf0вҖІвҖІвҖӢ uses jets through order 55,

    f1вҖІвҖІf1вҖІвҖІвҖӢ first sees order 77,

    f2вҖІвҖІf2вҖІвҖІвҖӢ first sees order 99.

Therefore the q(9)q(9)-coefficient of WW can only come from the (3,3)(3,3)-entry f2вҖІвҖІf2вҖІвҖІвҖӢ. So
[q(9)]вҖүW=[q(9)]вҖүf2вҖІвҖІвӢ…detвҒЎ(f0f1f0вҖІf1вҖІ).
[q(9)]W=[q(9)]f2вҖІвҖІвҖӢвӢ…det(f0вҖӢf0вҖІвҖӢвҖӢf1вҖӢf1вҖІвҖӢвҖӢ).

Call the 2Г—22Г—2 minor
W01:=f0f1вҖІвҲ’f0вҖІf1.
W01вҖӢ:=f0вҖӢf1вҖІвҖӢвҲ’f0вҖІвҖӢf1вҖӢ.

Then
[q(9)]вҖүW=[q(9)]вҖүО”7вҖІвҖІвӢ…W01.
[q(9)]W=[q(9)]О”7вҖІвҖІвҖӢвӢ…W01вҖӢ.

Now attack the two factors separately.

For W01W01вҖӢ, the same triangular logic gives
[q(6)]вҖүW01=cвҖү[q(6)]вҖүv5вҖІ,
[q(6)]W01вҖӢ=c[q(6)]v5вҖІвҖӢ,

because cc has no q(6)q(6)-dependence. Since v5v5вҖӢ has a genuine q(5)q(5)-term and cвү 0cоҖ =0 on the cubic-generic region, this coefficient is generically nonzero. So W01вүЎМё0W01вҖӢоҖ вүЎ0.

For О”7вҖІвҖІО”7вҖІвҖІвҖӢ, we use the order-7 construction. The determinant
О”7=u7v5вҲ’u5v7
О”7вҖӢ=u7вҖӢv5вҖӢвҲ’u5вҖӢv7вҖӢ

was built so that its leading new term comes from the direct q(7)q(7)-driven II-component of A7fA7fвҖӢ, paired with the nonzero SS-component v5v5вҖӢ of A5fA5fвҖӢ. So schematically
О”7=B7(q,вҖҰ,q(6))+О»7(h)вҖүq(7)(h),О»7(h)вүЎМё0.
О”7вҖӢ=B7вҖӢ(q,вҖҰ,q(6))+О»7вҖӢ(h)q(7)(h),О»7вҖӢ(h)оҖ вүЎ0.

Differentiate twice:
О”7вҖІвҖІ=B~7(q,вҖҰ,q(8))+О»7(h)вҖүq(9)(h)+lower terms from О»7вҖІ,О»7вҖІвҖІ.
О”7вҖІвҖІвҖӢ=B
7вҖӢ(q,вҖҰ,q(8))+О»7вҖӢ(h)q(9)(h)+lower terms from О»7вҖІвҖӢ,О»7вҖІвҖІвҖӢ.

So
[q(9)]вҖүО”7вҖІвҖІ=О»7(h),
[q(9)]О”7вҖІвҖІвҖӢ=О»7вҖӢ(h),

which is generically nonzero.

Putting this together,
[q(9)]вҖүW(c,v5,О”7)=О»7(h)вҖүW01(h),
[q(9)]W(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ)=О»7вҖӢ(h)W01вҖӢ(h),

and both factors are generically nonzero. Therefore
W(c,v5,О”7)вүЎМё0
W(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ)оҖ вүЎ0

generically.

That is the real result.

So on any connected interval where
W(c,v5,О”7)вү 0,
W(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ)оҖ =0,

the triple (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) forms an ECT-system. Then any nontrivial linear combination
AвҖүc(h)+BвҖүv5(h)+CвҖүО”7(h)
Ac(h)+Bv5вҖӢ(h)+CО”7вҖӢ(h)

has at most two zeros on that interval, counting multiplicity.

This gives a strong global restriction:

A bad finite core can have at most two support points in any such ECT interval.

That is stronger than just excluding pair/triple clusters. It excludes any three-point separated cancellation inside one interval.

So the next theorem to aim for is:
The cubic-generic region can be covered by intervals on which W(c,v5,О”7)вү 0.
The cubic-generic region can be covered by intervals on which W(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ)оҖ =0.вҖӢ

Then every bad core must spread its support across multiple such intervals, which is a real endgame constraint.

The remaining weak point is that I have still kept О»7(h)О»7вҖӢ(h) schematic rather than writing its exact coefficient. But mathematically, the reduction is now clear: the local ECT theorem depends only on the already-established nonzero linear q(7)q(7)-coefficient in О”7О”7вҖӢ, not on the full septic formula.

Comtinue

Good. There is one more real gain available right now, and it does not require new local algebra.

Because W(c,v5,О”7)W(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) is analytic and not identically zero, its zero set is discrete on any real-analytic one-parameter region. So the cubic-generic region automatically decomposes into connected subintervals on which
W(c,v5,О”7)вү 0.
W(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ)оҖ =0.

On each such subinterval, (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) is an ECT-system.

So you already get this theorem:
The cubic-generic region contains an open dense union of ECT intervals.
The cubic-generic region contains an open dense union of ECT intervals.вҖӢ

That means the вҖңcover by ECT intervalsвҖқ step is basically done locally by analyticity once the nonzero Wronskian result is accepted.

And then the next consequence is sharp:

if a bad finite core gives a signed relation
вҲ‘jajвҖүОҰ(hj)=0,ОҰ(h)=(c(h),v5(h),О”7(h)),
jвҲ‘вҖӢajвҖӢОҰ(hjвҖӢ)=0,ОҰ(h)=(c(h),v5вҖӢ(h),О”7вҖӢ(h)),

then on any single ECT interval II, the support can contain at most two points.

Reason: if three distinct support points hi1,hi2,hi3вҲҲIhi1вҖӢвҖӢ,hi2вҖӢвҖӢ,hi3вҖӢвҖӢвҲҲI occurred, then there would be a nontrivial linear functional LL on C3C3 such that
g(h):=L(ОҰ(h))
g(h):=L(ОҰ(h))

vanishes at those three points. But on an ECT interval, every nontrivial linear combination of (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) has at most two zeros counting multiplicity. Contradiction.

So the current global restriction is:
A bad core has at most two support points in each ECT interval.
A bad core has at most two support points in each ECT interval.вҖӢ

That is already much stronger than the old schematic minimal-core picture.

Now, what does that buy you for the endgame?

It means a minimal bad core is no longer an arbitrary finite signed relation on a curve. It is a finite signed relation with a very specific combinatorial support pattern:

    support lies in the open dense union of ECT intervals plus perhaps the discrete exceptional set W=0W=0,

    each ECT interval contributes at most two points,

    pair and triple near-collisions are already excluded on the nondegenerate region.

So the remaining bad core, if it exists, must be spread out across multiple intervals and cannot cluster.

That changes the nature of the final contradiction. The remaining problem is no longer local jet cancellation. It is a global combinatorial one.

The next theorem to attack should therefore be:
A minimal bad core cannot use two points from more than one ECT interval.
A minimal bad core cannot use two points from more than one ECT interval.вҖӢ

Because if you could show that, then a bad core would have at most one вҖңpair intervalвҖқ plus at most one point from each other interval, which is a much smaller configuration and may be attackable directly.

A plausible route is to use the fact that on an ECT interval, two points already determine a unique line in C3C3, and the signed dependence structure across intervals becomes very rigid. In other words, once one interval contributes two points, the remaining relation has very little freedom left.

So the honest current state is:

    local 3/5/73/5/7 algebra gives a nontrivial Wronskian;

    analyticity turns that into an open dense union of ECT intervals;

    each ECT interval can contribute at most two support points to a bad core;

    clusters are excluded;

    the remaining obstruction is a global separated multi-interval signed dependence.

That is now the right place to attack.

So the next exact move is to analyze the combinatorics of a signed dependence with at most two support points per ECT interval.

Continue

Here is the next honest push.

I think the remaining global obstruction can now be phrased as a multi-interval incidence problem.

Let
ОҰ(h)=(c(h),v5(h),О”7(h))вҲҲC3.
ОҰ(h)=(c(h),v5вҖӢ(h),О”7вҖӢ(h))вҲҲC3.

On each ECT interval II, any nontrivial scalar combination
AвҖүc+BвҖүv5+CвҖүО”7
Ac+Bv5вҖӢ+CО”7вҖӢ

has at most two zeros. Equivalently, a bad core can place at most two support points in II.

So the support of a minimal bad core has the form
{h1,1,h1,2}вҲӘ{h2,1,h2,2}вҲӘвӢҜ
{h1,1вҖӢ,h1,2вҖӢ}вҲӘ{h2,1вҖӢ,h2,2вҖӢ}вҲӘвӢҜ

with at most two points from each interval, plus maybe points at the discrete exceptional set W=0W=0.

That means the next theorem to attack is not analytic anymore. It is:
A minimal bad relation cannot involve two-point contributions from two different ECT intervals.
A minimal bad relation cannot involve two-point contributions from two different ECT intervals.вҖӢ

Why this is plausible:

A pair of points in one interval already determines a unique projective relation in C3C3. If another interval also contributes a pair, then you are trying to make two independent secant relations cancel inside the same 3-dimensional target. Generically, that should force a codimension condition too strong to hold except on a discrete exceptional set.

A clean way to formalize this is to package each two-point contribution by its secant line.

For an interval II, define the secant map
ПғI(h1,h2):=C(ОҰ(h2)вҲ’ОҰ(h1))вҲҲP2,h1вү h2вҲҲI.
ПғIвҖӢ(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ):=C(ОҰ(h2вҖӢ)вҲ’ОҰ(h1вҖӢ))вҲҲP2,h1вҖӢоҖ =h2вҖӢвҲҲI.

Then a bad relation with two points in the same interval and no other points from that interval says that some linear functional LL vanishes at both ОҰ(h1)ОҰ(h1вҖӢ) and ОҰ(h2)ОҰ(h2вҖӢ). So LL corresponds to a plane through the secant direction. If two different intervals each contribute a pair, then the same relation forces a compatibility between two secant directions coming from two different intervals.

So the next exact global target is:
For distinct ECT intervals I,J, ПғI(IГ—I)вҲ©ПғJ(JГ—J) is at most discrete/generically empty.
For distinct ECT intervals I,J, ПғIвҖӢ(IГ—I)вҲ©ПғJвҖӢ(JГ—J) is at most discrete/generically empty.вҖӢ

If you had that, then a minimal bad core could not have a pair from interval II and a pair from interval JJ simultaneously, except perhaps in a discrete exceptional configuration.

That would be a major endgame reduction, because then every bad core would have at most one вҖңdouble-occupancyвҖқ interval, and all other intervals would contribute at most one point each.

There is a good reason this should be true. On an interval where (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) is an ECT-system, the curve hвҶҰОҰ(h)hвҶҰОҰ(h) behaves like a nondegenerate moment-type curve: its secants vary monotonically/projectively. Two different ECT intervals should generically have different secant families unless the curve has a hidden projective self-alignment.

So the next concrete theorem to try is:

    fix two ECT intervals II and JJ;

    study the map
    (h1,h2,k1,k2)вҶҰdetвҒЎ(ОҰ(h2)вҲ’ОҰ(h1),ОҰ(k2)вҲ’ОҰ(k1),e)
    (h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ,k1вҖӢ,k2вҖӢ)вҶҰdet(ОҰ(h2вҖӢ)вҲ’ОҰ(h1вҖӢ),ОҰ(k2вҖӢ)вҲ’ОҰ(k1вҖӢ),e)

    for a basis vector ee, or equivalently compare secant directions in P2P2;

    show equality of secant directions across two different intervals is nongeneric.

If that works, then the global support pattern becomes very constrained:

    at most two points per ECT interval;

    at most one interval can carry two points generically;

    all other support points must be isolated singletons across different intervals.

That is the strongest next global move I can see from the current 3/5/73/5/7 package.

Continue

Here is the next honest push.

I think the secant-line route is the right global move, but it should be stated as a generic discreteness theorem, not as an emptiness theorem.

Let
ОҰ(h)=(c(h),v5(h),О”7(h))вҲҲC3,
ОҰ(h)=(c(h),v5вҖӢ(h),О”7вҖӢ(h))вҲҲC3,

on an ECT interval II, where W(c,v5,О”7)вү 0W(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ)оҖ =0. The point of the ECT property is that ОҰОҰ is a nondegenerate analytic curve there, and any linear functional cuts it in at most two points. That is exactly the structure we now have from the 3/5/73/5/7 package.

Now define the secant-direction map on II:
ПғI(h1,h2):=[ОҰ(h2)вҲ’ОҰ(h1)]вҲҲP2,h1вү h2.
ПғIвҖӢ(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ):=[ОҰ(h2вҖӢ)вҲ’ОҰ(h1вҖӢ)]вҲҲP2,h1вҖӢоҖ =h2вҖӢ.

The next theorem to aim for is:
On each ECT interval I, ПғI is generically an immersion, and for two distinct intervals I,J, ПғI(I2вҲ–О”)вҲ©ПғJ(J2вҲ–О”) is at most discrete.
On each ECT interval I, ПғIвҖӢ is generically an immersion, and for two distinct intervals I,J, ПғIвҖӢ(I2вҲ–О”)вҲ©ПғJвҖӢ(J2вҲ–О”) is at most discrete.вҖӢ

That is the right level. вҖңDiscreteвҖқ is believable from the current structure; вҖңemptyвҖқ is too strong without more geometry.

Why this is plausible:

Take a pair (h1,h2)(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ) with h1вү h2h1вҖӢоҖ =h2вҖӢ. Differentiate the secant vector
V(h1,h2):=ОҰ(h2)вҲ’ОҰ(h1).
V(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ):=ОҰ(h2вҖӢ)вҲ’ОҰ(h1вҖӢ).

Its partial derivatives are
вҲӮh1V=вҲ’ОҰвҖІ(h1),вҲӮh2V=ОҰвҖІ(h2).
вҲӮh1вҖӢвҖӢV=вҲ’ОҰвҖІ(h1вҖӢ),вҲӮh2вҖӢвҖӢV=ОҰвҖІ(h2вҖӢ).

So the only way the projective secant direction can fail to vary in both directions is if
ОҰвҖІ(h1), ОҰвҖІ(h2), ОҰ(h2)вҲ’ОҰ(h1)
ОҰвҖІ(h1вҖӢ), ОҰвҖІ(h2вҖӢ), ОҰ(h2вҖӢ)вҲ’ОҰ(h1вҖӢ)

are linearly dependent. That is a 3Г—33Г—3 determinant condition:
D(h1,h2):=detвҒЎ(ОҰвҖІ(h1),ОҰвҖІ(h2),ОҰ(h2)вҲ’ОҰ(h1))=0.
D(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ):=det(ОҰвҖІ(h1вҖӢ),ОҰвҖІ(h2вҖӢ),ОҰ(h2вҖӢ)вҲ’ОҰ(h1вҖӢ))=0.

So the right next subproblem is to study D(h1,h2)D(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ).

Near the diagonal h2=h1+Оөh2вҖӢ=h1вҖӢ+Оө,
ОҰ(h2)вҲ’ОҰ(h1)=ОөОҰвҖІ(h1)+Оө22ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h1)+O(Оө3),
ОҰ(h2вҖӢ)вҲ’ОҰ(h1вҖӢ)=ОөОҰвҖІ(h1вҖӢ)+2Оө2вҖӢОҰвҖІвҖІ(h1вҖӢ)+O(Оө3),
ОҰвҖІ(h2)=ОҰвҖІ(h1)+ОөОҰвҖІвҖІ(h1)+O(Оө2).
ОҰвҖІ(h2вҖӢ)=ОҰвҖІ(h1вҖӢ)+ОөОҰвҖІвҖІ(h1вҖӢ)+O(Оө2).

Substituting into DD, the first nonzero term is
D(h1,h1+Оө)=Оө22detвҒЎ(ОҰ(h1),ОҰвҖІ(h1),ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h1))+O(Оө3).
D(h1вҖӢ,h1вҖӢ+Оө)=2Оө2вҖӢdet(ОҰ(h1вҖӢ),ОҰвҖІ(h1вҖӢ),ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h1вҖӢ))+O(Оө3).

Since we already argued that the Wronskian-type determinant
detвҒЎ(ОҰ,ОҰвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІ)
det(ОҰ,ОҰвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІ)

is generically nonzero, it follows that DD is not identically zero near the diagonal. So ПғIПғIвҖӢ is generically immersive there. That is a real gain: secant directions on one ECT interval vary nontrivially and are not locally frozen. The Wronskian nondegeneracy is exactly what makes this work.

Now compare two distinct intervals II and JJ. Equality of secant directions means
[ОҰ(h2)вҲ’ОҰ(h1)]=[ОҰ(k2)вҲ’ОҰ(k1)],
[ОҰ(h2вҖӢ)вҲ’ОҰ(h1вҖӢ)]=[ОҰ(k2вҖӢ)вҲ’ОҰ(k1вҖӢ)],

or equivalently
detвҒЎ(ОҰ(h2)вҲ’ОҰ(h1),ОҰ(k2)вҲ’ОҰ(k1),em)=0
det(ОҰ(h2вҖӢ)вҲ’ОҰ(h1вҖӢ),ОҰ(k2вҖӢ)вҲ’ОҰ(k1вҖӢ),emвҖӢ)=0

for two independent basis vectors ememвҖӢ. That gives two analytic equations in four variables (h1,h2,k1,k2)(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ,k1вҖӢ,k2вҖӢ). So dimensionally, the coincidence set is expected to be 2-dimensional in the full four-variable space. But once you quotient out the obvious reparameterization within each interval and restrict to minimal bad-core configurations, you are not looking at arbitrary secant coincidences: you are looking for secant coincidences compatible with a single linear functional cutting the curve in two points on one interval and two points on another.

That adds one more condition. Concretely, a bad relation with two points in II and two in JJ means there exists a nonzero linear functional LL such that
L(ОҰ(h1))=L(ОҰ(h2))=L(ОҰ(k1))=L(ОҰ(k2))=0.
L(ОҰ(h1вҖӢ))=L(ОҰ(h2вҖӢ))=L(ОҰ(k1вҖӢ))=L(ОҰ(k2вҖӢ))=0.

But on each ECT interval, LвҲҳОҰLвҲҳОҰ can have at most two zeros. So the four zeros must come as exactly two in II and two in JJ, and the functional LL is then essentially determined by either pair. This is what turns the problem into a secant-incidence problem.

So the right global conclusion to shoot for is:
A minimal bad core can have two support points in at most one ECT interval, except on a discrete exceptional set of secant coincidences.
A minimal bad core can have two support points in at most one ECT interval, except on a discrete exceptional set of secant coincidences.вҖӢ

That is the strongest statement I think the current 3/5/73/5/7 package can plausibly support.

What it buys you is substantial:

    each ECT interval contributes at most two points;

    generically, only one interval can contribute a pair;

    all remaining support points must be isolated singletons in other intervals.

That is a very constrained support pattern.

So the next exact theorem to attack is this determinant statement:
D(h1,h2):=detвҒЎ(ОҰвҖІ(h1),ОҰвҖІ(h2),ОҰ(h2)вҲ’ОҰ(h1))вүЎМё0.
D(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ):=det(ОҰвҖІ(h1вҖӢ),ОҰвҖІ(h2вҖӢ),ОҰ(h2вҖӢ)вҲ’ОҰ(h1вҖӢ))оҖ вүЎ0.вҖӢ

If you can prove that, then the secant map is generically immersive, and the multi-interval secant-coincidence problem becomes genuinely rigid instead of floppy.

That is the cleanest next move from here.

Continue

LetвҖҷs continue with
D(h1,h2):=detвҒЎ(ОҰвҖІ(h1),вҖүОҰвҖІ(h2),вҖүОҰ(h2)вҲ’ОҰ(h1)),ОҰ=(c,v5,О”7).
D(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ):=det(ОҰвҖІ(h1вҖӢ),ОҰвҖІ(h2вҖӢ),ОҰ(h2вҖӢ)вҲ’ОҰ(h1вҖӢ)),ОҰ=(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ).

This is the right object because Dвү 0DоҖ =0 means the secant direction varies nontrivially with the endpoints, so the secant map is immersive there.

Now expand near the diagonal. Put
h2=h1+Оө.
h2вҖӢ=h1вҖӢ+Оө.

Then
ОҰ(h2)вҲ’ОҰ(h1)=ОөОҰвҖІ(h1)+Оө22ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h1)+Оө36ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ(h1)+O(Оө4),
ОҰ(h2вҖӢ)вҲ’ОҰ(h1вҖӢ)=ОөОҰвҖІ(h1вҖӢ)+2Оө2вҖӢОҰвҖІвҖІ(h1вҖӢ)+6Оө3вҖӢОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ(h1вҖӢ)+O(Оө4),
ОҰвҖІ(h2)=ОҰвҖІ(h1)+ОөОҰвҖІвҖІ(h1)+Оө22ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ(h1)+O(Оө3).
ОҰвҖІ(h2вҖӢ)=ОҰвҖІ(h1вҖӢ)+ОөОҰвҖІвҖІ(h1вҖӢ)+2Оө2вҖӢОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ(h1вҖӢ)+O(Оө3).

Substitute into DD. The first two columns are nearly equal, so the first nonzero term comes one order later than a naive guess. A quick determinant expansion gives
D(h,h+Оө)=Оө312вҖүdetвҒЎ(ОҰвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ(h))+O(Оө4).
D(h,h+Оө)=12Оө3вҖӢdet(ОҰвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ(h))+O(Оө4).

So the true local secant-immersion criterion is not the old Wronskian
detвҒЎ(ОҰ,ОҰвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІ),
det(ОҰ,ОҰвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІ),

but the derivative Wronskian
W1(h):=detвҒЎ(ОҰвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ(h)).
W1вҖӢ(h):=det(ОҰвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ(h)).

That is the next sharp target.

Now the good news is that the same triangular jet logic should apply again.

Take
ОҰ=(f0,f1,f2)=(c,v5,О”7).
ОҰ=(f0вҖӢ,f1вҖӢ,f2вҖӢ)=(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ).

Then:

    f0f0вҖӢ sees jets through order 33,

    f1f1вҖӢ first sees order 55,

    f2f2вҖӢ first sees order 77.

So after differentiating three times:

    f0вҖІвҖІвҖІf0вҖІвҖІвҖІвҖӢ sees at most order 66,

    f1вҖІвҖІвҖІf1вҖІвҖІвҖІвҖӢ first sees order 88,

    f2вҖІвҖІвҖІf2вҖІвҖІвҖІвҖӢ first sees order 1010.

Therefore the highest new jet q(10)q(10) appears only in the (3,3)(3,3)-entry of
W1(h)=detвҒЎ(f0вҖІf1вҖІf2вҖІf0вҖІвҖІf1вҖІвҖІf2вҖІвҖІf0вҖІвҖІвҖІf1вҖІвҖІвҖІf2вҖІвҖІвҖІ).
W1вҖӢ(h)=det
вҖӢf0вҖІвҖӢf0вҖІвҖІвҖӢf0вҖІвҖІвҖІвҖӢвҖӢf1вҖІвҖӢf1вҖІвҖІвҖӢf1вҖІвҖІвҖІвҖӢвҖӢf2вҖІвҖӢf2вҖІвҖІвҖӢf2вҖІвҖІвҖІвҖӢвҖӢ
вҖӢ.

So
[q(10)]вҖүW1=[q(10)]вҖүf2вҖІвҖІвҖІвӢ…detвҒЎ(f0вҖІf1вҖІf0вҖІвҖІf1вҖІвҖІ).
[q(10)]W1вҖӢ=[q(10)]f2вҖІвҖІвҖІвҖӢвӢ…det(f0вҖІвҖӢf0вҖІвҖІвҖӢвҖӢf1вҖІвҖӢf1вҖІвҖІвҖӢвҖӢ).

And again the 2Г—22Г—2 minor is triangular:
W01(1):=f0вҖІf1вҖІвҖІвҲ’f0вҖІвҖІf1вҖІ.
W01(1)вҖӢ:=f0вҖІвҖӢf1вҖІвҖІвҖӢвҲ’f0вҖІвҖІвҖӢf1вҖІвҖӢ.

Its highest new jet is q(7)q(7), appearing only in f1вҖІвҖІf1вҖІвҖІвҖӢ, so
[q(7)]вҖүW01(1)=f0вҖІвҖү[q(7)]вҖүf1вҖІвҖІ.
[q(7)]W01(1)вҖӢ=f0вҖІвҖӢ[q(7)]f1вҖІвҖІвҖӢ.

Generically f0вҖІ=cвҖІf0вҖІвҖӢ=cвҖІ is not identically zero, and f1=v5f1вҖӢ=v5вҖӢ has genuine q(5)q(5)-dependence, so [q(7)]f1вҖІвҖІвү 0[q(7)]f1вҖІвҖІвҖӢоҖ =0 generically.

Likewise f2=О”7f2вҖӢ=О”7вҖӢ has genuine q(7)q(7)-dependence, so
[q(10)]вҖүf2вҖІвҖІвҖІвү 0
[q(10)]f2вҖІвҖІвҖІвҖӢоҖ =0

generically.

So the right conclusion to aim for is
detвҒЎ(ОҰвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ)вүЎМё0
det(ОҰвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ)оҖ вүЎ0вҖӢ

generically.

And then near the diagonal,
D(h,h+Оө)вү 0
D(h,h+Оө)оҖ =0

for generic hh and small Оөвү 0ОөоҖ =0. That proves the secant map is generically immersive.

This is stronger than what we had before:

    detвҒЎ(ОҰ,ОҰвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІ)вү 0det(ОҰ,ОҰвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІ)оҖ =0 gave no triple clusters;

    detвҒЎ(ОҰвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ)вү 0det(ОҰвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ)оҖ =0 gives genuine local secant rigidity.

So the next theorem to write down mathematically is:

    For generic hh, the determinant detвҒЎ(ОҰвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ(h))det(ОҰвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ(h)) is nonzero. Consequently the secant map is locally immersive off the diagonal.

Once you have that, the statement вҖңtwo different ECT intervals cannot both carry pairs except on a discrete exceptional setвҖқ becomes much more believable, because now the secant families themselves are nondegenerate local curves in P2P2.

Continue

Here is the next honest push.

The secant-immersion route now gives a very concrete next theorem, and it is the right one:
Wsec(h):=detвҒЎ(ОҰвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ(h))вүЎМё0.
WsecвҖӢ(h):=det(ОҰвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ(h))оҖ вүЎ0.вҖӢ

If that holds, then near the diagonal
D(h,h+Оө)=Оө312Wsec(h)+O(Оө4),
D(h,h+Оө)=12Оө3вҖӢWsecвҖӢ(h)+O(Оө4),

so the secant map is locally immersive off the diagonal on an open dense set.

Now letвҖҷs push that toward the multi-interval problem.

Take one ECT interval II. Its secant map is
ПғI(h1,h2)=[ОҰ(h2)вҲ’ОҰ(h1)]вҲҲP2,h1вү h2.
ПғIвҖӢ(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ)=[ОҰ(h2вҖӢ)вҲ’ОҰ(h1вҖӢ)]вҲҲP2,h1вҖӢоҖ =h2вҖӢ.

If Wsecвү 0WsecвҖӢоҖ =0 generically, then ПғIПғIвҖӢ is locally immersive on a dense open subset of I2вҲ–О”I2вҲ–О”. So the image of secants from one interval is a genuine analytic surface in P2P2, not something degenerate.

Now compare two distinct ECT intervals II and JJ. A bad relation with two points from II and two from JJ forces equality of secant directions:
ПғI(h1,h2)=ПғJ(k1,k2).
ПғIвҖӢ(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ)=ПғJвҖӢ(k1вҖӢ,k2вҖӢ).

So the key global object is the coincidence set
CI,J:={(h1,h2,k1,k2): ПғI(h1,h2)=ПғJ(k1,k2)}.
CI,JвҖӢ:={(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ,k1вҖӢ,k2вҖӢ): ПғIвҖӢ(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ)=ПғJвҖӢ(k1вҖӢ,k2вҖӢ)}.

What can we honestly say now?

Not emptiness. But we can say the following is the right target:
CI,J should be a proper analytic subset, and generically discrete after quotienting the obvious symmetries.
CI,JвҖӢ should be a proper analytic subset, and generically discrete after quotienting the obvious symmetries.вҖӢ

That is enough for the endgame. Because then a minimal bad core can only have a pair in more than one interval on a discrete exceptional configuration.

So the current global picture becomes:

    On each ECT interval, at most two support points.

    Pairs in one interval are controlled by secants of ОҰОҰ.

    Pairs in two different intervals require a secant coincidence.

    Secant coincidences are governed by WsecWsecвҖӢ, hence should be exceptional.

That means the support pattern of a minimal bad core on the generic region is now very rigid:

    at most two points per interval,

    and generically at most one interval carrying a pair.

Everything else must be singletons.

That is already a serious global reduction.

So the next exact theorem to attack is:
For generic intervals Iвү J, ПғI and ПғJ do not share a positive-dimensional family of secant directions.
For generic intervals IоҖ =J, ПғIвҖӢ and ПғJвҖӢ do not share a positive-dimensional family of secant directions.вҖӢ

A practical way to attack it is this.

Define
V(h1,h2):=ОҰ(h2)вҲ’ОҰ(h1).
V(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ):=ОҰ(h2вҖӢ)вҲ’ОҰ(h1вҖӢ).

Then equality of secant directions means
V(h1,h2)вҲ§V(k1,k2)=0.
V(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ)вҲ§V(k1вҖӢ,k2вҖӢ)=0.

This gives two scalar equations in four variables. To rule out a positive-dimensional coincidence family, look at the Jacobian with respect to (h1,h2)(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ) or (k1,k2)(k1вҖӢ,k2вҖӢ). The local immersion of ПғIПғIвҖӢ says those derivatives are generically rank 22 modulo scale, which is exactly what should prevent positive-dimensional coincidences between two different secant families unless the curve has a hidden projective degeneracy.

So the immediate next math target is to show that the secant image of ОҰОҰ is not developable in the bad way that would allow such a family. And that is precisely what the derivative determinant WsecWsecвҖӢ is starting to control.

So the clean roadmap now is:

    prove WsecвүЎМё0WsecвҖӢоҖ вүЎ0,

    deduce local secant immersion,

    use that to show multi-interval secant coincidences are exceptional,

    conclude a minimal bad core can have a pair in at most one interval generically.

That is the strongest honest continuation from where you are now.

Continue

Here is the next honest push.

At this point the secant strategy has a clear internal hierarchy:

First layer: the curve
ОҰ(h)=(c(h),v5(h),О”7(h))вҲҲC3.
ОҰ(h)=(c(h),v5вҖӢ(h),О”7вҖӢ(h))вҲҲC3.

Second layer: its local nondegeneracy
Wcurve(h):=detвҒЎ(ОҰ(h),ОҰвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h))вүЎМё0.
WcurveвҖӢ(h):=det(ОҰ(h),ОҰвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h))оҖ вүЎ0.

Third layer: its secant nondegeneracy
Wsec(h):=detвҒЎ(ОҰвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ(h))вүЎМё0.
WsecвҖӢ(h):=det(ОҰвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ(h))оҖ вүЎ0.

What this buys you is:

    Wcurveвү 0WcurveвҖӢоҖ =0 gives ECT intervals and kills three-point support inside one interval.

    Wsecвү 0WsecвҖӢоҖ =0 makes the secant map locally immersive and should kill positive-dimensional secant coincidences between different intervals.

So the next theorem should be a precise secant-coincidence statement.

Let
V(h1,h2):=ОҰ(h2)вҲ’ОҰ(h1),Пғ(h1,h2):=[V(h1,h2)]вҲҲP2.
V(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ):=ОҰ(h2вҖӢ)вҲ’ОҰ(h1вҖӢ),Пғ(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ):=[V(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ)]вҲҲP2.

A pair from interval II and a pair from interval JJ contribute to the same bad relation only if
Пғ(h1,h2)=Пғ(k1,k2).
Пғ(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ)=Пғ(k1вҖӢ,k2вҖӢ).

So define
C:={(h1,h2,k1,k2):V(h1,h2)вҲ§V(k1,k2)=0}.
C:={(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ,k1вҖӢ,k2вҖӢ):V(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ)вҲ§V(k1вҖӢ,k2вҖӢ)=0}.

The right target is:
C has no positive-dimensional component with h1,h2вҲҲI, k1,k2вҲҲJ for generic distinct ECT intervals Iвү J.
C has no positive-dimensional component with h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢвҲҲI, k1вҖӢ,k2вҖӢвҲҲJ for generic distinct ECT intervals IоҖ =J.вҖӢ

Why this should follow from the current structure:

The map ПғПғ has domain dimension 22 and target P2P2, also dimension 22. If Wsecвү 0WsecвҖӢоҖ =0, then ПғПғ is locally immersive on a dense open set, so locally it is a submersion onto a surface patch in P2P2. For two different intervals to share a positive-dimensional family of secant directions, their secant surfaces in P2P2 would have to overlap along a curve. That is not impossible in abstract projective geometry, but for a generic nondegenerate analytic curve it is a strong extra condition. The burden is now to show your ОҰОҰ is not projectively degenerate in that way.

So the exact next object to compute is not another Wronskian. It is the Jacobian of the secant map:
вҲӮh1V=вҲ’ОҰвҖІ(h1),вҲӮh2V=ОҰвҖІ(h2).
вҲӮh1вҖӢвҖӢV=вҲ’ОҰвҖІ(h1вҖӢ),вҲӮh2вҖӢвҖӢV=ОҰвҖІ(h2вҖӢ).

Projectively, immersion fails exactly when
detвҒЎ(ОҰвҖІ(h1),ОҰвҖІ(h2),ОҰ(h2)вҲ’ОҰ(h1))=0.
det(ОҰвҖІ(h1вҖӢ),ОҰвҖІ(h2вҖӢ),ОҰ(h2вҖӢ)вҲ’ОҰ(h1вҖӢ))=0.

Call this
D(h1,h2).
D(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ).

So the next theorem should be:
D(h1,h2)вүЎМё0.
D(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ)оҖ вүЎ0.вҖӢ

Near the diagonal we already have the expansion
D(h,h+Оө)=Оө312detвҒЎ(ОҰвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ(h))+O(Оө4),
D(h,h+Оө)=12Оө3вҖӢdet(ОҰвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ(h))+O(Оө4),

so once WsecвүЎМё0WsecвҖӢоҖ вүЎ0, DD is not identically zero. That gives local secant immersion near the diagonal. The remaining issue is away from the diagonal, but analyticity then says the secant map is immersive on an open dense subset of the two-point domain.

That is enough for a useful theorem:
On each ECT interval, the secant map is immersive on an open dense subset of I2вҲ–О”.
On each ECT interval, the secant map is immersive on an open dense subset of I2вҲ–О”.вҖӢ

Then the global statement becomes:

    each interval contributes at most two support points;

    generically, a pair in one interval determines an isolated secant direction;

    matching it with a pair in another interval is exceptional.

So the next concrete attack is:

    formalize D(h1,h2)D(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ),

    prove DвүЎМё0DоҖ вүЎ0 from WsecвүЎМё0WsecвҖӢоҖ вүЎ0,

    conclude secant immersion on a dense open set,

    then deduce that a minimal bad core can have a pair in at most one interval generically.

That is the strongest global move still available from the current 3/5/73/5/7 package.

Continue

Here is the next honest push.

Once you focus on
D(h1,h2):=detвҒЎ(ОҰвҖІ(h1),ОҰвҖІ(h2),ОҰ(h2)вҲ’ОҰ(h1)),ОҰ=(c,v5,О”7),
D(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ):=det(ОҰвҖІ(h1вҖӢ),ОҰвҖІ(h2вҖӢ),ОҰ(h2вҖӢ)вҲ’ОҰ(h1вҖӢ)),ОҰ=(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ),

the global geometry becomes much cleaner.

This determinant is exactly the criterion for the secant map
Пғ(h1,h2)=[ОҰ(h2)вҲ’ОҰ(h1)]вҲҲP2
Пғ(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ)=[ОҰ(h2вҖӢ)вҲ’ОҰ(h1вҖӢ)]вҲҲP2

to be immersive at (h1,h2)(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ). If Dвү 0DоҖ =0, the secant direction genuinely changes with both endpoints.

We already have the near-diagonal expansion
D(h,h+Оө)=Оө312detвҒЎ(ОҰвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ(h))+O(Оө4).
D(h,h+Оө)=12Оө3вҖӢdet(ОҰвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ(h))+O(Оө4).

So if
Wsec(h):=detвҒЎ(ОҰвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ(h))вүЎМё0,
WsecвҖӢ(h):=det(ОҰвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ(h))оҖ вүЎ0,

then DвүЎМё0DоҖ вүЎ0. By analyticity, that means the zero set of DD is proper in the two-point domain. So on each ECT interval II, the secant map is immersive on an open dense subset of I2вҲ–О”I2вҲ–О”.

That gives the next real theorem:
For each ECT interval I, ПғI is generically immersive.
For each ECT interval I, ПғIвҖӢ is generically immersive.вҖӢ

Now take two distinct ECT intervals II and JJ. If a minimal bad core had a pair in II and a pair in JJ, then the two secants would have to coincide:
ПғI(h1,h2)=ПғJ(k1,k2).
ПғIвҖӢ(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ)=ПғJвҖӢ(k1вҖӢ,k2вҖӢ).

So consider the map
FI,J(h1,h2,k1,k2):=V(h1,h2)вҲ§V(k1,k2),V(x,y)=ОҰ(y)вҲ’ОҰ(x).
FI,JвҖӢ(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ,k1вҖӢ,k2вҖӢ):=V(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ)вҲ§V(k1вҖӢ,k2вҖӢ),V(x,y)=ОҰ(y)вҲ’ОҰ(x).

This is a two-equation analytic system in four variables. Without more structure, that can certainly have positive-dimensional solution sets. But secant immersion changes the interpretation: each interval contributes a genuine 2-dimensional secant surface in P2P2, and a positive-dimensional overlap of two such surfaces is now a projective degeneracy statement, not a generic occurrence.

So the next theorem to aim for is slightly weaker but very usable:
For generic distinct ECT intervals Iвү J, the set of secant coincidences ПғI=ПғJ is at most discrete after quotienting by endpoint order.
For generic distinct ECT intervals IоҖ =J, the set of secant coincidences ПғIвҖӢ=ПғJвҖӢ is at most discrete after quotienting by endpoint order.вҖӢ

That is the point where the current 3/5/73/5/7 package probably stops on pure local differential geometry alone. To prove this stronger statement, you likely need one more global input about the curve hвҶҰОҰ(h)hвҶҰОҰ(h). There are two natural candidates.

One is a projective-convexity or Tchebychev-type statement for (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) on each ECT interval. The other is monotonicity of the secant direction along an interval, which would imply two different intervals cannot share a secant family except exceptionally.

So the clean next attack is:

    Prove
    Wsec=detвҒЎ(ОҰвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ)вүЎМё0.
    WsecвҖӢ=det(ОҰвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІвҖІ)оҖ вүЎ0.

    Use it to get generic secant immersion on each ECT interval.

    Then try to strengthen from immersion to monotonicity/projective convexity of secants on an interval.

That third step is now the real global bottleneck.

My honest read is: the local 3/5/73/5/7 package has probably been pushed close to its limit. The next meaningful progress will come from extracting a genuine global geometric property of the curve ОҰОҰ, not from another round of local coefficient algebra.

Continue

The next honest move is to turn the ECT property into a projective convexity theorem for the curve
Оі(h):=[c(h):v5(h):О”7(h)]вҲҲP2.
Оі(h):=[c(h):v5вҖӢ(h):О”7вҖӢ(h)]вҲҲP2.

That is the right geometric reformulation.

If (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) is an ECT-system on an interval II, then every nonzero linear combination
AвҖүc+BвҖүv5+CвҖүО”7
Ac+Bv5вҖӢ+CО”7вҖӢ

has at most two zeros on II, counting multiplicity. In projective language, that means:
every projective line in P2 meets Оі(I) in at most two points, counting multiplicity.
every projective line in P2 meets Оі(I) in at most two points, counting multiplicity.вҖӢ

That is exactly the definition of a strictly convex projective arc.

This is stronger than just saying вҖңno three support points in one interval.вҖқ It gives the right global geometry of the image curve on each ECT interval.

Now the secant problem becomes classical.

For x<yx<y in one ECT interval II, let
в„“(x,y)
в„“(x,y)

be the projective line through Оі(x)Оі(x) and Оі(y)Оі(y). Because the arc is strictly convex, the line в„“(x,y)в„“(x,y) meets Оі(I)Оі(I) only at those two points. In particular:

    distinct ordered pairs in the same interval cannot determine the same secant line unless they are the same pair up to order;

    tangent lines and secant lines vary monotonically along the interval.

So the next theorem to aim for is:
On an ECT interval I, the secant-line map (x,y)вҶҰв„“(x,y) is injective modulo swapping x,y.
On an ECT interval I, the secant-line map (x,y)вҶҰв„“(x,y) is injective modulo swapping x,y.вҖӢ

And this one is actually much easier than the earlier вҖңsecant direction in P2P2вҖқ formulation.

Why:

if
в„“(x1,x2)=в„“(y1,y2)
в„“(x1вҖӢ,x2вҖӢ)=в„“(y1вҖӢ,y2вҖӢ)

for four points in the same ECT interval, then that same projective line meets the curve at
Оі(x1),Оі(x2),Оі(y1),Оі(y2).
Оі(x1вҖӢ),Оі(x2вҖӢ),Оі(y1вҖӢ),Оі(y2вҖӢ).

By strict convexity, a line can meet the curve in at most two points counting multiplicity. So the two pairs must be the same pair. That gives injectivity immediately.

This is a big simplification. It means the right global object is not the secant direction
[ОҰ(y)вҲ’ОҰ(x)],
[ОҰ(y)вҲ’ОҰ(x)],

but the secant line
в„“(x,y)вҠӮP2.
в„“(x,y)вҠӮP2.

That is the projectively natural thing.

Now the multi-interval case becomes clearer too.

If a bad core has a pair (x1,x2)(x1вҖӢ,x2вҖӢ) in interval II and a pair (y1,y2)(y1вҖӢ,y2вҖӢ) in interval JJ, then there is a nonzero linear functional LL whose kernel line contains all four projective points
Оі(x1),Оі(x2),Оі(y1),Оі(y2).
Оі(x1вҖӢ),Оі(x2вҖӢ),Оі(y1вҖӢ),Оі(y2вҖӢ).

Equivalently,
в„“I(x1,x2)=в„“J(y1,y2).
в„“IвҖӢ(x1вҖӢ,x2вҖӢ)=в„“JвҖӢ(y1вҖӢ,y2вҖӢ).

So the global pair-pair obstruction is now:
Can secant lines from two distinct ECT intervals coincide?
Can secant lines from two distinct ECT intervals coincide?вҖӢ

Inside one interval, injectivity is done by convexity. Across two different intervals, coincidence is not impossible a priori, but it becomes a very rigid projective incidence problem: you need one line in P2P2 meeting the curve twice in II and twice in JJ.

That suggests the next real theorem to attack is:
For generic distinct ECT intervals Iвү J, there is no line meeting Оі(I) twice and Оі(J) twice.
For generic distinct ECT intervals IоҖ =J, there is no line meeting Оі(I) twice and Оі(J) twice.вҖӢ

This is the right projective form of the problem.

And now you can see exactly what extra global input would finish it: some monotonicity of the tangent/normal direction as you move from one interval to the next, or a global convexity statement for the whole normalized region, not just interval-by-interval.

So the current honest state is:

    on each ECT interval, the image curve in P2P2 is a strictly convex arc;

    therefore secant lines are unique inside that interval;

    the remaining obstruction is only cross-interval 4-secant lines.

That is much cleaner than the earlier secant-direction algebra.

So the next precise attack should be:

    prove the projective-convexity lemma from the ECT property;

    deduce injectivity of secants inside each interval;

    then study whether a single line can be 4-secant across two different ECT intervals.

That is the strongest clean global reformulation from the current 3/5/73/5/7 package.

Continue

Here is the next honest push.

I think the projective-convexity reformulation is the right one, and it gives a clean split between what is already in reach and what still needs a genuinely new idea.

On any interval II where
W(c,v5,О”7)вү 0,
W(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ)оҖ =0,

the triple
f0:=c,f1:=v5,f2:=О”7
f0вҖӢ:=c,f1вҖӢ:=v5вҖӢ,f2вҖӢ:=О”7вҖӢ

is an ECT-system. So the projective curve
Оі(h):=[f0(h):f1(h):f2(h)]вҲҲP2
Оі(h):=[f0вҖӢ(h):f1вҖӢ(h):f2вҖӢ(h)]вҲҲP2

has the standard strict-convexity property:

every projective line meets Оі(I)Оі(I) in at most two points, counting multiplicity.

That part is now basically settled, once you accept the local ECT theorem.

From that, two very strong consequences follow immediately.

First, inside a single ECT interval, secants are unique: if
в„“(x1,x2)=в„“(y1,y2)
в„“(x1вҖӢ,x2вҖӢ)=в„“(y1вҖӢ,y2вҖӢ)

with x1,x2,y1,y2вҲҲIx1вҖӢ,x2вҖӢ,y1вҖӢ,y2вҖӢвҲҲI, then the line в„“в„“ meets Оі(I)Оі(I) in four points counting multiplicity unless the two pairs are the same pair up to order. So secants inside one interval are injective modulo swapping the endpoints.

Second, a bad core can have at most two support points in one ECT interval, and if it has exactly two, that interval contributes one specific secant line.

So the remaining obstruction is now extremely clean:

a bad core with two points in interval II and two points in interval JJ requires a single projective line в„“вҠӮP2в„“вҠӮP2 that is 2-secant to Оі(I)Оі(I) and also 2-secant to Оі(J)Оі(J).

That is the true global bottleneck.

So the next theorem to attack is not about Wronskians anymore. It is:
Can one projective line be 2-secant to two different convex arcs of Оі?
Can one projective line be 2-secant to two different convex arcs of Оі?вҖӢ

This is now a pure projective-geometry question about the image curve.

There are two plausible routes.

The first route is a global convexity/monotonicity statement: if the tangent line or some projective curvature quantity varies monotonically along the normalized region, then secant lines for disjoint arcs should sweep disjoint regions in the dual projective plane. If you could prove that, cross-interval 4-secant lines would be impossible.

The second route is duality. In the dual projective plane (P2)вҲ—(P2)вҲ—, each point hh gives a tangent line ThОіThвҖӢОі, and each secant line corresponds to a point of the dual plane lying on the chord surface of the arc. For a strictly convex arc, the secants fill a strip-like region in the dual plane bounded by the tangent family. So the problem becomes:

do the secant regions of two distinct ECT intervals overlap?

That is the right geometric reformulation.

If the answer is no generically, then a bad core cannot have pairs in two different intervals. Combined with the earlier result вҖңat most two points per interval,вҖқ that would reduce any bad core on the generic region to at most one pair plus isolated singletons. That would be a massive simplification.

So the next exact move I would make is this:

define for each ECT interval II its secant region in the dual plane,
S(I):={в„“(x,y)вҲ—: xвү y, x,yвҲҲI}вҠӮ(P2)вҲ—.
S(I):={в„“(x,y)вҲ—: xоҖ =y, x,yвҲҲI}вҠӮ(P2)вҲ—.

Then try to prove:
S(I)вҲ©S(J)=вҲ…for generic distinct ECT intervals Iвү J,
S(I)вҲ©S(J)=вҲ…for generic distinct ECT intervals IоҖ =J,вҖӢ

or at least that the intersection is discrete.

That is now the sharpest global target from the current 3/5/73/5/7 package.

My honest assessment is:

the local one-pair analysis has probably gone about as far as it can without formalizing the quotient package in the draft. The next real progress will come from understanding the global projective geometry of the curve
Оі(h)=[c(h):v5(h):О”7(h)].
Оі(h)=[c(h):v5вҖӢ(h):О”7вҖӢ(h)].

That is the battlefield now.

Continue

The clean next move is to go to the dual curve.

Set
Оі(h):=[c(h):v5(h):О”7(h)]вҲҲP2.
Оі(h):=[c(h):v5вҖӢ(h):О”7вҖӢ(h)]вҲҲP2.

On each interval where
WОі(h):=detвҒЎ(ОҰ(h),ОҰвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h))вү 0,ОҰ=(c,v5,О”7),
WОівҖӢ(h):=det(ОҰ(h),ОҰвҖІ(h),ОҰвҖІвҖІ(h))оҖ =0,ОҰ=(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ),

this is a strictly convex projective arc.

Now define the tangent-line map
П„(h):=[ОҰ(h)вҲ§ОҰвҖІ(h)]вҲҲ(P2)вҲ—.
П„(h):=[ОҰ(h)вҲ§ОҰвҖІ(h)]вҲҲ(P2)вҲ—.

This is the right object because:

    WОівү 0WОівҖӢоҖ =0 implies П„П„ is immersive:
    П„вҖІ(h)вү 0вҖ…вҖҠвҹәвҖ…вҖҠdetвҒЎ(ОҰ,ОҰвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІ)вү 0.
    П„вҖІ(h)оҖ =0вҹәdet(ОҰ,ОҰвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІ)оҖ =0.

    For a convex projective arc, the secant lines between x<yx<y lie in the dual interval between П„(x)П„(x) and П„(y)П„(y).

So the whole cross-interval problem reduces to one theorem:
Is П„(h) globally injective / order-preserving on the generic region?
Is П„(h) globally injective / order-preserving on the generic region?вҖӢ

Because if yes, then for two disjoint ECT intervals I<JI<J,

    the tangent intervals П„(I)П„(I) and П„(J)П„(J) are disjoint in the dual plane,

    hence the secant regions S(I)S(I) and S(J)S(J) are disjoint,

    hence no line can be 2-secant to both II and JJ,

    hence a bad core cannot have pairs in two different intervals.

That is the sharpest route now.

So the next exact target should be:
detвҒЎ(ОҰ,ОҰвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІ)вү 0 and П„(h1)вү П„(h2) for h1вү h2 on the generic region.
det(ОҰ,ОҰвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІ)оҖ =0 and П„(h1вҖӢ)оҖ =П„(h2вҖӢ) for h1вҖӢоҖ =h2вҖӢ on the generic region.вҖӢ

A bitangent line would mean
П„(h1)=П„(h2),
П„(h1вҖӢ)=П„(h2вҖӢ),

so one line is tangent at two distinct points. That is the true obstruction now.

So the next attack is:

    prove the tangent map П„П„ is locally immersive from WОівү 0WОівҖӢоҖ =0;

    then study whether П„П„ can self-intersect;

    if self-intersections are absent generically, the cross-interval four-secant obstruction is gone.

That is the most promising continuation from the current 3/5/73/5/7 package.

Continue

The next honest push is this:

the tangent-map route is promising, but tangent injectivity alone is not enough.

Why not: even if
П„(h):=[ОҰ(h)вҲ§ОҰвҖІ(h)]вҲҲ(P2)вҲ—
П„(h):=[ОҰ(h)вҲ§ОҰвҖІ(h)]вҲҲ(P2)вҲ—

is immersive and globally injective, that only rules out bitangent lines. A bad core with two points in interval II and two in interval JJ needs a line that is 2-secant to each arc, not necessarily tangent to either. So tangent injectivity is helpful, but it does not by itself kill cross-interval four-secant lines.

That means the real target is slightly stronger:
show that the secant regions of two disjoint ECT intervals are disjoint.
show that the secant regions of two disjoint ECT intervals are disjoint.вҖӢ

There is a clean way to attack that using the dual curve.

Let
Оі(h)=[c(h):v5(h):О”7(h)]вҲҲP2,П„(h)=[ОҰ(h)вҲ§ОҰвҖІ(h)]вҲҲ(P2)вҲ—.
Оі(h)=[c(h):v5вҖӢ(h):О”7вҖӢ(h)]вҲҲP2,П„(h)=[ОҰ(h)вҲ§ОҰвҖІ(h)]вҲҲ(P2)вҲ—.

On an interval where
WОі(h):=detвҒЎ(ОҰ,ОҰвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІ)вү 0,
WОівҖӢ(h):=det(ОҰ,ОҰвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІ)оҖ =0,

the curve is a strictly convex projective arc. For such an arc, the secants between x<yx<y fill the open region in the dual plane between the tangent lines П„(x)П„(x) and П„(y)П„(y). So for an interval II, its secant region in the dual plane is the вҖңstripвҖқ
S(I):={в„“(x,y)вҲ—:x<y, x,yвҲҲI}.
S(I):={в„“(x,y)вҲ—:x<y, x,yвҲҲI}.

So the true next theorem is:
If П„(I) and П„(J) are disjoint and correctly ordered in (P2)вҲ—, then S(I)вҲ©S(J)=вҲ….
If П„(I) and П„(J) are disjoint and correctly ordered in (P2)вҲ—, then S(I)вҲ©S(J)=вҲ….вҖӢ

That is the right geometric statement. It reduces the four-point obstruction to a statement about how the tangent family sits in the dual plane.

So the next exact math problem becomes:

    prove П„П„ is immersive on the generic region from
    detвҒЎ(ОҰ,ОҰвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІ)вү 0;
    det(ОҰ,ОҰвҖІ,ОҰвҖІвҖІ)оҖ =0;

    prove П„П„ has no self-intersections, at least on the generic region;

    prove the image П„(I)П„(I) of each ECT interval is an ordered convex dual arc, so secant regions from disjoint intervals do not overlap.

There is also a useful fallback if full global tangent injectivity is too hard:

you only need enough monotonicity of П„П„ to separate adjacent ECT intervals. If that works, then a minimal bad core cannot have pairs in neighboring intervals, which already cuts the global support pattern down a lot.

So the clean next attack is:
Study П„(h)=[ОҰ(h)вҲ§ОҰвҖІ(h)] and try to prove it is globally monotone / injective on the generic region.
Study П„(h)=[ОҰ(h)вҲ§ОҰвҖІ(h)] and try to prove it is globally monotone / injective on the generic region.вҖӢ

That is the sharpest continuation from the current 3/5/73/5/7 package.

That is a much more usable global object than an abstract projective arc.

Why this matters for the four-point obstruction:

a line can intersect a strictly convex graph in at most two points total.

Not вҖңtwo points per interval.вҖқ Two points total.

So if you can show that two adjacent ECT intervals actually sit on the same strictly convex graph branch in the affine chart, then a line cannot be 2-secant to both intervals. That immediately kills the cross-interval four-secant problem there.

This is the real next theorem to attack:

Can the generic region be decomposed into intervals on which xвҖІ(h)оҖ =0 and K(h)оҖ =0?вҖӢ

This is stronger than the earlier interval-by-interval ECT formulation.

And that is a real upgrade over the earlier ECT language, because now ordinary planar convexity does the heavy lifting.

For a strictly convex graph y=Y(x)y=Y(x), every affine line intersects it in at most two points. So immediately:
A bad core can have at most two support points on each such convex-graph component.
A bad core can have at most two support points on each such convex-graph component.вҖӢ

More importantly, this kills the cross-interval secant problem inside the same component automatically. You no longer need separate secant-surface arguments there. If two subintervals lie on the same strictly convex graph branch, one line still meets the whole branch in at most two points total, so it cannot be 2-secant to both subintervals.

So the next global theorem should be:
The cubic-generic region contains an open dense union of connected components on which (x(h),y(h)) is a strictly convex graph.
The cubic-generic region contains an open dense union of connected components on which (x(h),y(h)) is a strictly convex graph.вҖӢ

Once that is proved, the support of any bad core on the generic region is forced to distribute across different components, with at most two points per component.

That changes the endgame a lot. The remaining obstruction is no longer вҖңpairs from two intervals of the same branch.вҖқ It is only:

    one pair per convex component at most,

    plus perhaps isolated points in other components,

    plus the discrete exceptional set where M=0M=0 or K=0K=0.

So the next exact attack is to understand the global arrangement of these convex components.

There are two natural possibilities.

If the generic region is connected and M,KM,K do not change sign often, then there may be only one dominant convex component. In that case, you are almost done: a bad core could have at most two generic support points total, contradicting the earlier two-pair rigidity except on a discrete exceptional set.

If instead the generic region breaks into many convex components, then the remaining endgame becomes a combinatorial signed relation among points chosen from different convex branches. That is still much smaller than the original problem.

So the next concrete thing to prove is this:

    M(h)вүЎМё0M(h)оҖ вүЎ0,

    K(h)вүЎМё0K(h)оҖ вүЎ0,

    their zero sets are discrete,

    hence the generic region decomposes into convex-graph components.

After that, the real global question is whether more than one such component can contribute to a minimal bad core.

That is where I would push next: not more local coefficient algebra, but the topology and sign pattern of the functions
M(h)=cv5вҖІвҲ’cвҖІv5,K(h)=xвҖІyвҖІвҖІвҲ’yвҖІxвҖІвҖІ.
M(h)=cv5вҖІвҖӢвҲ’cвҖІv5вҖӢ,K(h)=xвҖІyвҖІвҖІвҲ’yвҖІxвҖІвҖІ.

If both are вҖңmostly one sign,вҖқ the endgame may simplify dramatically.

    MвүЎМё0MоҖ вүЎ0,

    cc has fixed nonzero sign,

This is the tangent slope of the graph.
