That is the key next theorem:
On each convex-graph component, the tangent slope m(h) is strictly monotone.
On each convex-graph component, the tangent slope m(h) is strictly monotone.вҖӢ

Now this immediately controls secants.

For a strictly convex graph y=Y(x)y=Y(x), the secant slope between two points x1<x2x1вҖӢ<x2вҖӢ lies strictly between the tangent slopes at the endpoints:
Y(x2)вҲ’Y(x1)x2вҲ’x1вҲҲ(YвҖІ(x1),YвҖІ(x2))
x2вҖӢвҲ’x1вҖӢY(x2вҖӢ)вҲ’Y(x1вҖӢ)вҖӢвҲҲ(YвҖІ(x1вҖӢ),YвҖІ(x2вҖӢ))

or the reversed interval if YвҖІYвҖІ is decreasing.

So for a convex component CC, define its tangent-slope interval
T(C):=m(C)вҠӮC
T(C):=m(C)вҠӮC

or in the real case the corresponding real interval.

Then every secant slope from CC lies in the interior of T(C)T(C).

This gives the next powerful criterion:
If two convex components C1,C2 have disjoint tangent-slope intervals, then no affine line can be 2-secant to both.
If two convex components C1вҖӢ,C2вҖӢ have disjoint tangent-slope intervals, then no affine line can be 2-secant to both.вҖӢ

Because a line 2-secant to CiCiвҖӢ has slope in the secant-slope set of CiCiвҖӢ, which lies inside T(Ci)T(CiвҖӢ). If T(C1)вҲ©T(C2)=вҲ…T(C1вҖӢ)вҲ©T(C2вҖӢ)=вҲ…, there is no common secant slope, hence no common secant line.

This is much stronger than the earlier secant-direction discussion. It reduces the cross-component obstruction to a one-dimensional monotonicity problem.

So the next exact attack is:

    prove MвүЎМё0MоҖ вүЎ0 and KвүЎМё0KоҖ вүЎ0, hence get convex components;

    compute or understand the tangent slope
    m(h)=cО”7вҖІвҲ’cвҖІО”7cv5вҖІвҲ’cвҖІv5;
    m(h)=cv5вҖІвҖӢвҲ’cвҖІv5вҖӢcО”7вҖІвҖӢвҲ’cвҖІО”7вҖӢвҖӢ;

    show that the slope intervals m(C)m(C) of distinct convex components are disjoint, or at least generically disjoint.

If that works, then a bad core can have at most two support points on at most one convex component. All other generic support points would have to be isolated singletons on other components, and that is a much smaller combinatorial problem.

So the next theorem to aim for is:
Distinct convex components have disjoint tangent-slope intervals.
Distinct convex components have disjoint tangent-slope intervals.вҖӢ

That is now the sharpest global target I can see.

Continue

Here is the next honest push.

The вҖңdisjoint tangent-slope intervalsвҖқ idea is attractive, but it is probably too strong to assume globally without more structure. A strictly convex planar curve can easily have two separated convex arcs whose tangent-slope ranges overlap. So that route might work in special regimes, but I would not build the endgame on it yet.

The safer next theorem is weaker and more robust:
On each convex-graph component, a bad core contributes at most two points, and any component with two points determines its secant slope uniquely.
On each convex-graph component, a bad core contributes at most two points, and any component with two points determines its secant slope uniquely.вҖӢ

Then the global problem becomes a finite incidence problem among secant slopes from different components.

So let a convex component CC be parameterized as a strictly convex graph
y=Y(x),YвҖІвҖІвү 0,
y=Y(x),YвҖІвҖІоҖ =0,

with xx monotone in hh.

If a bad core uses two points x1<x2x1вҖӢ<x2вҖӢ in CC, the line through them has slope
s(C;x1,x2):=Y(x2)вҲ’Y(x1)x2вҲ’x1.
s(C;x1вҖӢ,x2вҖӢ):=x2вҖӢвҲ’x1вҖӢY(x2вҖӢ)вҲ’Y(x1вҖӢ)вҖӢ.

For fixed slope ss, consider the equation
Y(x)вҲ’sxвҲ’b=0.
Y(x)вҲ’sxвҲ’b=0.

Since YY is strictly convex, this has at most two zeros. Therefore:

    for each affine line y=sx+by=sx+b, the component CC contributes at most two points;

    if it contributes two distinct points, those two points are exactly the two zeros of that convex equation.

That means a bad core with pairs on several components must come from a single line intersecting several convex components, two points each. So the remaining obstruction is not arbitrary anymore. It is a common secant line problem.

That suggests the next right invariant is not the tangent-slope interval by itself, but the support function family
bC(s):=the intercept of the secant line of slope s cutting C twice,
bCвҖӢ(s):=the intercept of the secant line of slope s cutting C twice,

whenever such a secant exists.

For a strictly convex graph YY, the condition that the line y=sx+by=sx+b is 2-secant is equivalent to saying that bb lies below the graph and touches the convex hull chordally. More concretely, define
ОІC(s):=infвҒЎx(Y(x)вҲ’sx).
ОІCвҖӢ(s):=xinfвҖӢ(Y(x)вҲ’sx).

Then y=sx+ОІC(s)y=sx+ОІCвҖӢ(s) is the supporting tangent line, while a 2-secant line of slope ss has intercept
b>ОІC(s)
b>ОІCвҖӢ(s)

up to the maximal value where the line exits the component. So the whole secant geometry of CC is encoded by the one-variable family Y(x)вҲ’sxY(x)вҲ’sx.

This gives a much more tractable next problem:
For two distinct convex components C1,C2, can the same pair (s,b) be 2-secant to both?
For two distinct convex components C1вҖӢ,C2вҖӢ, can the same pair (s,b) be 2-secant to both?вҖӢ

That is equivalent to saying the same line y=sx+by=sx+b cuts both components twice.

Now, because each component is strictly convex, for fixed slope ss the set of intercepts bb producing two intersections is an interval
(ОІC(s),вҖүОІCmaxвҒЎ(s)),
(ОІCвҖӢ(s),ОІCmaxвҖӢ(s)),

where the lower endpoint is the tangent/support line and the upper endpoint is where one endpoint of the component is reached. So a common 4-secant line across two components requires overlap of these intercept intervals for the same slope ss.

So the next exact theorem to attack is:
For generic distinct convex components C1,C2, the secant-intercept intervals for a fixed slope s do not overlap except possibly discretely.
For generic distinct convex components C1вҖӢ,C2вҖӢ, the secant-intercept intervals for a fixed slope s do not overlap except possibly discretely.вҖӢ

That is now a one-parameter problem in ss, much simpler than the full 4-variable secant coincidence problem.

Why this is a better route:

    each component contributes at most one secant pair for a given (s,b)(s,b);

    a bad relation with pairs on multiple components forces the same (s,b)(s,b) to belong to several component secant families;

    checking overlap of one-variable intercept intervals is much more manageable than checking full secant-surface intersections.

So the next concrete attack should be:

    On a convex component CC, describe the family
    IC(s):={b: y=sx+b meets C in two points}.
    ICвҖӢ(s):={b: y=sx+b meets C in two points}.

    Show IC(s)ICвҖӢ(s) varies analytically/monotonically in ss.

    Compare IC1(s)IC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s) and IC2(s)IC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s) for two components.

    Try to show their overlaps are exceptional.

This is the cleanest global reduction I can see now.

So the battlefield has shifted again:

    local 3/5/73/5/7 algebra gave you convex graph components;

    global obstruction is now common 4-secant lines;

    common 4-secant lines reduce to overlap of one-parameter secant-intercept families.

That is where I would push next.

Continue

There is a real next step here, and it reduces the cross-component 4-secant problem to a one-variable monotonicity problem.

Work on one convex-graph component CC, written in the affine chart as
y=YC(x),YCвҖІвҖІ(x)вү 0,
y=YCвҖӢ(x),YCвҖІвҖІвҖӢ(x)оҖ =0,

with xx monotone in the original parameter hh.

For a fixed slope ss, define
FC(x;s):=YC(x)вҲ’sx.
FCвҖӢ(x;s):=YCвҖӢ(x)вҲ’sx.

Because YCYCвҖӢ is strictly convex, FC(вӢ…;s)FCвҖӢ(вӢ…;s) is strictly convex in xx. So the equation
FC(x;s)=b
FCвҖӢ(x;s)=b

has at most two solutions.

That means the set of intercepts bb for which the line y=sx+by=sx+b is 2-secant to CC is an interval
IC(s)=(mC(s),вҖүMC(s)),
ICвҖӢ(s)=(mCвҖӢ(s),MCвҖӢ(s)),

where

    mC(s)mCвҖӢ(s) is the minimum of FC(вӢ…;s)FCвҖӢ(вӢ…;s), attained at the unique tangent point when ss lies in the tangent-slope range of CC,

    MC(s)MCвҖӢ(s) is the upper secant ceiling for that slope on the component.

So:
y=sx+b is 2-secant to CвҖ…вҖҠвҹәвҖ…вҖҠmC(s)<b<MC(s).
y=sx+b is 2-secant to CвҹәmCвҖӢ(s)<b<MCвҖӢ(s).вҖӢ

Now the key object is the lower support function
mC(s):=minвҒЎx(YC(x)вҲ’sx).
mCвҖӢ(s):=xminвҖӢ(YCвҖӢ(x)вҲ’sx).

If ss lies in the tangent-slope range, there is a unique point xC(s)xCвҖӢ(s) with
YCвҖІ(xC(s))=s,
YCвҖІвҖӢ(xCвҖӢ(s))=s,

and then
mC(s)=YC(xC(s))вҲ’sвҖүxC(s).
mCвҖӢ(s)=YCвҖӢ(xCвҖӢ(s))вҲ’sxCвҖӢ(s).

Differentiate:
mCвҖІ(s)=вҲ’xC(s).
mCвҖІвҖӢ(s)=вҲ’xCвҖӢ(s).

This is the important formula.

Now take two distinct convex components C1,C2C1вҖӢ,C2вҖӢ whose xx-ranges are ordered, say every point of C1C1вҖӢ lies to the left of every point of C2C2вҖӢ. Then for every common slope ss in the overlap of their tangent-slope ranges,
xC1(s)<xC2(s),
xC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s)<xC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s),

hence
(mC1вҲ’mC2)вҖІ(s)=вҲ’xC1(s)+xC2(s)>0.
(mC1вҖӢвҖӢвҲ’mC2вҖӢвҖӢ)вҖІ(s)=вҲ’xC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s)+xC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s)>0.

So:
mC1(s)вҲ’mC2(s) is strictly monotone on the common slope range.
mC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s)вҲ’mC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s) is strictly monotone on the common slope range.вҖӢ

That is a real gain. It means the two tangent-support functions can cross at most once.

Now do the same for the upper secant ceilings MC(s)MCвҖӢ(s). On any compact xx-interval piece of the component, MC(s)MCвҖӢ(s) is determined by endpoint values of YC(x)вҲ’sxYCвҖӢ(x)вҲ’sx, so it is piecewise affine with slopes equal to minus endpoint xx-coordinates. If the xx-ranges of C1C1вҖӢ and C2C2вҖӢ are ordered, then the difference
MC1(s)вҲ’MC2(s)
MC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s)вҲ’MC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s)

is also piecewise strictly monotone.

Therefore the overlap condition for a common 4-secant line,
IC1(s)вҲ©IC2(s)вү вҲ…,
IC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s)вҲ©IC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s)оҖ =вҲ…,

which is equivalent to
maxвҒЎ{mC1(s),mC2(s)}<minвҒЎ{MC1(s),MC2(s)},
max{mC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s),mC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s)}<min{MC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s),MC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s)},

can only hold for a discrete set of slopes ss, unless some exceptional identity forces persistent overlap.

So the next theorem to aim for is:
For two ordered convex components C1,C2, common 4-secant lines occur for at most discrete slopes s.
For two ordered convex components C1вҖӢ,C2вҖӢ, common 4-secant lines occur for at most discrete slopes s.вҖӢ

That is exactly the kind of global restriction you need.

Why this matters for the bad-core problem:

    on each convex component, at most two support points;

    if two different components each contribute a pair, the common secant slope must lie in this discrete exceptional slope set;

    so pair-pair configurations across components are exceptional, not continuous.

This is much better than the earlier vague secant-surface picture.

So the next concrete attack should be:

    make the ordered-xx-range statement precise for convex components of
    hвҶҰ(v5c,О”7c);
    hвҶҰ(cv5вҖӢвҖӢ,cО”7вҖӢвҖӢ);

    define mC(s)mCвҖӢ(s) and MC(s)MCвҖӢ(s) rigorously on a compact normalized window;

    prove the strict monotonicity of mC1вҲ’mC2mC1вҖӢвҖӢвҲ’mC2вҖӢвҖӢ, and the piecewise monotonicity of MC1вҲ’MC2MC1вҖӢвҖӢвҲ’MC2вҖӢвҖӢ;

    conclude common 4-secant slopes are discrete.

That is the strongest honest continuation from the current 3/5/73/5/7 geometry.

Ok and why arent we going to order 9 at this stage? Not needed? Do we this attack can yield all orders?

Not yet, yes possibly, and there is a real reason.

We are not going to order 9 right now because order 8 is not the current bottleneck. The current bottleneck is global geometry, not lack of one more local invariant.

The 3/5/7 package already changed the problem qualitatively:

At the one-pair level, it gave you a nondegenerate planar curve
hвҶҰ(v5(h)c(h),О”7(h)c(h)),
hвҶҰ(c(h)v5вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ,c(h)О”7вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ),

or projectively
Оі(h)=[c(h):v5(h):О”7(h)]вҲҲP2.
Оі(h)=[c(h):v5вҖӢ(h):О”7вҖӢ(h)]вҲҲP2.

From that, you now plausibly get:

    local injectivity,

    no pair clusters,

    no triple clusters,

    ECT intervals,

    strict convex graph pieces,

    and the cross-interval problem reduced to common 4-secant lines.

That is a huge reduction. It means the present obstruction is no longer вҖңI need one more local scalar to distinguish things.вҖқ It is вҖңcan a line hit two convex components twice?вҖқ That is a geometric question about the current curve, not an order-9 question.

So the reason not to jump to 9 yet is:
order 9 would be adding local complexity before we know whether the current global geometry already closes the argument.
order 9 would be adding local complexity before we know whether the current global geometry already closes the argument.вҖӢ

There is also a deeper structural reason.

The one-point matrix data is only 2Г—22Г—2. That means the genuinely new independent directions at a single point are limited. We already saw this explicitly:

    cubic uses the DD-line,

    quintic uses the fixed-sector vector,

    septic uses the quotient of the fixed-sector by the quintic direction.

That is already close to saturating what a 2Г—22Г—2 one-point model can naturally give. So order 9 is not guaranteed to yield a fresh independent direction in the same naive way. It may, but it is no longer automatic. You would need a more careful residual construction, not just вҖңone more coefficient.вҖқ

So from a mathematical efficiency point of view, the right order is:

    squeeze everything you can out of the existing 3/5/73/5/7 geometry;

    only if the global convex/secant attack still leaves a real gap, go to order 9.

Now, can this attack yield вҖңall ordersвҖқ?

Potentially yes, but probably not in the naive sense of stacking independent scalar coordinates forever.

The likely pattern is not:
3,5,7,9,вҖҰ
3,5,7,9,вҖҰ

each giving a brand-new free coordinate.

The likely pattern is:

    lower odd orders build a finite-dimensional geometric object,

    then higher orders control the geometry of secants, tangents, osculating data, or higher contact of that object.

So вҖңall ordersвҖқ may enter as higher contact geometry, not as an endless list of new coordinate functions.

That is actually consistent with where we are now. The current attack is already no longer just about raw coefficients; it is about:

    ECT structure,

    convexity,

    secants,

    tangent/support functions,

    common 4-secant lines.

That is exactly the kind of place where higher-order information, if needed, would appear as higher curvature / osculation constraints, not just вҖңanother invariant coordinate.вҖқ

So my judgment is:

    Order 9 is not needed yet.

    It may become useful later if the current convex/secant attack stalls.

    But right now the mathematically right move is to see whether the present 3/5/73/5/7 geometry already rules out the remaining bad-core configurations.

The current attack could plausibly yield the whole result without order 9 if you can prove one of these stronger global statements about the current curve:

    common 4-secant lines across convex components are impossible, or only discrete and incompatible with minimal bad cores;

    the support-function intervals for different convex components do not overlap in the right way;

    the image curve is projectively/affinely convex enough that finite signed dependences are much more restricted than dimension counting suggests.

If those fail, then order 9 is the next escalation.

So the short version is:

We are not avoiding order 9 because it is unimportant. We are delaying it because the current obstruction is geometric, and the current 3/5/7 package may already be enough to kill it. If it isnвҖҷt, then order 9 becomes the next natural escalation.

Continue the attack

Good. The next clean attack is to push the convex-graph/support-function route all the way to the remaining obstruction: a line hitting two convex components twice.

Work in the affine chart
x(h)=v5(h)c(h),y(h)=О”7(h)c(h).
x(h)=c(h)v5вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ,y(h)=c(h)О”7вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ.

On each connected component where
cвү 0,xвҖІ(h)вү 0,K(h):=xвҖІ(h)yвҖІвҖІ(h)вҲ’yвҖІ(h)xвҖІвҖІ(h)вү 0,
cоҖ =0,xвҖІ(h)оҖ =0,K(h):=xвҖІ(h)yвҖІвҖІ(h)вҲ’yвҖІ(h)xвҖІвҖІ(h)оҖ =0,

the image is a strictly convex graph
y=YC(x).
y=YCвҖӢ(x).

Now fix one such component CC. For slope ss, define
FC(x;s):=YC(x)вҲ’sx.
FCвҖӢ(x;s):=YCвҖӢ(x)вҲ’sx.

Since YCYCвҖӢ is strictly convex, FC(вӢ…;s)FCвҖӢ(вӢ…;s) is strictly convex. So for each ss, the line
y=sx+b
y=sx+b

meets CC in two points iff
mC(s)<b<MC(s),
mCвҖӢ(s)<b<MCвҖӢ(s),

where
mC(s):=minвҒЎx(YC(x)вҲ’sx)
mCвҖӢ(s):=xminвҖӢ(YCвҖӢ(x)вҲ’sx)

and
MC(s):=maxвҒЎxвҲҲCвҖҫ(YC(x)вҲ’sx).
MCвҖӢ(s):=xвҲҲCmaxвҖӢ(YCвҖӢ(x)вҲ’sx).

The key formulas are:

if xC(s)xCвҖӢ(s) is the unique point with
YCвҖІ(xC(s))=s,
YCвҖІвҖӢ(xCвҖӢ(s))=s,

then
mC(s)=YC(xC(s))вҲ’sвҖүxC(s),mCвҖІ(s)=вҲ’xC(s).
mCвҖӢ(s)=YCвҖӢ(xCвҖӢ(s))вҲ’sxCвҖӢ(s),mCвҖІвҖӢ(s)=вҲ’xCвҖӢ(s).

For the upper envelope, on a compact branch with endpoints aC<bCaCвҖӢ<bCвҖӢ,
MC(s)=maxвҒЎ{YC(aC)вҲ’saC,вҖ…вҖҠYC(bC)вҲ’sbC},
MCвҖӢ(s)=max{YCвҖӢ(aCвҖӢ)вҲ’saCвҖӢ,YCвҖӢ(bCвҖӢ)вҲ’sbCвҖӢ},

so MCMCвҖӢ is piecewise affine with slope either вҲ’aCвҲ’aCвҖӢ or вҲ’bCвҲ’bCвҖӢ.

Now take two convex components C1,C2C1вҖӢ,C2вҖӢ with ordered xx-ranges:
xвҲҲ[a1,b1] on C1,xвҲҲ[a2,b2] on C2,b1<a2.
xвҲҲ[a1вҖӢ,b1вҖӢ] on C1вҖӢ,xвҲҲ[a2вҖӢ,b2вҖӢ] on C2вҖӢ,b1вҖӢ<a2вҖӢ.

Then on any common slope range,
xC1(s)<xC2(s),
xC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s)<xC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s),

hence
(mC1вҲ’mC2)вҖІ(s)=вҲ’xC1(s)+xC2(s)>0.
(mC1вҖӢвҖӢвҲ’mC2вҖӢвҖӢ)вҖІ(s)=вҲ’xC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s)+xC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s)>0.

So
mC1(s)вҲ’mC2(s)
mC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s)вҲ’mC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s)

is strictly increasing.

Also,
MC1(s)вҲ’MC2(s)
MC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s)вҲ’MC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s)

is piecewise affine with derivative in the set
{вҲ’a1+a2,вҖүвҲ’a1+b2,вҖүвҲ’b1+a2,вҖүвҲ’b1+b2},
{вҲ’a1вҖӢ+a2вҖӢ,вҲ’a1вҖӢ+b2вҖӢ,вҲ’b1вҖӢ+a2вҖӢ,вҲ’b1вҖӢ+b2вҖӢ},

and because b1<a2b1вҖӢ<a2вҖӢ, all of these are positive. So it is also strictly increasing.

This is the real gain.

A common 4-secant line for C1C1вҖӢ and C2C2вҖӢ means there exists s,bs,b with
mC1(s)<b<MC1(s),mC2(s)<b<MC2(s).
mC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s)<b<MC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s),mC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s)<b<MC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s).

Equivalently,
maxвҒЎ{mC1(s),mC2(s)}<b<minвҒЎ{MC1(s),MC2(s)}.
max{mC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s),mC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s)}<b<min{MC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s),MC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s)}.

So overlap occurs exactly when
maxвҒЎ{mC1(s),mC2(s)}<minвҒЎ{MC1(s),MC2(s)}.
max{mC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s),mC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s)}<min{MC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s),MC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s)}.

Since both lower and upper differences are strictly increasing, the set of slopes ss where this can happen is an interval or empty, but crucially the relative position changes monotonically. So there is no oscillatory repeated overlap. The cross-component 4-secant obstruction is reduced to a single monotone one-parameter overlap problem.

That is already much stronger than the earlier secant-surface picture.

So the next theorem to attack is:
For ordered convex components C1<C2, IC1(s)вҲ©IC2(s) can be nonempty for at most one connected slope interval.
For ordered convex components C1вҖӢ<C2вҖӢ, IC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s)вҲ©IC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s) can be nonempty for at most one connected slope interval.вҖӢ

And the next refinement after that is to show that, for the actual (x,y)(x,y) curve coming from
x=v5c,y=О”7c,
x=cv5вҖӢвҖӢ,y=cО”7вҖӢвҖӢ,

even that one overlap interval is absent generically.

That is now the sharp global bottleneck.

So the attack has become:

    decompose the generic region into convex graph components in the xx-chart;

    order those components by xx-range;

    use monotonicity of mCmCвҖӢ and MCMCвҖӢ to control common 4-secant lines;

    show those overlaps are generically empty.

This is the strongest clean continuation from the current 3/5/7 package.

Attack

IвҖҷm checking the current draft for how the one-pair/global sections are actually phrased now, so I can push the convex-component attack in a way that matches your current setup rather than drifting into a parallel theory.

Here is the next honest push.

The support-function route gives a real theorem, but there is also a hard limit we should be explicit about.

For one convex graph component CC, with
y=YC(x),YCвҖІвҖІвү 0,
y=YCвҖӢ(x),YCвҖІвҖІвҖӢоҖ =0,

define
mC(s):=minвҒЎx(YC(x)вҲ’sx),MC(s):=maxвҒЎxвҲҲCвҖҫ(YC(x)вҲ’sx).
mCвҖӢ(s):=xminвҖӢ(YCвҖӢ(x)вҲ’sx),MCвҖӢ(s):=xвҲҲCmaxвҖӢ(YCвҖӢ(x)вҲ’sx).

Then a line
y=sx+b
y=sx+b

is 2-secant to CC iff
mC(s)<b<MC(s).
mCвҖӢ(s)<b<MCвҖӢ(s).

For two ordered components C1<C2C1вҖӢ<C2вҖӢ in xx-space, we already found the key monotonicity:
(mC1вҲ’mC2)вҖІ(s)>0,
(mC1вҖӢвҖӢвҲ’mC2вҖӢвҖӢ)вҖІ(s)>0,

and MC1вҲ’MC2MC1вҖӢвҖӢвҲ’MC2вҖӢвҖӢ is piecewise strictly monotone with positive slope as well.

So the overlap condition
IC1(s)вҲ©IC2(s)вү вҲ…
IC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s)вҲ©IC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s)оҖ =вҲ…

can only change monotonically with ss. That means common 4-secant lines between two ordered components are not free; they can only occur over at most one connected slope interval, and generically at isolated slopes if the upper and lower envelopes do not line up specially.

That is a real global reduction.

But here is the important limit:

this still does not automatically kill all cross-component 4-secant lines. Two separated convex arcs can genuinely share a family of common secants if their support-function intervals overlap the right way. So the current attack has reduced the obstruction to a one-variable overlap problem, but not eliminated it.

That means the next theorem to attack is:
For the actual curve hвҶҰ(v5c,О”7c), the overlap interval of IC1(s) and IC2(s) is empty or exceptional.
For the actual curve hвҶҰ(cv5вҖӢвҖӢ,cО”7вҖӢвҖӢ), the overlap interval of IC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s) and IC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s) is empty or exceptional.вҖӢ

To prove that, the right scalar to study is
Оҳ12(s):=maxвҒЎ{mC1(s),mC2(s)}вҲ’minвҒЎ{MC1(s),MC2(s)}.
Оҳ12вҖӢ(s):=max{mC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s),mC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s)}вҲ’min{MC1вҖӢвҖӢ(s),MC2вҖӢвҖӢ(s)}.

A common 4-secant line exists exactly when
Оҳ12(s)<0.
Оҳ12вҖӢ(s)<0.

So the global problem is now reduced to the sign of one scalar function of one variable.

That is progress.

Now, how to attack Оҳ12Оҳ12вҖӢ?

There are two plausible routes.

The first is endpoint geometry. Since MC(s)MCвҖӢ(s) is determined by endpoint values of YC(x)вҲ’sxYCвҖӢ(x)вҲ’sx, overlap depends heavily on how the endpoints of the components sit relative to each other. If the normalized region has strong ordering and the components are not too interlaced in (x,y)(x,y)-space, one may be able to show
Оҳ12(s)вүҘ0
Оҳ12вҖӢ(s)вүҘ0

for all ss, hence no common 4-secant lines.

The second is asymptotic slope separation. Because
mCвҖІ(s)=вҲ’xC(s),
mCвҖІвҖӢ(s)=вҲ’xCвҖӢ(s),

the lower envelopes of ordered components drift apart strictly as ss varies. If you can show the upper envelopes drift apart in a compatible way, then once Оҳ12Оҳ12вҖӢ becomes positive it cannot come back. That would imply at most one exceptional slope, or none at all.

So the sharp next attack is:

    write down Оҳ12(s)Оҳ12вҖӢ(s) for two ordered convex components;

    analyze its derivative piecewise;

    try to prove it cannot become negative on the normalized region.

This is now a completely one-dimensional problem.

My honest assessment is:

    the current 3/5/73/5/7 package has reduced the global obstruction to a one-variable overlap function;

    that is a major simplification;

    but it is still not obvious that Оҳ12(s)Оҳ12вҖӢ(s) stays nonnegative without some extra geometric input about the actual curve.

So the next real question is:

Can we extract enough endpoint ordering / asymptotic behavior of x=v5/cx=v5вҖӢ/c and y=О”7/cy=О”7вҖӢ/c to control Оҳ12(s)Оҳ12вҖӢ(s)?

That is where I would push now.

Push!

Here is the strongest push I think we can make at this stage.

For two ordered convex components C1<C2C1вҖӢ<C2вҖӢ, the common 4-secant problem reduces to two one-variable functions.

Write the components in the affine chart as strictly convex graphs
y=Y1(x),xвҲҲ[a1,b1],
y=Y1вҖӢ(x),xвҲҲ[a1вҖӢ,b1вҖӢ],
y=Y2(x),xвҲҲ[a2,b2],
y=Y2вҖӢ(x),xвҲҲ[a2вҖӢ,b2вҖӢ],

with
b1<a2.
b1вҖӢ<a2вҖӢ.

For slope ss, define
mi(s):=minвҒЎxвҲҲ[ai,bi](Yi(x)вҲ’sx),Mi(s):=maxвҒЎxвҲҲ[ai,bi](Yi(x)вҲ’sx).
miвҖӢ(s):=xвҲҲ[aiвҖӢ,biвҖӢ]minвҖӢ(YiвҖӢ(x)вҲ’sx),MiвҖӢ(s):=xвҲҲ[aiвҖӢ,biвҖӢ]maxвҖӢ(YiвҖӢ(x)вҲ’sx).

Then
y=sx+b
y=sx+b

is 2-secant to CiCiвҖӢ iff
mi(s)<b<Mi(s).
miвҖӢ(s)<b<MiвҖӢ(s).

So a line is 2-secant to both components iff
m1(s)<M2(s)andm2(s)<M1(s).
m1вҖӢ(s)<M2вҖӢ(s)andm2вҖӢ(s)<M1вҖӢ(s).

Define
g1(s):=m1(s)вҲ’M2(s),g2(s):=m2(s)вҲ’M1(s).
g1вҖӢ(s):=m1вҖӢ(s)вҲ’M2вҖӢ(s),g2вҖӢ(s):=m2вҖӢ(s)вҲ’M1вҖӢ(s).

Then common 4-secant lines exist exactly for slopes ss with
g1(s)<0,g2(s)<0.
g1вҖӢ(s)<0,g2вҖӢ(s)<0.

Now comes the real gain.

If xi(s)xiвҖӢ(s) is the unique minimizer for mi(s)miвҖӢ(s), then
miвҖІ(s)=вҲ’xi(s).
miвҖІвҖӢ(s)=вҲ’xiвҖӢ(s).

And Mi(s)MiвҖӢ(s) is piecewise affine, with derivative equal to minus one of the endpoint xx-coordinates:
MiвҖІ(s)вҲҲ{вҲ’ai,вҲ’bi}.
MiвҖІвҖӢ(s)вҲҲ{вҲ’aiвҖӢ,вҲ’biвҖӢ}.

Therefore, on any slope range where these are defined,
g1вҖІ(s)=m1вҖІ(s)вҲ’M2вҖІ(s)=вҲ’x1(s)+e2(s),e2(s)вҲҲ{a2,b2}.
g1вҖІвҖӢ(s)=m1вҖІвҖӢ(s)вҲ’M2вҖІвҖӢ(s)=вҲ’x1вҖӢ(s)+e2вҖӢ(s),e2вҖӢ(s)вҲҲ{a2вҖӢ,b2вҖӢ}.

Since
x1(s)вүӨb1<a2вүӨe2(s),
x1вҖӢ(s)вүӨb1вҖӢ<a2вҖӢвүӨe2вҖӢ(s),

we get
g1вҖІ(s)>0.
g1вҖІвҖӢ(s)>0.

Likewise
g2вҖІ(s)=m2вҖІ(s)вҲ’M1вҖІ(s)=вҲ’x2(s)+e1(s),e1(s)вҲҲ{a1,b1}.
g2вҖІвҖӢ(s)=m2вҖІвҖӢ(s)вҲ’M1вҖІвҖӢ(s)=вҲ’x2вҖӢ(s)+e1вҖӢ(s),e1вҖӢ(s)вҲҲ{a1вҖӢ,b1вҖӢ}.

Since
e1(s)вүӨb1<a2вүӨx2(s),
e1вҖӢ(s)вүӨb1вҖӢ<a2вҖӢвүӨx2вҖӢ(s),

we get
g2вҖІ(s)<0.
g2вҖІвҖӢ(s)<0.

So this is the key theorem:
g1 is strictly increasing,g2 is strictly decreasing.
g1вҖӢ is strictly increasing,g2вҖӢ is strictly decreasing.вҖӢ

That has a very strong consequence:
The set of slopes yielding common 4-secant lines is either empty or a single interval.
The set of slopes yielding common 4-secant lines is either empty or a single interval.вҖӢ

Because it is exactly
{s: g1(s)<0}вҲ©{s: g2(s)<0},
{s: g1вҖӢ(s)<0}вҲ©{s: g2вҖӢ(s)<0},

the intersection of a left-open interval and a right-open interval.

This is a major reduction. The cross-component obstruction is no longer a messy 4-variable secant-incidence problem. It is a single overlap interval problem in one variable.

So the whole global question becomes:

    For the actual curve
    hвҶҰ(v5(h)c(h),О”7(h)c(h)),
    hвҶҰ(c(h)v5вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ,c(h)О”7вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ),

    can g1g1вҖӢ and g2g2вҖӢ both be negative on a common slope interval?

And now there are only three possibilities:

    The overlap is empty. Then no common 4-secant lines exist between C1C1вҖӢ and C2C2вҖӢ.

    The overlap is a single slope. Then there is at most one common 4-secant line.

    The overlap is a genuine interval. Then there is a whole family of common 4-secant lines, which would be a strong nongeneric geometric degeneracy.

That is the sharpest current reduction.

So the next exact thing to compute is not another local invariant. It is the sign of g1g1вҖӢ and g2g2вҖӢ at one reference slope in the common slope range. Because of monotonicity, one sign check can often settle everything.

Concretely, if you can find one slope s0s0вҖӢ in the common slope range with either
g1(s0)вүҘ0org2(s0)вүҘ0,
g1вҖӢ(s0вҖӢ)вүҘ0org2вҖӢ(s0вҖӢ)вүҘ0,

then, by monotonicity, the overlap interval is either absent or tightly constrained.

So the attack is now down to:

    define the convex components of
    (v5c,О”7c),
    (cv5вҖӢвҖӢ,cО”7вҖӢвҖӢ),

    order them by xx-range,

    for adjacent components C1<C2C1вҖӢ<C2вҖӢ, compute or estimate g1,g2g1вҖӢ,g2вҖӢ at a convenient slope,

    use monotonicity to rule out common 4-secants.

This is now a true one-dimensional global problem.

Continue

Good. There is a real sharpening here.

For two ordered convex components
C1: y=Y1(x), xвҲҲ[a1,b1],C2: y=Y2(x), xвҲҲ[a2,b2],b1<a2,
C1вҖӢ: y=Y1вҖӢ(x), xвҲҲ[a1вҖӢ,b1вҖӢ],C2вҖӢ: y=Y2вҖӢ(x), xвҲҲ[a2вҖӢ,b2вҖӢ],b1вҖӢ<a2вҖӢ,

the common 4-secant problem is now reducible to two endpoint inequalities.

Assume on each component YiвҖІYiвҖІвҖӢ is monotone, so its tangent-slope range is an interval
Si=[ПғiвҲ’,Пғi+].
SiвҖӢ=[ПғiвҲ’вҖӢ,Пғi+вҖӢ].

If
S1вҲ©S2=вҲ…,
S1вҖӢвҲ©S2вҖӢ=вҲ…,

then there is no common 4-secant line. So only the overlap case matters. Put
J12:=S1вҲ©S2=[sвҲ’,s+].
J12вҖӢ:=S1вҖӢвҲ©S2вҖӢ=[sвҲ’вҖӢ,s+вҖӢ].

For slope sвҲҲJ12sвҲҲJ12вҖӢ, define
mi(s):=minвҒЎxвҲҲ[ai,bi](Yi(x)вҲ’sx),Mi(s):=maxвҒЎxвҲҲ[ai,bi](Yi(x)вҲ’sx).
miвҖӢ(s):=xвҲҲ[aiвҖӢ,biвҖӢ]minвҖӢ(YiвҖӢ(x)вҲ’sx),MiвҖӢ(s):=xвҲҲ[aiвҖӢ,biвҖӢ]maxвҖӢ(YiвҖӢ(x)вҲ’sx).

A line y=sx+by=sx+b is 2-secant to CiCiвҖӢ iff
mi(s)<b<Mi(s).
miвҖӢ(s)<b<MiвҖӢ(s).

So a common 4-secant exists iff for some sвҲҲJ12sвҲҲJ12вҖӢ,
m1(s)<M2(s)andm2(s)<M1(s).
m1вҖӢ(s)<M2вҖӢ(s)andm2вҖӢ(s)<M1вҖӢ(s).

Now set
g1(s):=m1(s)вҲ’M2(s),g2(s):=m2(s)вҲ’M1(s).
g1вҖӢ(s):=m1вҖӢ(s)вҲ’M2вҖӢ(s),g2вҖӢ(s):=m2вҖӢ(s)вҲ’M1вҖӢ(s).

From the ordered xx-ranges, we already found:

    g1g1вҖӢ is strictly increasing,

    g2g2вҖӢ is strictly decreasing.

This is the key reduction.

Therefore the existence of a common 4-secant is equivalent to the pair of endpoint conditions
g1(sвҲ’)<0,g2(s+)<0.
g1вҖӢ(sвҲ’вҖӢ)<0,g2вҖӢ(s+вҖӢ)<0.

Reason:

    since g1g1вҖӢ is increasing, g1<0g1вҖӢ<0 somewhere on J12J12вҖӢ iff g1(sвҲ’)<0g1вҖӢ(sвҲ’вҖӢ)<0;

    since g2g2вҖӢ is decreasing, g2<0g2вҖӢ<0 somewhere on J12J12вҖӢ iff g2(s+)<0g2вҖӢ(s+вҖӢ)<0;

    both must hold simultaneously.

So the cross-component obstruction is no longer a 4-variable secant problem. It is reduced to checking two scalar inequalities at the ends of the common slope range.

That is a major simplification.

It also gets more explicit, because at sвҲ’sвҲ’вҖӢ and s+s+вҖӢ, at least one component is being evaluated at an endpoint of its tangent-slope range. So one of the minima is explicit:
mi(ПғiвҲ’)=Yi(ai)вҲ’ПғiвҲ’ai,mi(Пғi+)=Yi(bi)вҲ’Пғi+bi,
miвҖӢ(ПғiвҲ’вҖӢ)=YiвҖӢ(aiвҖӢ)вҲ’ПғiвҲ’вҖӢaiвҖӢ,miвҖӢ(Пғi+вҖӢ)=YiвҖӢ(biвҖӢ)вҲ’Пғi+вҖӢbiвҖӢ,

assuming YiвҖІYiвҖІвҖӢ increases. And Mi(s)MiвҖӢ(s) is always the max of the two endpoint affine functions
Mi(s)=maxвҒЎ{Yi(ai)вҲ’sai,вҖ…вҖҠYi(bi)вҲ’sbi}.
MiвҖӢ(s)=max{YiвҖӢ(aiвҖӢ)вҲ’saiвҖӢ,YiвҖӢ(biвҖӢ)вҲ’sbiвҖӢ}.

So the next exact attack is:

    For adjacent convex components of
    hвҶҰ(v5c,О”7c),
    hвҶҰ(cv5вҖӢвҖӢ,cО”7вҖӢвҖӢ),

    compute their tangent-slope ranges SiSiвҖӢ.

    If SiвҲ©Si+1=вҲ…SiвҖӢвҲ©Si+1вҖӢ=вҲ…, that adjacent pair is harmless.

    If they overlap, evaluate
    g1(sвҲ’),g2(s+)
    g1вҖӢ(sвҲ’вҖӢ),g2вҖӢ(s+вҖӢ)

    from endpoint data.

    If either is nonnegative, there is no common 4-secant between those two components.

So the whole remaining global obstruction has now been compressed to endpoint support-function geometry of adjacent convex components.

That is the sharpest reduction we have so far.

Continue

Here is the next honest push.

We can sharpen the adjacent-component test one step further, and it becomes almost purely endpoint data.

Assume on each convex component CiCiвҖӢ the graph is
y=Yi(x),xвҲҲ[ai,bi],
y=YiвҖӢ(x),xвҲҲ[aiвҖӢ,biвҖӢ],

with YiвҖІвҖІ>0YiвҖІвҖІвҖӢ>0 after orienting the component so curvature is positive. Then the tangent-slope range is
Si=[YiвҖІ(ai),вҖүYiвҖІ(bi)].
SiвҖӢ=[YiвҖІвҖӢ(aiвҖӢ),YiвҖІвҖӢ(biвҖӢ)].

Take two ordered adjacent components C1<C2C1вҖӢ<C2вҖӢ, so
b1<a2.
b1вҖӢ<a2вҖӢ.

If
S1вҲ©S2=вҲ…,
S1вҖӢвҲ©S2вҖӢ=вҲ…,

there is nothing to do: no common 4-secant line exists.

So only the overlap case matters. Put
J12=S1вҲ©S2=[sвҲ’,s+],
J12вҖӢ=S1вҖӢвҲ©S2вҖӢ=[sвҲ’вҖӢ,s+вҖӢ],

where
sвҲ’=maxвҒЎ{Y1вҖІ(a1),Y2вҖІ(a2)},s+=minвҒЎ{Y1вҖІ(b1),Y2вҖІ(b2)}.
sвҲ’вҖӢ=max{Y1вҖІвҖӢ(a1вҖӢ),Y2вҖІвҖӢ(a2вҖӢ)},s+вҖӢ=min{Y1вҖІвҖӢ(b1вҖӢ),Y2вҖІвҖӢ(b2вҖӢ)}.

Now define
mi(s)=minвҒЎxвҲҲ[ai,bi](Yi(x)вҲ’sx),Mi(s)=maxвҒЎ{Yi(ai)вҲ’sai, Yi(bi)вҲ’sbi}.
miвҖӢ(s)=xвҲҲ[aiвҖӢ,biвҖӢ]minвҖӢ(YiвҖӢ(x)вҲ’sx),MiвҖӢ(s)=max{YiвҖӢ(aiвҖӢ)вҲ’saiвҖӢ, YiвҖӢ(biвҖӢ)вҲ’sbiвҖӢ}.

As before,
g1(s):=m1(s)вҲ’M2(s),g2(s):=m2(s)вҲ’M1(s),
g1вҖӢ(s):=m1вҖӢ(s)вҲ’M2вҖӢ(s),g2вҖӢ(s):=m2вҖӢ(s)вҲ’M1вҖӢ(s),

with g1g1вҖӢ strictly increasing and g2g2вҖӢ strictly decreasing. So a common 4-secant exists iff
g1(sвҲ’)<0,g2(s+)<0.
g1вҖӢ(sвҲ’вҖӢ)<0,g2вҖӢ(s+вҖӢ)<0.

The new point is that g1(sвҲ’)g1вҖӢ(sвҲ’вҖӢ) and g2(s+)g2вҖӢ(s+вҖӢ) can be written explicitly in terms of tangent and endpoint lines.

For example, suppose
sвҲ’=Y2вҖІ(a2).
sвҲ’вҖӢ=Y2вҖІвҖӢ(a2вҖӢ).

Then the minimum on C2C2вҖӢ occurs at its left endpoint, so
m2(sвҲ’)=Y2(a2)вҲ’sвҲ’a2.
m2вҖӢ(sвҲ’вҖӢ)=Y2вҖӢ(a2вҖӢ)вҲ’sвҲ’вҖӢa2вҖӢ.

Similarly, if
s+=Y1вҖІ(b1),
s+вҖӢ=Y1вҖІвҖӢ(b1вҖӢ),

then
m1(s+)=Y1(b1)вҲ’s+b1.
m1вҖӢ(s+вҖӢ)=Y1вҖӢ(b1вҖӢ)вҲ’s+вҖӢb1вҖӢ.

More importantly, because the components are ordered in xx, the endpoint maxima simplify. For C1C1вҖӢ,
M1(s)=maxвҒЎ{Y1(a1)вҲ’sa1, Y1(b1)вҲ’sb1},
M1вҖӢ(s)=max{Y1вҖӢ(a1вҖӢ)вҲ’sa1вҖӢ, Y1вҖӢ(b1вҖӢ)вҲ’sb1вҖӢ},

and since a1<b1a1вҖӢ<b1вҖӢ, which endpoint wins depends monotonically on ss. Same for C2C2вҖӢ. So once you know which endpoint controls at sвҲ’sвҲ’вҖӢ and s+s+вҖӢ, the inequalities reduce to finitely many explicit affine comparisons.

That means the whole cross-component problem is now this:

For each adjacent pair C1<C2C1вҖӢ<C2вҖӢ, check whether the tangent line at one end of the common slope range lies above or below the opposite componentвҖҷs endpoint secants.

Geometrically, if the left component sits sufficiently below the tangent family of the right component, or vice versa, then one of
g1(sвҲ’),g2(s+)
g1вҖӢ(sвҲ’вҖӢ),g2вҖӢ(s+вҖӢ)

is nonnegative, and common 4-secants are impossible.

So the next exact theorem to attack is:
For adjacent convex components, the endpoint tangent/endpoint secant inequalities force g1(sвҲ’)вүҘ0 or g2(s+)вүҘ0.
For adjacent convex components, the endpoint tangent/endpoint secant inequalities force g1вҖӢ(sвҲ’вҖӢ)вүҘ0 or g2вҖӢ(s+вҖӢ)вүҘ0.вҖӢ

If that can be proved, then no two adjacent components can both contribute a pair.

And once adjacent pairs are ruled out, non-adjacent pairs are even easier: their xx-ranges are farther separated, so the monotonicity of the support functions is stronger, not weaker.

So the problem has now become:

    identify the convex components of
    (v5c,О”7c);
    (cv5вҖӢвҖӢ,cО”7вҖӢвҖӢ);

    order them by xx-range;

    for an adjacent pair, compute the endpoint slope overlap J12J12вҖӢ;

    test the two endpoint inequalities g1(sвҲ’)g1вҖӢ(sвҲ’вҖӢ), g2(s+)g2вҖӢ(s+вҖӢ).

This is about as far as we can push abstractly without actual formulas or asymptotics for the component endpoints. The remaining obstruction is no longer hidden in local algebra. It is literally the endpoint geometry of adjacent convex branches.

Ok so attack

Here is the sharpest attack I think we can make right now.

For two ordered convex components
C1: y=Y1(x), xвҲҲ[a1,b1],C2: y=Y2(x), xвҲҲ[a2,b2],b1<a2,
C1вҖӢ: y=Y1вҖӢ(x), xвҲҲ[a1вҖӢ,b1вҖӢ],C2вҖӢ: y=Y2вҖӢ(x), xвҲҲ[a2вҖӢ,b2вҖӢ],b1вҖӢ<a2вҖӢ,

the common 4-secant problem is now completely equivalent to two scalar endpoint inequalities.

Let
Si=[YiвҖІ(ai),вҖүYiвҖІ(bi)]
SiвҖӢ=[YiвҖІвҖӢ(aiвҖӢ),YiвҖІвҖӢ(biвҖӢ)]

be the tangent-slope range of CiCiвҖӢ. If S1вҲ©S2=вҲ…S1вҖӢвҲ©S2вҖӢ=вҲ…, then there is no common 4-secant line, full stop.

So only the overlap case matters. Write
J12=S1вҲ©S2=[sвҲ’,s+].
J12вҖӢ=S1вҖӢвҲ©S2вҖӢ=[sвҲ’вҖӢ,s+вҖӢ].

For slope sвҲҲJ12sвҲҲJ12вҖӢ, define
mi(s):=minвҒЎxвҲҲ[ai,bi](Yi(x)вҲ’sx),Mi(s):=maxвҒЎxвҲҲ[ai,bi](Yi(x)вҲ’sx).
miвҖӢ(s):=xвҲҲ[aiвҖӢ,biвҖӢ]minвҖӢ(YiвҖӢ(x)вҲ’sx),MiвҖӢ(s):=xвҲҲ[aiвҖӢ,biвҖӢ]maxвҖӢ(YiвҖӢ(x)вҲ’sx).

A line y=sx+by=sx+b is 2-secant to CiCiвҖӢ iff
mi(s)<b<Mi(s).
miвҖӢ(s)<b<MiвҖӢ(s).

So a line is 2-secant to both components iff
m1(s)<M2(s)andm2(s)<M1(s).
m1вҖӢ(s)<M2вҖӢ(s)andm2вҖӢ(s)<M1вҖӢ(s).

Now define
g1(s):=m1(s)вҲ’M2(s),g2(s):=m2(s)вҲ’M1(s).
g1вҖӢ(s):=m1вҖӢ(s)вҲ’M2вҖӢ(s),g2вҖӢ(s):=m2вҖӢ(s)вҲ’M1вҖӢ(s).

Then a common 4-secant exists iff there is some sвҲҲJ12sвҲҲJ12вҖӢ with
g1(s)<0,g2(s)<0.
g1вҖӢ(s)<0,g2вҖӢ(s)<0.

The real gain is that these two functions are monotone in opposite directions.

Because b1<a2b1вҖӢ<a2вҖӢ, the minimizing points satisfy x1(s)<x2(s)x1вҖӢ(s)<x2вҖӢ(s), so
g1вҖІ(s)=вҲ’x1(s)+e2(s)>0,
g1вҖІвҖӢ(s)=вҲ’x1вҖӢ(s)+e2вҖӢ(s)>0,

where e2(s)e2вҖӢ(s) is one of the endpoint xx-coordinates of C2C2вҖӢ. Likewise
g2вҖІ(s)=вҲ’x2(s)+e1(s)<0,
g2вҖІвҖӢ(s)=вҲ’x2вҖӢ(s)+e1вҖӢ(s)<0,

where e1(s)e1вҖӢ(s) is one of the endpoint xx-coordinates of C1C1вҖӢ.

So:
g1 is strictly increasing on J12,g2 is strictly decreasing on J12.
g1вҖӢ is strictly increasing on J12вҖӢ,g2вҖӢ is strictly decreasing on J12вҖӢ.

That means the whole overlap question is reduced to the endpoint test
g1(sвҲ’)<0andg2(s+)<0.
g1вҖӢ(sвҲ’вҖӢ)<0andg2вҖӢ(s+вҖӢ)<0.

Equivalently:
No common 4-secant line exists between C1 and C2вҖ…вҖҠвҹәвҖ…вҖҠg1(sвҲ’)вүҘ0 or g2(s+)вүҘ0.
No common 4-secant line exists between C1вҖӢ and C2вҖӢвҹәg1вҖӢ(sвҲ’вҖӢ)вүҘ0 or g2вҖӢ(s+вҖӢ)вүҘ0.вҖӢ

That is the attack result.

So the remaining global obstruction is no longer vague. It is exactly this:

at the left end of the common slope range, is the tangent-support level of C1C1вҖӢ already above the secant ceiling of C2C2вҖӢ? Or at the right end, is the tangent-support level of C2C2вҖӢ already above the secant ceiling of C1C1вҖӢ?

Those are concrete endpoint comparisons.

There are four endpoint cases depending on which component contributes sвҲ’sвҲ’вҖӢ and which contributes s+s+вҖӢ. For example, if
sвҲ’=Y2вҖІ(a2),s+=Y1вҖІ(b1),
sвҲ’вҖӢ=Y2вҖІвҖӢ(a2вҖӢ),s+вҖӢ=Y1вҖІвҖӢ(b1вҖӢ),

then
m2(sвҲ’)=Y2(a2)вҲ’sвҲ’a2,m1(s+)=Y1(b1)вҲ’s+b1,
m2вҖӢ(sвҲ’вҖӢ)=Y2вҖӢ(a2вҖӢ)вҲ’sвҲ’вҖӢa2вҖӢ,m1вҖӢ(s+вҖӢ)=Y1вҖӢ(b1вҖӢ)вҲ’s+вҖӢb1вҖӢ,

and
M1(s+)=maxвҒЎ{Y1(a1)вҲ’s+a1, Y1(b1)вҲ’s+b1},
M1вҖӢ(s+вҖӢ)=max{Y1вҖӢ(a1вҖӢ)вҲ’s+вҖӢa1вҖӢ, Y1вҖӢ(b1вҖӢ)вҲ’s+вҖӢb1вҖӢ},
M2(sвҲ’)=maxвҒЎ{Y2(a2)вҲ’sвҲ’a2, Y2(b2)вҲ’sвҲ’b2}.
M2вҖӢ(sвҲ’вҖӢ)=max{Y2вҖӢ(a2вҖӢ)вҲ’sвҲ’вҖӢa2вҖӢ, Y2вҖӢ(b2вҖӢ)вҲ’sвҲ’вҖӢb2вҖӢ}.

So the two tests become explicit affine inequalities in endpoint data.

That is now the real next task:

for the actual curve
x(h)=v5(h)c(h),y(h)=О”7(h)c(h),
x(h)=c(h)v5вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ,y(h)=c(h)О”7вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ,

understand the endpoint geometry of each convex component well enough to decide the signs of
g1(sвҲ’),g2(s+).
g1вҖӢ(sвҲ’вҖӢ),g2вҖӢ(s+вҖӢ).

This is as far as the abstract convexity argument goes. It has turned the last obstruction into a check on endpoint tangent and secant data.

So the attack target now is very specific:
derive whatever asymptotics, ordering, or sign information you can for the endpoints of adjacent convex components of
(v5c,О”7c).
(cv5вҖӢвҖӢ,cО”7вҖӢвҖӢ).

If those endpoint inequalities come out the right way, the cross-component 4-secant obstruction dies, and with it the remaining generic minimal-core configurations.

Continue

Here is the next honest continuation.

At this point, the abstract convexity machinery has reduced the remaining obstruction as far as it will go without actual endpoint information for the convex components of
hвҶҰ(v5(h)c(h),О”7(h)c(h)).
hвҶҰ(c(h)v5вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ,c(h)О”7вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ).

So the next attack is not another abstract lemma. It is to identify what must be shown at the endpoints.

For adjacent ordered convex components
C1: y=Y1(x), xвҲҲ[a1,b1],C2: y=Y2(x), xвҲҲ[a2,b2],b1<a2,
C1вҖӢ: y=Y1вҖӢ(x), xвҲҲ[a1вҖӢ,b1вҖӢ],C2вҖӢ: y=Y2вҖӢ(x), xвҲҲ[a2вҖӢ,b2вҖӢ],b1вҖӢ<a2вҖӢ,

we already reduced common 4-secants to the endpoint test
g1(sвҲ’)вүҘ0org2(s+)вүҘ0,
g1вҖӢ(sвҲ’вҖӢ)вүҘ0org2вҖӢ(s+вҖӢ)вүҘ0,

where
g1(s)=m1(s)вҲ’M2(s),g2(s)=m2(s)вҲ’M1(s),

and sВұsВұвҖӢ are the endpoints of the common tangent-slope range.

So the only real remaining question is:
How are the endpoint tangent lines of C1 and C2 positioned relative to the opposite component?
How are the endpoint tangent lines of C1вҖӢ and C2вҖӢ positioned relative to the opposite component?вҖӢ

That is the right geometric bottleneck.

There is a very clean sufficient condition.

If for every adjacent pair C1<C2C1вҖӢ<C2вҖӢ,

    the right-end tangent line of C1C1вҖӢ lies below all of C2C2вҖӢ, or

    the left-end tangent line of C2C2вҖӢ lies above all of C1C1вҖӢ,

then no common 4-secant line can exist between them.

Why: those conditions are exactly
g1(sвҲ’)вүҘ0org2(s+)вүҘ0
g1вҖӢ(sвҲ’вҖӢ)вүҘ0org2вҖӢ(s+вҖӢ)вүҘ0

written geometrically.

So the next theorem to attack is:
Adjacent convex components are endpoint-separated by an endpoint tangent line.
Adjacent convex components are endpoint-separated by an endpoint tangent line.вҖӢ

This is now the sharpest possible formulation.

It also has a natural analytic version in the original parameter hh. Since
x(h)=v5(h)c(h),y(h)=О”7(h)c(h),
x(h)=c(h)v5вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ,y(h)=c(h)О”7вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ,

the endpoint tangent slope is
m(h)=dydx=cвҖүО”7вҖІвҲ’cвҖІО”7cвҖүv5вҖІвҲ’cвҖІv5.
m(h)=dxdyвҖӢ=cv5вҖІвҖӢвҲ’cвҖІv5вҖӢcО”7вҖІвҖӢвҲ’cвҖІО”7вҖӢвҖӢ.

So an endpoint tangent separation statement becomes a sign statement about
y(h)вҲ’m(hвҲ—)вҖүx(h)
y(h)вҲ’m(hвҲ—вҖӢ)x(h)

evaluated on the neighboring component.

Concretely, if hвҲ—hвҲ—вҖӢ is the right endpoint of C1C1вҖӢ, then the tangent line there is
в„“вҲ—(x)=y(hвҲ—)+m(hвҲ—)(xвҲ’x(hвҲ—)).
в„“вҲ—вҖӢ(x)=y(hвҲ—вҖӢ)+m(hвҲ—вҖӢ)(xвҲ’x(hвҲ—вҖӢ)).

To show it lies below C2C2вҖӢ, you need
y(h)вҲ’в„“вҲ—(x(h))>0for all hвҲҲC2.
y(h)вҲ’в„“вҲ—вҖӢ(x(h))>0for all hвҲҲC2вҖӢ.

ОҳhвҲ—(h):=О”7(h)c(hвҲ—)вҲ’О”7(hвҲ—)c(h)вҲ’m(hвҲ—)(v5(h)c(hвҲ—)вҲ’v5(hвҲ—)c(h)).
ОҳhвҲ—вҖӢвҖӢ(h):=О”7вҖӢ(h)c(hвҲ—вҖӢ)вҲ’О”7вҖӢ(hвҲ—вҖӢ)c(h)вҲ’m(hвҲ—вҖӢ)(v5вҖӢ(h)c(hвҲ—вҖӢ)вҲ’v5вҖӢ(hвҲ—вҖӢ)c(h)).

y(h)вҲ’в„“вҲ—(x(h))
y(h)вҲ’в„“вҲ—вҖӢ(x(h))

cleared of denominators. Its sign on the neighboring component decides whether the endpoint tangent separates the two branches.

That is now a completely concrete target.

So the next attack should be:

    identify adjacent convex components in the hh-parameter;

    at an endpoint hвҲ—hвҲ—вҖӢ, form the tangent-separation scalar ОҳhвҲ—(h)ОҳhвҲ—вҖӢвҖӢ(h);

    see whether its sign on the neighboring component is controlled by the already-known monotonicity of xx, mm, and curvature.

There is one more important point.

At this stage, the proof effort has genuinely split:

    the local 3/5/7 analysis has given a strong curve (x(h),y(h))(x(h),y(h));

    the remaining problem is now a global endpoint-separation theorem for adjacent convex branches.

That is a different kind of mathematics from the earlier jet algebra.

So my blunt assessment is:
The next real progress will come from proving endpoint tangent separation, not from more local coefficient expansions.
The next real progress will come from proving endpoint tangent separation, not from more local coefficient expansions.вҖӢ

That is the battlefield now.

Attack

IвҖҷm attacking the endpoint-separation question by splitting adjacent branch boundaries into cases: xвҖІ=0xвҖІ=0, K=0K=0, or loss of the cвү 0cоҖ =0 chart. If one of those cases already forces slope-range separation, the global problem gets much smaller immediately.

Here is a real attack result.

The endpoint-separation problem splits into two different kinds of adjacent convex components, and one of them is now basically done.

Work in the affine chart
x(h)=v5(h)c(h),y(h)=О”7(h)c(h),
x(h)=c(h)v5вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ,y(h)=c(h)О”7вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ,

and on the generic region define
xвҖІ(h)вү 0,K(h):=xвҖІ(h)yвҖІвҖІ(h)вҲ’yвҖІ(h)xвҖІвҖІ(h)вү 0.
xвҖІ(h)оҖ =0,K(h):=xвҖІ(h)yвҖІвҖІ(h)вҲ’yвҖІ(h)xвҖІвҖІ(h)оҖ =0.

Convex components are connected components of
{cвү 0, xвҖІвү 0, Kвү 0}.
{cоҖ =0, xвҖІоҖ =0, KоҖ =0}.

Define the tangent slope
m(h):=dydx=yвҖІ(h)xвҖІ(h).
m(h):=dxdyвҖӢ=xвҖІ(h)yвҖІ(h)вҖӢ.

Then
dmdh=K(h)xвҖІ(h)2.
dhdmвҖӢ=xвҖІ(h)2K(h)вҖӢ.

So on any component, the sign of mвҖІ(h)mвҖІ(h) is the sign of K(h)K(h). In particular, mm is strictly monotone on each convex component.

That is the key fact.

Now take two convex components that are adjacent in the hh-parameter and separated by a boundary point hвҲ—hвҲ—вҖӢ. There are two cases.

First case: the boundary is caused by
K(hвҲ—)=0,xвҖІ(hвҲ—)вү 0.
K(hвҲ—вҖӢ)=0,xвҖІ(hвҲ—вҖӢ)оҖ =0.

Then xx stays a good parameter across the boundary, but curvature changes sign. Since
mвҖІ(h)=K(h)xвҖІ(h)2,
mвҖІ(h)=xвҖІ(h)2K(h)вҖӢ,

the tangent slope m(h)m(h) is monotone on each side with opposite monotonicity. Therefore the tangent-slope ranges of the two adjacent convex components meet at at most one endpoint value, namely
m(hвҲ—).
m(hвҲ—вҖӢ).

So their slope intervals have no common interior.

But a line is 2-secant to a strictly convex graph only for slopes in the interior of its tangent-slope interval. Therefore:
Two adjacent convex components separated by a K=0 boundary cannot share a common 4-secant line.
Two adjacent convex components separated by a K=0 boundary cannot share a common 4-secant line.вҖӢ

That is a genuine theorem-level gain.

It means the whole earlier support-function overlap problem is completely unnecessary for curvature-flip adjacencies. Those are harmless.

Second case: the boundary is caused by
xвҖІ(hвҲ—)=0.
xвҖІ(hвҲ—вҖӢ)=0.

This is different. Here the affine graph parameter xx itself breaks down, typically at a vertical tangent or xx-extremum. These are the only boundaries where the previous slope-interval argument does not automatically kill cross-component secants.

So the remaining global obstruction is now much smaller:
Only components separated by xвҖІ=0 boundaries can still potentially share common 4-secant lines.
Only components separated by xвҖІ=0 boundaries can still potentially share common 4-secant lines.вҖӢ

That is the real reduction.

And it is a big one, because it means all the вҖңadjacent convex componentsвҖқ created merely by curvature sign changes are already ruled out. The only problematic adjacencies are turning points of the xx-coordinate.

So the next exact attack is no longer broad вҖңadjacent components.вҖқ It is specifically:

    What happens near an xвҖІ=0xвҖІ=0 boundary?

That is a much more concrete local geometry problem.

Near such a point hвҲ—hвҲ—вҖӢ, you have
xвҖІ(hвҲ—)=0.
xвҖІ(hвҲ—вҖӢ)=0.

Generically, if xвҖІвҖІ(hвҲ—)вү 0xвҖІвҖІ(hвҲ—вҖӢ)оҖ =0, then xx has a local extremum there, so the two neighboring convex components lie on opposite sides of the same vertical tangent in the affine plane. That suggests a strong picture: the components are not simply two disjoint ordered graphs; they are two branches meeting the same vertical support line. In that geometry, a nonvertical line typically cannot be 2-secant to both branches twice unless a very special symmetry occurs.

So the next theorem to attack is:
Adjacent convex components separated by a generic xвҖІ=0 boundary do not admit a common 4-secant line.
Adjacent convex components separated by a generic xвҖІ=0 boundary do not admit a common 4-secant line.вҖӢ

If you get that, then all adjacent-component pair-pair configurations are gone.

And that would be huge, because then any minimal bad core could have pairs only on components that are not adjacent in the hh-parameter, which makes the support extremely spread out and much easier to constrain.

So the current state is:

    curvature-zero boundaries are already harmless;

    the only remaining geometric obstruction is at xвҖІ=0xвҖІ=0 turning points.

That is now the sharpest battlefield.

Good. This is now the right place to attack.

Let hвҲ—hвҲ—вҖӢ be a boundary point between two adjacent convex components caused by
xвҖІ(hвҲ—)=0.
xвҖІ(hвҲ—вҖӢ)=0.

Assume the generic nondegenerate case
xвҖІвҖІ(hвҲ—)вү 0.
xвҖІвҖІ(hвҲ—вҖӢ)оҖ =0.

Why: xвҖІ(0)=0xвҖІ(0)=0, xвҖІвҖІ(0)=2Оұвү 0xвҖІвҖІ(0)=2ОұоҖ =0, while for a generic boundary the curve is still regular, so yвҖІ(0)=ОІвү 0yвҖІ(0)=ОІоҖ =0 after possibly swapping coordinates. The two adjacent convex components are the t<0t<0 and t>0t>0 branches of this same local arc.

Now ask whether one affine line can meet both branches twice near t=0t=0. Write the line as
y=sx+b.
y=sx+b.

Substitute:
F(t):=y(t)вҲ’sx(t)вҲ’b=(y0вҲ’sx0вҲ’b)+ОІt+(ОівҲ’sОұ)t2+O(t3).
F(t):=y(t)вҲ’sx(t)вҲ’b=(y0вҖӢвҲ’sx0вҖӢвҲ’b)+ОІt+(ОівҲ’sОұ)t2+O(t3).

This is the real local obstruction.

A common 4-secant near the turning point would require FF to have two zeros on t<0t<0 and two zeros on t>0t>0. But generically FF is a cubic-order perturbation of a function with a nonzero linear term ОІtОІt. So near t=0t=0, FF has at most one zero on each side, not two on each side.

More concretely:

    because ОІвү 0ОІоҖ =0, FвҖІ(0)=ОІвү 0FвҖІ(0)=ОІоҖ =0;

    therefore FF is monotone in a neighborhood of 00;

    hence FF has at most one zero in that neighborhood.

So a single line cannot produce a local 4-secant configuration across the two branches near a generic xвҖІ=0xвҖІ=0 boundary.

That gives the theorem:
Adjacent convex components separated by a generic xвҖІ=0 boundary do not admit a common 4-secant line in a sufficiently small neighborhood of the boundary.
Adjacent convex components separated by a generic xвҖІ=0 boundary do not admit a common 4-secant line in a sufficiently small neighborhood of the boundary.вҖӢ

This is a real gain. Combined with the earlier curvature-zero result, it means:

    components separated by K=0K=0 are harmless;

    components separated by generic xвҖІ=0xвҖІ=0 are locally harmless too.

So all adjacent pair-pair obstructions are now gone, at least generically and locally.

That is a big step.

What remains globally is only this:

could a line be 2-secant to two non-adjacent convex components?

That is a much more spread-out configuration. It is no longer a local adjacency problem. And that makes it much more plausible that compactness/minimality can handle it, because the support would have to jump over at least one intermediate component.

So the next exact theorem to attack is:
A minimal bad core cannot have pairs on two non-adjacent convex components.
A minimal bad core cannot have pairs on two non-adjacent convex components.вҖӢ

The natural route is an ordering argument: if components are ordered in xx, then a line meeting component CiCiвҖӢ twice and component CkCkвҖӢ twice with i<j<ki<j<k should typically force at least one additional intersection with the intermediate component CjCjвҖӢ, contradicting the вҖңat most two points per componentвҖқ rule or the ECT property there.

That is now the sharpest remaining global obstruction.

A common 4-secant line for CiCiвҖӢ and CjCjвҖӢ exists iff there is a slope ss with
gij(1)(s)<0,gij(2)(s)<0.
gij(1)вҖӢ(s)<0,gij(2)вҖӢ(s)<0.

Now the key comparison is this:

if i<j<ki<j<k, then because the xx-ranges are ordered,
Mk(s)вүӨMj(s)
MkвҖӢ(s)вүӨMjвҖӢ(s)

for every ss, and
mk(s)вүӨmj(s)
mkвҖӢ(s)вүӨmjвҖӢ(s)

need not hold, but what matters is the pair of inequalities against a fixed left component CiCiвҖӢ.

For the first obstruction function,
gik(1)(s)=mi(s)вҲ’Mk(s).
gik(1)вҖӢ(s)=miвҖӢ(s)вҲ’MkвҖӢ(s).

Since CkCkвҖӢ lies farther to the right than CjCjвҖӢ, the upper envelope MkMkвҖӢ is shifted more strongly by slope than MjMjвҖӢ. In fact the derivative comparison is stricter:
dds(Mj(s)вҲ’Mk(s))>0
dsdвҖӢ(MjвҖӢ(s)вҲ’MkвҖӢ(s))>0

piecewise, because all endpoint xx-coordinates of CkCkвҖӢ exceed those of CjCjвҖӢ. So once gij(1)gij(1)вҖӢ is nonnegative at one end of the common slope range, gik(1)gik(1)вҖӢ is even less favorable to overlap.

Likewise for the second obstruction function,
gik(2)(s)=mk(s)вҲ’Mi(s),
gik(2)вҖӢ(s)=mkвҖӢ(s)вҲ’MiвҖӢ(s),

the farther-right component CkCkвҖӢ has minimizer xk(s)xkвҖӢ(s) strictly larger than that of CjCjвҖӢ, so
mkвҖІ(s)=вҲ’xk(s)<вҲ’xj(s)=mjвҖІ(s).
mkвҖІвҖӢ(s)=вҲ’xkвҖӢ(s)<вҲ’xjвҖӢ(s)=mjвҖІвҖӢ(s).

Thus gik(2)gik(2)вҖӢ decreases faster than gij(2)gij(2)вҖӢ.

What this means geometrically is:
If adjacent pairs are endpoint-separated, then non-adjacent pairs are even more separated.
If adjacent pairs are endpoint-separated, then non-adjacent pairs are even more separated.вҖӢ

So the real next theorem to aim for is:

    It is enough to rule out common 4-secant lines for adjacent convex components.

Because once that is done, monotonicity in the xx-ordering propagates the exclusion to all farther-separated pairs.

That is a genuine simplification.

So the entire remaining global problem has now collapsed to:
For each adjacent pair of convex components, prove endpoint tangent separation.
For each adjacent pair of convex components, prove endpoint tangent separation.вҖӢ

And once that is proved, the consequences are strong:

    no component can share a 4-secant line with any other component;

    each convex component contributes at most two support points;

    and at most one component can contribute a pair in a bad core.

At that point a minimal bad core on the generic region would have support pattern:

    either all singletons, or

    one pair plus singletons.

That is much smaller than where we started.

Now, can we push the adjacent endpoint-separation a bit further abstractly?

Yes. There are only two generic kinds of adjacent boundaries:

    K=0K=0 with xвҖІвү 0xвҖІоҖ =0, which we already handled: tangent-slope ranges only touch at an endpoint, so no common 4-secant.

    xвҖІ=0xвҖІ=0 with xвҖІвҖІвү 0xвҖІвҖІоҖ =0, where the local normal form
    x(t)=x0+Оұt2+O(t3),y(t)=y0+ОІt+Оіt2+O(t3),ОІвү 0
    x(t)=x0вҖӢ+Оұt2+O(t3),y(t)=y0вҖӢ+ОІt+Оіt2+O(t3),ОІоҖ =0

    already shows no local common 4-secant near the boundary.

So the only way an adjacent pair could still share a common 4-secant is via a genuinely nonlocal line cutting both branches far from the boundary. That is exactly where the endpoint tangent-separation scalar
ОҳhвҲ—(h)=О”7(h)c(hвҲ—)вҲ’О”7(hвҲ—)c(h)вҲ’m(hвҲ—)(v5(h)c(hвҲ—)вҲ’v5(hвҲ—)c(h))
ОҳhвҲ—вҖӢвҖӢ(h)=О”7вҖӢ(h)c(hвҲ—вҖӢ)вҲ’О”7вҖӢ(hвҲ—вҖӢ)c(h)вҲ’m(hвҲ—вҖӢ)(v5вҖӢ(h)c(hвҲ—вҖӢ)вҲ’v5вҖӢ(hвҲ—вҖӢ)c(h))

comes in.

So the next precise attack is:

    take an adjacent boundary point hвҲ—hвҲ—вҖӢ,

    compute the tangent slope
    m(hвҲ—)=c(hвҲ—)О”7вҖІ(hвҲ—)вҲ’cвҖІ(hвҲ—)О”7(hвҲ—)c(hвҲ—)v5вҖІ(hвҲ—)вҲ’cвҖІ(hвҲ—)v5(hвҲ—),
    m(hвҲ—вҖӢ)=c(hвҲ—вҖӢ)v5вҖІвҖӢ(hвҲ—вҖӢ)вҲ’cвҖІ(hвҲ—вҖӢ)v5вҖӢ(hвҲ—вҖӢ)c(hвҲ—вҖӢ)О”7вҖІвҖӢ(hвҲ—вҖӢ)вҲ’cвҖІ(hвҲ—вҖӢ)О”7вҖӢ(hвҲ—вҖӢ)вҖӢ,

    study the sign of ОҳhвҲ—(h)ОҳhвҲ—вҖӢвҖӢ(h) on the neighboring component.

If ОҳhвҲ—(h)ОҳhвҲ—вҖӢвҖӢ(h) keeps one sign there, then the endpoint tangent line separates the two components, and adjacent common 4-secants are impossible.

So the current attack has reached a very sharp endpoint:

the remaining proof obligation is a sign theorem for ОҳhвҲ—(h)ОҳhвҲ—вҖӢвҖӢ(h) on adjacent branches. If that goes through, the whole generic pair-pair obstruction disappears.

Continue

Here is the next honest push.

I do not think we can honestly prove the full sign theorem for
ОҳhвҲ—(h)=О”7(h)c(hвҲ—)вҲ’О”7(hвҲ—)c(h)вҲ’m(hвҲ—)(v5(h)c(hвҲ—)вҲ’v5(hвҲ—)c(h))
ОҳhвҲ—вҖӢвҖӢ(h)=О”7вҖӢ(h)c(hвҲ—вҖӢ)вҲ’О”7вҖӢ(hвҲ—вҖӢ)c(h)вҲ’m(hвҲ—вҖӢ)(v5вҖӢ(h)c(hвҲ—вҖӢ)вҲ’v5вҖӢ(hвҲ—вҖӢ)c(h))

on an entire neighboring component from the current information alone.

But we can push it one important step further:

we can show what the sign theorem would reduce to locally, and exactly what extra input is missing.

Write the affine graph coordinates as
x(h)=v5(h)c(h),y(h)=О”7(h)c(h).
x(h)=c(h)v5вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ,y(h)=c(h)О”7вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ.

Then
ОҳhвҲ—(h)=c(h)c(hвҲ—)(y(h)вҲ’в„“hвҲ—(x(h))),
ОҳhвҲ—вҖӢвҖӢ(h)=c(h)c(hвҲ—вҖӢ)(y(h)вҲ’в„“hвҲ—вҖӢвҖӢ(x(h))),

where в„“hвҲ—в„“hвҲ—вҖӢвҖӢ is the tangent line at the boundary point hвҲ—hвҲ—вҖӢ:
в„“hвҲ—(x)=y(hвҲ—)+m(hвҲ—)(xвҲ’x(hвҲ—)).
в„“hвҲ—вҖӢвҖӢ(x)=y(hвҲ—вҖӢ)+m(hвҲ—вҖӢ)(xвҲ’x(hвҲ—вҖӢ)).

So the sign of ОҳhвҲ—ОҳhвҲ—вҖӢвҖӢ is exactly the sign of вҖңneighboring branch minus the tangent line at hвҲ—hвҲ—вҖӢ,вҖқ up to the sign of c(h)c(hвҲ—)c(h)c(hвҲ—вҖӢ).

That means the problem is pure planar geometry.

Now take the generic xвҖІ=0xвҖІ=0 boundary case. After shifting hвҲ—=0hвҲ—вҖӢ=0 and the point to the origin, the local normal form is
x(t)=Оұt2+Оұ3t3+O(t4),Оұвү 0,
x(t)=Оұt2+Оұ3вҖӢt3+O(t4),ОұоҖ =0,
y(t)=ОІt+Оіt2+Оҙt3+O(t4),ОІвү 0.
y(t)=ОІt+Оіt2+Оҙt3+O(t4),ОІоҖ =0.

This is the right place to compute.

First, since xвҖІ(0)=0xвҖІ(0)=0 and yвҖІ(0)=ОІвү 0yвҖІ(0)=ОІоҖ =0, the tangent is vertical in the (x,y)(x,y)-chart. So the вҖңendpoint tangent lineвҖқ is not a finite-slope affine line at all: it is the vertical line
x=0.
x=0.

That is very important. It means the earlier formula using the slope
m(hвҲ—)=yвҖІxвҖІ
m(hвҲ—вҖӢ)=xвҖІyвҖІвҖӢ

does not make sense at a generic xвҖІ=0xвҖІ=0 boundary. So the endpoint-tangent-separation theorem splits again:

    finite-slope endpoint tangents for ordinary component endpoints;

    vertical tangents at xвҖІ=0xвҖІ=0 turning points.

For the xвҖІ=0xвҖІ=0 case, the correct separation scalar is not ОҳhвҲ—ОҳhвҲ—вҖӢвҖӢ built from m(hвҲ—)m(hвҲ—вҖӢ). It is simply
x(h)вҲ’x(hвҲ—).
x(h)вҲ’x(hвҲ—вҖӢ).

And now the local geometry is actually easy:

from
x(t)=Оұt2+O(t3),
x(t)=Оұt2+O(t3),

both adjacent branches lie on the same side of the vertical tangent x=0x=0 if Оұ>0Оұ>0, or the opposite side if Оұ<0Оұ<0. So the vertical tangent line itself does not separate the two branches. That means vertical-tangent separation is the wrong picture.

But recall what we already proved locally: because
y(t)=ОІt+O(t2),ОІвү 0,
y(t)=ОІt+O(t2),ОІоҖ =0,

any ordinary affine line intersects the local union of the two branches in at most one point near t=0t=0. So local 4-secant obstruction is already gone. The remaining issue is only nonlocal secants hitting the two branches far from the turning point.

So for xвҖІ=0xвҖІ=0 boundaries, the correct global question is not tangent separation by the tangent line at the boundary. It is whether some nonlocal secant line can cut both branches twice. That needs more than the local normal form.

Now look at the other boundary type: K=0K=0, xвҖІвү 0xвҖІоҖ =0. There the slope
m(h)=yвҖІxвҖІ
m(h)=xвҖІyвҖІвҖӢ

is finite and smooth, and the earlier endpoint-tangent-separation scalar ОҳhвҲ—ОҳhвҲ—вҖӢвҖӢ makes sense. But in that case we already showed something stronger: the tangent-slope intervals of the two adjacent convex components only meet at an endpoint, so no common 4-secant exists anyway. So ОҳhвҲ—ОҳhвҲ—вҖӢвҖӢ is not needed there either.

This is the real conclusion:
ОҳhвҲ— is not the universal remaining bottleneck.
ОҳhвҲ—вҖӢвҖӢ is not the universal remaining bottleneck.вҖӢ

At K=0K=0 boundaries, adjacency is already harmless.
At xвҖІ=0xвҖІ=0 boundaries, the tangent is vertical and ОҳhвҲ—ОҳhвҲ—вҖӢвҖӢ is not the right object.

So the adjacent-component problem is actually basically done generically:

    K=0K=0 boundaries: no common 4-secant by slope-range separation.

    xвҖІ=0xвҖІ=0 boundaries: no local common 4-secant by the normal form.

What remains is genuinely nonlocal: could one line cut two non-adjacent convex components twice, or cut two branches adjacent across an xвҖІ=0xвҖІ=0 turning point twice but far from the turning point?

That is no longer an endpoint tangent problem. It is a global line-incidence problem for the whole planar curve
hвҶҰ(v5(h)c(h),О”7(h)c(h)).
hвҶҰ(c(h)v5вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ,c(h)О”7вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ).

So the next honest attack is:

    stop trying to prove ОҳhвҲ—ОҳhвҲ—вҖӢвҖӢ sign on every adjacent branch;

    treat K=0K=0 adjacencies as solved;

    treat xвҖІ=0xвҖІ=0 adjacencies as locally solved;

    focus on the truly remaining question:

Can one affine line meet two separated convex graph components twice each?
Can one affine line meet two separated convex graph components twice each?вҖӢ

And that is where the support-function overlap formulation
mi(s)<b<Mi(s)
miвҖӢ(s)<b<MiвҖӢ(s)

for each component is still the right global tool.

So the attack has clarified the battlefield again:

the adjacent-boundary tangent-sign theorem is not the right universal endpoint. The real remaining obstruction is purely global 4-secant incidence across separated convex components.

Continue

IвҖҷm staying with the global 4-secant obstruction now. The adjacent-boundary analysis seems basically exhausted; the remaining question is whether a line can hit two separated convex graph components twice each, and that is now a support-function overlap problem rather than another local expansion.

Here is the next honest push.

The remaining obstruction is now genuinely this:
Can one affine line be 2-secant to two separated convex graph components?
Can one affine line be 2-secant to two separated convex graph components?вҖӢ

And the right language is still the support-function one.

Take two separated convex graph components
Ci: y=Yi(x),xвҲҲ[ai,bi],i=1,2,
CiвҖӢ: y=YiвҖӢ(x),xвҲҲ[aiвҖӢ,biвҖӢ],i=1,2,

with
b1<a2.
b1вҖӢ<a2вҖӢ.

For slope ss, define
mi(s):=minвҒЎxвҲҲ[ai,bi](Yi(x)вҲ’sx),Mi(s):=maxвҒЎxвҲҲ[ai,bi](Yi(x)вҲ’sx).
miвҖӢ(s):=xвҲҲ[aiвҖӢ,biвҖӢ]minвҖӢ(YiвҖӢ(x)вҲ’sx),MiвҖӢ(s):=xвҲҲ[aiвҖӢ,biвҖӢ]maxвҖӢ(YiвҖӢ(x)вҲ’sx).

Then a line y=sx+by=sx+b is 2-secant to CiCiвҖӢ iff
mi(s)<b<Mi(s).
miвҖӢ(s)<b<MiвҖӢ(s).

So a common 4-secant line exists iff there is an ss such that
m1(s)<M2(s)andm2(s)<M1(s).
m1вҖӢ(s)<M2вҖӢ(s)andm2вҖӢ(s)<M1вҖӢ(s).

Now the crucial observation is:

for separated components, the second inequality
m2(s)<M1(s)
m2вҖӢ(s)<M1вҖӢ(s)

is the hard one, not the first.

Why? Because C2C2вҖӢ is to the right of C1C1вҖӢ, so m1m1вҖӢ is the lower support of the left branch and M2M2вҖӢ is the upper secant ceiling of the right branch. There is usually plenty of room for
m1(s)<M2(s).
m1вҖӢ(s)<M2вҖӢ(s).

But
m2(s)<M1(s)
m2вҖӢ(s)<M1вҖӢ(s)

means the tangent support of the right branch drops below the top secant envelope of the left branch. That is the real crossing condition.

So define the key scalar
G12(s):=m2(s)вҲ’M1(s).
G12вҖӢ(s):=m2вҖӢ(s)вҲ’M1вҖӢ(s).

Then a common 4-secant requires
G12(s)<0.
G12вҖӢ(s)<0.

Now, because C2C2вҖӢ is to the right of C1C1вҖӢ,

    m2вҖІ(s)=вҲ’x2(s)m2вҖІвҖӢ(s)=вҲ’x2вҖӢ(s), where x2(s)вҲҲ[a2,b2]x2вҖӢ(s)вҲҲ[a2вҖӢ,b2вҖӢ],

    M1вҖІ(s)вҲҲ{вҲ’a1,вҲ’b1}M1вҖІвҖӢ(s)вҲҲ{вҲ’a1вҖӢ,вҲ’b1вҖӢ}.

Hence
G12вҖІ(s)=вҲ’x2(s)+e1(s),e1(s)вҲҲ{a1,b1}.
G12вҖІвҖӢ(s)=вҲ’x2вҖӢ(s)+e1вҖӢ(s),e1вҖӢ(s)вҲҲ{a1вҖӢ,b1вҖӢ}.

Since
e1(s)вүӨb1<a2вүӨx2(s),
e1вҖӢ(s)вүӨb1вҖӢ<a2вҖӢвүӨx2вҖӢ(s),

we get
G12вҖІ(s)<0.
G12вҖІвҖӢ(s)<0.

So this function is strictly decreasing.

That is the real one-variable monotonicity theorem:
G12(s)=m2(s)вҲ’M1(s) is strictly decreasing on any common slope range.
G12вҖӢ(s)=m2вҖӢ(s)вҲ’M1вҖӢ(s) is strictly decreasing on any common slope range.вҖӢ

This has a strong consequence:

the set of slopes ss for which a common 4-secant can exist is either empty or a single interval determined by one threshold crossing of G12G12вҖӢ.

So the problem is reduced to checking the sign of G12G12вҖӢ at one reference slope.

And now there is a natural reference slope:
the slope where the support point on C2C2вҖӢ is at its leftmost endpoint a2a2вҖӢ, namely
s=Y2вҖІ(a2),
s=Y2вҖІвҖӢ(a2вҖӢ),

assuming increasing slope on the branch.

At that slope,
m2(s)=Y2(a2)вҲ’sa2.
m2вҖӢ(s)=Y2вҖӢ(a2вҖӢ)вҲ’sa2вҖӢ.

And
M1(s)=maxвҒЎ{Y1(a1)вҲ’sa1,вҖ…вҖҠY1(b1)вҲ’sb1}.
M1вҖӢ(s)=max{Y1вҖӢ(a1вҖӢ)вҲ’sa1вҖӢ,Y1вҖӢ(b1вҖӢ)вҲ’sb1вҖӢ}.

So the existence of any common 4-secant line between C1C1вҖӢ and C2C2вҖӢ is controlled by a single endpoint comparison:
Y2(a2)вҲ’sa2<?maxвҒЎ{Y1(a1)вҲ’sa1,вҖ…вҖҠY1(b1)вҲ’sb1},s=Y2вҖІ(a2).
Y2вҖӢ(a2вҖӢ)вҲ’sa2вҖӢ<?вҖӢmax{Y1вҖӢ(a1вҖӢ)вҲ’sa1вҖӢ,Y1вҖӢ(b1вҖӢ)вҲ’sb1вҖӢ},s=Y2вҖІвҖӢ(a2вҖӢ).

Geometrically, this asks whether the left-end tangent support value of the right component lies below the secant ceiling of the left component.

That is the cleanest reduction we have.

So the next exact attack should be:

    identify adjacent or separated convex components in the actual curve
    hвҶҰ(v5(h)c(h),О”7(h)c(h));
    hвҶҰ(c(h)v5вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ,c(h)О”7вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ);

    at the left endpoint of each right component C2C2вҖӢ, compute the tangent slope
    s=Y2вҖІ(a2);
    s=Y2вҖІвҖӢ(a2вҖӢ);

    compare
    Y2(a2)вҲ’sa2
    Y2вҖӢ(a2вҖӢ)вҲ’sa2вҖӢ

    against the endpoint secant ceiling of the left component.

If this comparison goes the favorable way, then because G12G12вҖӢ is strictly decreasing, there is no common 4-secant at any slope.

So the battlefield is now a single endpoint inequality per ordered pair of convex components. That is about as far as the abstract reduction can go.

Continue

Here is the next honest push.

At this point, the abstract reduction really has reached its sharp endpoint. For two ordered convex components
C1: y=Y1(x), xвҲҲ[a1,b1],C2: y=Y2(x), xвҲҲ[a2,b2],b1<a2,
C1вҖӢ: y=Y1вҖӢ(x), xвҲҲ[a1вҖӢ,b1вҖӢ],C2вҖӢ: y=Y2вҖӢ(x), xвҲҲ[a2вҖӢ,b2вҖӢ],b1вҖӢ<a2вҖӢ,

the entire 4-secant question is controlled by the single function
G12(s):=m2(s)вҲ’M1(s),
G12вҖӢ(s):=m2вҖӢ(s)вҲ’M1вҖӢ(s),

where
m2(s)=minвҒЎxвҲҲ[a2,b2](Y2(x)вҲ’sx),M1(s)=maxвҒЎxвҲҲ[a1,b1](Y1(x)вҲ’sx).
m2вҖӢ(s)=xвҲҲ[a2вҖӢ,b2вҖӢ]minвҖӢ(Y2вҖӢ(x)вҲ’sx),M1вҖӢ(s)=xвҲҲ[a1вҖӢ,b1вҖӢ]maxвҖӢ(Y1вҖӢ(x)вҲ’sx).

A common 4-secant line requires G12(s)<0G12вҖӢ(s)<0 for some slope ss. And we already proved the decisive monotonicity:
G12вҖІ(s)<0
G12вҖІвҖӢ(s)<0

on any common slope range. So G12G12вҖӢ is strictly decreasing.

That means there is exactly one real remaining question:
What is the sign of G12(s0) at one endpoint slope s0?
What is the sign of G12вҖӢ(s0вҖӢ) at one endpoint slope s0вҖӢ?вҖӢ

If it is nonnegative there, then by monotonicity the whole common-4-secant problem is dead for that ordered pair.

The cleanest reference slope is the left-end tangent slope of the right component:
s0:=Y2вҖІ(a2).
s0вҖӢ:=Y2вҖІвҖӢ(a2вҖӢ).

At this slope,
m2(s0)=Y2(a2)вҲ’s0a2.
m2вҖӢ(s0вҖӢ)=Y2вҖӢ(a2вҖӢ)вҲ’s0вҖӢa2вҖӢ.

So
G12(s0)=Y2(a2)вҲ’s0a2вҲ’maxвҒЎ{Y1(a1)вҲ’s0a1,вҖ…вҖҠY1(b1)вҲ’s0b1}.
G12вҖӢ(s0вҖӢ)=Y2вҖӢ(a2вҖӢ)вҲ’s0вҖӢa2вҖӢвҲ’max{Y1вҖӢ(a1вҖӢ)вҲ’s0вҖӢa1вҖӢ,Y1вҖӢ(b1вҖӢ)вҲ’s0вҖӢb1вҖӢ}.

Geometrically, this is just:

take the tangent line to C2C2вҖӢ at its left endpoint; compare its intercept with the highest endpoint-supported line of C1C1вҖӢ of the same slope.

So the whole remaining obstruction is equivalent to this endpoint tangent/endpoint secant comparison.

Now here is the strongest further simplification.

Because C1C1вҖӢ is convex, if the tangent line to C2C2вҖӢ at a2a2вҖӢ lies above both endpoints of C1C1вҖӢ, then it lies above the entire graph of C1C1вҖӢ. So it is enough to check the two endpoint inequalities

\subsection{Fixed-sector refinement at quintic and septic order}

The trace witness at quintic order suggests a more structured refinement of the
one-pair residual germ than the trace alone records. For bookkeeping purposes,
it is convenient to decompose the quintic and septic coefficients into fixed and
anti-fixed parts with respect to the involution
\[
\iota(A):=J_0AJ_0,
\qquad
J_0=
\begin{pmatrix}
0&1\\
1&0
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Write
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(A):=\frac12(A+J_0AJ_0),
\qquad
\pi_{\mathfrak a}(A):=\frac12(A-J_0AJ_0).
\]
Then
\[
\mathfrak f=\operatorname{span}\{I,S\},
\qquad
\mathfrak a=\operatorname{span}\{D,J\},
\]
where
\[
I=
\begin{pmatrix}
1&0\\0&1
\end{pmatrix},
\quad
S=
\begin{pmatrix}
0&1\\1&0
\end{pmatrix},
\quad
D=
\begin{pmatrix}
1&0\\0&-1
\end{pmatrix},
\quad
J=
\begin{pmatrix}
0&-1\\1&0
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

\begin{definition}[Fixed-sector quintic and septic coefficients]
\label{def:fixed-sector-quintic-septic}
Let
\[
F_h(z)=A_3(h)z^3+A_5(h)z^5+A_7(h)z^7+\cdots
\]
denote the normalized one-pair quotient odd germ on the cubic-generic region.
Its fixed-sector quintic and septic coefficients are defined by
\[
A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h):=\pi_{\mathfrak f}(A_5(h)),
\qquad
A_7^{\mathfrak f}(h):=\pi_{\mathfrak f}(A_7(h)).
\]
Write
\[
A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)=u_5(h)\,I+v_5(h)\,S,
\qquad
A_7^{\mathfrak f}(h)=u_7(h)\,I+v_7(h)\,S.
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Second residual determinant]
\label{def:second-residual-determinant}
On the cubic-generic region, define
\[
\Delta_7(h):=u_7(h)v_5(h)-u_5(h)v_7(h).
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{remark}[Interpretation]
\label{rem:fixed-sector-residual-package}
The quintic fixed-sector vector \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)\) refines the first
residual data beyond the trace detector alone, and the determinant
\(\Delta_7(h)\) records the next residual quotient at septic order. In the
working notes, these form the first two nontrivial layers of a one-pair
residual package:
\[
c(h),\qquad A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h),\qquad \Delta_7(h).
\]
The current draft has not yet fully translated these objects into the existing
coefficient notation \((u,v,w,\alpha,\beta,\gamma,r,\dots)\), but they are the
natural variables for the geometric reformulation of the multi-pair problem.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Work in progress: fixed-sector geometry]
\label{rem:wip-fixed-sector-geometry}
The later local analysis suggests the normalized affine chart
\[
x(h):=\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},
\qquad
y(h):=\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}
\]
on the cubic-generic region. The intended geometric program is to study the
planar curve
\[
h\longmapsto (x(h),y(h))
\]
rather than only the scalar quintic trace. In this formulation, the remaining
generic obstruction is reduced to common four-secant lines of this planar
curve.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Derived structural identities]
\label{rem:derived-fixed-sector-identities}
The working analysis gives the following identities, which should be proved in
a later draft in the paper's native coefficient language.

\begin{enumerate}
\item If
\[
X_1=
\begin{pmatrix}
u&v\\v&w
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
X_3=
\begin{pmatrix}
\alpha&\beta\\\beta&\gamma
\end{pmatrix},
\]
then the quintic fixed-sector \(S\)-component coming from the commutator-type
channel is
\[
v_5=\gamma u-\alpha w.
\]

\item The direct linear \(q^{(7)}\)-driven contribution to the septic
fixed-sector coefficient is \(I\)-directional. In particular, on that slice,
\[
v_7^{(q^{(7)})}=0,
\qquad
\Delta_7^{(q^{(7)})}=u_7^{(q^{(7)})}v_5.
\]
Thus the determinant \(\Delta_7\) is the natural second residual scalar once
\(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\) has been fixed.
\end{enumerate}
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Work in progress: planar convexity reformulation]
\label{rem:wip-planar-convexity-reformulation}

should be reformulated as a planar geometry problem for the curve
\[

\[

\qquad

the image breaks into strictly convex graph components. On each such component,
a line meets the curve in at most two points. The remaining generic global
obstruction is therefore reduced to the possibility of a common four-secant
line meeting two separated convex components twice each.

In this formulation, the unresolved endgame is no longer local quintic
bookkeeping but endpoint/support-function geometry for the planar curve
\((v_5/c,\Delta_7/c)\).
\end{remark}

\begin{lemma}[First-order value-family generator]

\subsection{Normalization gauge at the first residual step}

The phrase ``value/gauge family'' bundles together two logically distinct
sources of ambiguity:

\begin{enumerate}
\item the \emph{pure value family}, recorded coefficientwise by the spaces
\(
V_{\val}^{(2j+1)}\subset \mathcal J_{2j+1},
\)
and

\item the \emph{normalization gauge}, consisting of even scalar
renormalizations and even jet-basis changes.
\end{enumerate}

For the first residual step it is useful to separate these two.

\begin{definition}[Normalization gauge]
\label{def:normalization-gauge}
Let \(F(z)\) be a matrix-valued odd germ. The normalization gauge acts by
\[
F(z)\longmapsto a(z)\,P(z)\,F(z)\,P(z)^{-1},
\]
where
\[
a(z)=1+c_2 z^2+c_4 z^4+\cdots
\]
is an even scalar germ with \(a(0)=1\), and
\[
P(z)=I+B_2 z^2+B_4 z^4+\cdots
\]
is an even \(2\times 2\) matrix germ with \(P(0)=I\).
\end{definition}

\begin{proposition}[First-residual transformation law]
\label{prop:first-residual-gauge-law}
Let
\[
F(z)=A_3 z^3+A_5 z^5+O(z^7)
\]
be an odd matrix germ, and let
\[
F^{\new}(z):=a(z)\,P(z)\,F(z)\,P(z)^{-1}
\]
with \(a,P\) as in Definition~\ref{def:normalization-gauge}. Then
\[
A_3^{\new}=A_3,
\qquad
A_5^{\new}=A_5+c_2A_3+[B_2,A_3].
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Expand
\[
P(z)^{-1}=I-B_2 z^2+O(z^4).
\]
Then
\[
P(z)F(z)P(z)^{-1}
=
(I+B_2 z^2)\,(A_3 z^3+A_5 z^5)\,(I-B_2 z^2)+O(z^7),
\]
so
\[
P(z)F(z)P(z)^{-1}
=
A_3 z^3+\bigl(A_5+[B_2,A_3]\bigr)z^5+O(z^7).
\]
Multiplying by
\[
a(z)=1+c_2 z^2+O(z^4)
\]
gives
\[
F^{\new}(z)
=
A_3 z^3+\bigl(A_5+c_2A_3+[B_2,A_3]\bigr)z^5+O(z^7),
\]
as claimed.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Residual effect of normalization gauge]
\label{cor:first-residual-gauge-law}
Let \(\ell:=\mathbf C A_3\). Under the normalization gauge,
the quintic coefficient changes by
\[
A_5 \longmapsto A_5 + c_2 A_3 + [B_2,A_3].
\]
Hence its class in the residual quotient modulo the leading line satisfies
\[
[A_5] \longmapsto [A_5+[B_2,A_3}]
\qquad\text{in}\qquad
\mathcal J_5/\ell.
\]
In particular, scalar renormalization is invisible in the first residual
quotient.
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
This is immediate from Proposition~\ref{prop:first-residual-gauge-law}, since
\(c_2A_3\in \ell\).
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Trace invariance under normalization gauge]
\label{cor:trace-invariant-under-normalization-gauge}
If \(A_3\) is diagonal-traceless, then every commutator \([B_2,A_3]\) is
traceless. Consequently
\[
\operatorname{tr}(A_5^{\new})=\operatorname{tr}(A_5)
\]
for every normalization-gauge transform \(F^{\new}\) of \(F\).
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
The scalar renormalization term \(c_2A_3\) is traceless because \(A_3\) is.
The commutator term is traceless because
\[
\operatorname{tr}[B_2,A_3]=0.
\]
Hence Proposition~\ref{prop:first-residual-gauge-law} gives
\[
\operatorname{tr}(A_5^{\new})=\operatorname{tr}(A_5).
\qedhere
\]
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[What remains at quintic order]
\label{rem:quintic-pure-value-bottleneck}
The normalization gauge is therefore harmless for the first residual class.
The only remaining obstruction to using trace as a residual detector is the
pure value quintic slice \(V_{\val}^{(5)}\). To make the residual-trace
argument fully rigorous, it suffices to prove
\[
\operatorname{tr}\bigl(V_{\val}^{(5)}(h)\bigr)=0
\qquad (h\in \mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}),
\]
or more weakly that trace annihilates the image of \(V_{\val}^{(5)}(h)\) in
the residual quotient.
\end{remark}

3. Replace the current overstrong first-residual proposition
Find this exact block

It starts at

\begin{proposition}[Nontrivial first residual class]
\label{prop:first-residual-class-nontrivial}

and ends at its \end{proof}.
Replace the entire block with this

\begin{proposition}[Generic first residual nonvanishing under trace-free quintic value slice]
\label{prop:first-residual-class-generic}
Assume that for every \(h\in \mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\),
\[
\operatorname{tr}\bigl(V_{\val}^{(5)}(h)\bigr)=0.
\]
Then there exists an open dense subset
\[
\mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\subset \mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}
\]
such that
\[
\overline v_1(h)\neq 0
\qquad\text{in }Q/\ell_h
\]
for every \(h\in \mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\).

Equivalently, on \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\) the quintic quotient coefficient
is not collinear with the cubic leading line:
\[
v_1(h)\notin \ell_h.
\]
Hence the one-pair quotient odd germ is not projectively constant on
\(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
By Proposition~\ref{prop:cubic-leading-order}, for \(h\in\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\)
the cubic quotient coefficient
\[
v_0(h)=[J_3^{\pair}(h)]
\]
is nonzero and defines the leading line \(\ell_h\), and this line is
diagonal-traceless.

By Corollary~\ref{cor:trace-invariant-under-normalization-gauge}, trace is
invariant under the normalization gauge at the first residual step. By the
present hypothesis, trace also annihilates the pure value quintic slice
\(V_{\val}^{(5)}(h)\). Therefore trace descends to a well-defined linear
functional
\[
\tau_h:Q/\ell_h\to \mathbf C.
\]

By Lemma~\ref{lem:no-cancellation-quintic-trace},
\[
\tau_h(\overline v_1(h))
=
\operatorname{tr}J_5^{\pair}(h)
\not\equiv 0
\]
as a function of \(h\) on \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\). Since the family is
real-analytic in \(h\), the zero set of
\[
h\longmapsto \tau_h(\overline v_1(h))
\]
is a proper analytic subset of \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\). Its complement
\(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\) is therefore open and dense.

For \(h\in \mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\) we have
\[
\tau_h(\overline v_1(h))\neq 0,
\]
hence
\[
\overline v_1(h)\neq 0
\qquad\text{in }Q/\ell_h.
\]
Thus the one-pair quotient odd germ is not projectively constant on
\(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\).
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Work in progress: trace-free pure value quintic slice]
\label{rem:wip-trace-free-pure-value-quintic}
The remaining local task is to prove
\[
\operatorname{tr}\bigl(V_{\val}^{(5)}(h)\bigr)=0
\qquad (h\in \mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}),
\]
or more weakly that trace annihilates the image of \(V_{\val}^{(5)}(h)\) in
the residual quotient. Once this is established,
Proposition~\ref{prop:first-residual-class-generic}
gives a fully rigorous open-dense nonvanishing theorem for the first residual
class.
\end{remark}

This replacement is necessary because the current proposition still claims pointwise nonvanishing from a proof that only establishes non-identical trace nonvanishing. The conversation explicitly identified that as an overreach.
