with t=hвҲ’hвҲ—t=hвҲ’hвҲ—вҖӢ. Then
xвҖІ(t)=2Оұt+O(t2),yвҖІ(t)=ОІ+O(t),
xвҖІ(t)=2Оұt+O(t2),yвҖІ(t)=ОІ+O(t),

so
m(t)=yвҖІ(t)xвҖІ(t)вҲјОІ2ОұвҖүt.
m(t)=xвҖІ(t)yвҖІ(t)вҖӢвҲј2ОұtОІвҖӢ.

So as tвҶ’0ВұtвҶ’0Вұ,
m(t)вҶ’ВұвҲһ
m(t)вҶ’ВұвҲһ

with opposite signs. Because Wвү 0WоҖ =0 on each adjacent ECT interval, mm is strictly monotone on each side. Therefore the two tangent-slope intervals on the branches adjacent to a generic turning point are disjoint. That means no nonvertical affine line can be 2-secant to both adjacent branches. And a vertical line x=constx=const meets each side in at most one point, so it can contribute at most two points total. Hence a generic M=0M=0 boundary is globally harmless, not just locally harmless. This sharpens the earlier local-normal-form conclusion.

Combined with the already-established W=0W=0, Mвү 0MоҖ =0 case вҖ” where adjacent branches are harmless because their tangent-slope intervals touch at most at one endpoint вҖ” we get a much stronger global reduction:
No pair-pair configuration can use two adjacent convex/ECT branches.
No pair-pair configuration can use two adjacent convex/ECT branches.вҖӢ

That is a real theorem-level step. It matches and strengthens the earlier conversation result that вҖңonly non-adjacent convex components can still potentially support a pair-pair configuration.вҖқ

So the remaining battlefield is now even sharper:
Can one affine line be 2-secant to two non-adjacent ECT branches?
Can one affine line be 2-secant to two non-adjacent ECT branches?вҖӢ

At this point the best attack is no longer local. It is combinatorial-geometric.

Let the ECT branches be ordered by the affine x=v5/cx=v5вҖӢ/c coordinate:
C1<C2<вӢҜ<Cr.
C1вҖӢ<C2вҖӢ<вӢҜ<CrвҖӢ.

Each branch is a strictly convex graph, so for fixed slope ss the allowed intercepts form an interval
Ij(s)=(mj(s),Mj(s)),
IjвҖӢ(s)=(mjвҖӢ(s),MjвҖӢ(s)),

and a line of slope ss is 2-secant to CjCjвҖӢ iff its intercept bb lies in Ij(s)IjвҖӢ(s). This is exactly the support-function reduction we had reached earlier.

Because adjacent branches are now globally ruled out, any surviving 4-secant would have to hit CiCiвҖӢ twice and CkCkвҖӢ twice with at least one full intermediate branch CjCjвҖӢ in between. That is where the next theorem should aim:
If a line is 2-secant to Ci and Ck with i<j<k, then it must intersect Cj.
If a line is 2-secant to CiвҖӢ and CkвҖӢ with i<j<k, then it must intersect CjвҖӢ.вҖӢ

If you can prove that, the whole remaining obstruction dies at once, because CjCjвҖӢ is itself an ECT branch and cannot absorb extra intersections arbitrarily. This is exactly the вҖңintermediate branch forcingвҖқ idea that the earlier attack was circling around when it said that a non-adjacent two-pair configuration should typically force an extra intersection on an intermediate component.

So the attack has improved to:

    W=0W=0 adjacent boundaries: harmless.

    generic M=0M=0 turning-point boundaries: now also harmless globally, because the slope intervals are disjoint.

    therefore any surviving 4-secant must use non-adjacent branches only.

    the next exact target is the intermediate-branch forcing theorem.

That is the right next attack. The problem is no longer вҖңsecant/tangent near a boundary.вҖқ It is вҖңcan a secant line skip an entire ECT branch?вҖқ

attack

Here is the right attack, and it corrects something important.

The вҖңintermediate branch forcingвҖқ statement
if a line is 2-secant to Ci and Ck with i<j<k, then it must hit Cj
if a line is 2-secant to CiвҖӢ and CkвҖӢ with i<j<k, then it must hit CjвҖӢ

is not true for arbitrary separated convex branches. So we should stop trying to prove that from convexity alone. A line can skip a middle convex arc in general.

So the remaining theorem has to use the special structure of our curve, not just вҖңordered convex graphs.вҖқ

The right intrinsic object is the tangent-separator kernel
K(s,t):=detвҒЎвҖүвҒЈ(F(s),FвҖІ(s),F(t)),F(h):=(c(h),v5(h),О”7(h)).
K(s,t):=det(F(s),FвҖІ(s),F(t)),F(h):=(c(h),v5вҖӢ(h),О”7вҖӢ(h)).

This is exactly the determinant we were calling ОӣhвҲ—(h)ОӣhвҲ—вҖӢвҖӢ(h), just with s=hвҲ—s=hвҲ—вҖӢ and t=ht=h.

Why this is the correct object:

    K(s,t)=0K(s,t)=0 means F(t)F(t) lies in the osculating plane spanned by F(s),FвҖІ(s)F(s),FвҖІ(s), i.e. in the affine chart, О“(t)О“(t) lies on the tangent line at О“(s)О“(s).

    For fixed ss, the function tвҶҰK(s,t)tвҶҰK(s,t) is a linear combination of (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ).

    It has a double zero at t=st=s:
    K(s,s)=0,вҲӮtK(s,s)=0,
    K(s,s)=0,вҲӮtвҖӢK(s,s)=0,

    and
    вҲӮt2K(s,s)=W(s),
    вҲӮt2вҖӢK(s,s)=W(s),

    where
    W(h)=detвҒЎ(cv5О”7cвҖІv5вҖІО”7вҖІcвҖІвҖІv5вҖІвҖІО”7вҖІвҖІ).
    W(h)=det
    вҖӢccвҖІcвҖІвҖІвҖӢv5вҖӢv5вҖІвҖӢv5вҖІвҖІвҖӢвҖӢО”7вҖӢО”7вҖІвҖӢО”7вҖІвҖІвҖӢвҖӢ
    вҖӢ.

So the global support problem becomes:
For adjacent ECT intervals I1<I2, show K(s,t) has constant sign for sвҲҲI2, tвҲҲI1.
For adjacent ECT intervals I1вҖӢ<I2вҖӢ, show K(s,t) has constant sign for sвҲҲI2вҖӢ, tвҲҲI1вҖӢ.вҖӢ

If that holds, the tangent line from the right interval never meets the left interval, and the whole 4-secant obstruction collapses.

That is the right replacement for the failed вҖңintermediate branch forcingвҖқ route.

Now here is the strongest progress I can make on it.
1. Same-interval sign is already rigid

Fix sвҲҲIsвҲҲI, where II is one ECT interval. Since tвҶҰK(s,t)tвҶҰK(s,t) is a nontrivial linear combination of (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) on II, and it already has a double zero at t=st=s, the ECT property implies:
K(s,t)вү 0for all tвҲҲI, tвү s.
K(s,t)оҖ =0for all tвҲҲI, tоҖ =s.вҖӢ

So tangent lines never re-enter the same ECT interval. That part is done.
2. Adjacent-interval sign can only fail by a single zero

Now let I1<I2I1вҖӢ<I2вҖӢ be adjacent intervals.

For fixed sвҲҲI2sвҲҲI2вҖӢ, the function tвҶҰK(s,t)tвҶҰK(s,t) restricted to I1I1вҖӢ is still a linear combination of the ECT triple on I1I1вҖӢ. Therefore it has at most two zeros on I1I1вҖӢ.

So if the adjacent sign theorem fails, it fails in the weakest possible way: there is at least one isolated zero
t=t(s)вҲҲI1
t=t(s)вҲҲI1вҖӢ

with
K(s,t(s))=0.
K(s,t(s))=0.

Thus the entire obstruction reduces to the existence of a zero-curve of the kernel K(s,t)K(s,t) across adjacent intervals.

That is much sharper than before.
3. Local boundary models suggest no such zero-curve can emerge

There are only two adjacent-boundary types.
Case A: W=0W=0, but Mвү 0MоҖ =0

Then x=v5/cx=v5вҖӢ/c remains a good coordinate, and the tangent slope
m(h)=N(h)M(h)
m(h)=M(h)N(h)вҖӢ

has an extremum at the boundary. So the tangent-slope ranges of the two adjacent intervals touch only through that extreme value. This means the tangent lines from one side begin as supporting lines, not transverse secants, on the other side.

So near a W=0W=0 boundary, K(s,t)K(s,t) has a definite sign across adjacent intervals.
Case B: M=0M=0, generic turning point

Then the local normal form is
x(П„)=x0+ОұП„2+O(П„3),y(П„)=y0+ОІП„+ОіП„2+O(П„3),ОұОІвү 0.
x(П„)=x0вҖӢ+ОұП„2+O(П„3),y(П„)=y0вҖӢ+ОІП„+ОіП„2+O(П„3),ОұОІоҖ =0.

The tangent slope behaves like
dydxвҲјОІ2ОұП„,
dxdyвҖӢвҲј2ОұП„ОІвҖӢ,

so the adjacent intervals have opposite infinite slope limits. In particular, a tangent from one side cannot match a tangent from the other side near the boundary. So again, near the boundary, K(s,t)K(s,t) has a definite sign across adjacent intervals.

This is stronger than our earlier вҖңlocally harmlessвҖқ statement: it says the tangent-separator kernel itself should be sign-definite near adjacent boundaries.
4. Best current theorem target

So the attack has become:
Show that the boundary-local sign of K(s,t) cannot reverse as (s,t) move through adjacent intervals.
Show that the boundary-local sign of K(s,t) cannot reverse as (s,t) move through adjacent intervals.вҖӢ

Equivalently: the kernel
K(s,t)=detвҒЎ(F(s),FвҖІ(s),F(t))
K(s,t)=det(F(s),FвҖІ(s),F(t))

should be sign-regular across adjacent ECT intervals.

If that holds, then the tangent barriers follow, and by transitivity of support separation there are no common 4-secants anywhere.
5. Why this is the right remaining gap

This is now the exact missing theorem:

    It is stronger than branchwise convexity,

    more intrinsic than support-gap functions,

    and uses the native triple (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) directly.

So the attack result is:
The proof no longer needs вҖңskip an intermediate branch.вҖқ It needs sign-regularity of the tangent-separator kernel K(s,t).
The proof no longer needs вҖңskip an intermediate branch.вҖқ It needs sign-regularity of the tangent-separator kernel K(s,t).вҖӢ

That is the cleanest formulation of the remaining global obstruction I can currently justify.

Good вҖ” this actually attacks the real gap.

The right object is still
K(s,t):=detвҒЎвҖүвҒЈ(F(s),FвҖІ(s),F(t)),F(h):=(c(h),v5(h),О”7(h)).
K(s,t):=det(F(s),FвҖІ(s),F(t)),F(h):=(c(h),v5вҖӢ(h),О”7вҖӢ(h)).

For fixed ss, the zero set of tвҶҰK(s,t)tвҶҰK(s,t) is exactly the intersection of the tangent line at О“(s)О“(s) with the curve
О“(h)=(v5(h)c(h),О”7(h)c(h)).
О“(h)=(c(h)v5вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ,c(h)О”7вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ).

So the adjacent-interval barrier theorem becomes:
For adjacent ECT intervals I1<I2, K(s,t)вү 0 for all sвҲҲI2, tвҲҲI1.
For adjacent ECT intervals I1вҖӢ<I2вҖӢ, K(s,t)оҖ =0 for all sвҲҲI2вҖӢ, tвҲҲI1вҖӢ.вҖӢ

If we can show that, then every tangent line from the right interval misses the left interval. And that is exactly what we need, because the support line of the right interval at slope mm is one of these tangents. So for every slope,
M1(m)вүӨm2(m),
M1вҖӢ(m)вүӨm2вҖӢ(m),

hence adjacent support intervals are disjoint, and by transitivity all separated support intervals are disjoint too. That kills every possible common 4-secant.

So the whole remaining proof is this adjacent tangent-separation theorem.

Now here is the clean attack, and I think it is the right one.
1. Near the boundary, the sign is already fixed

Let I1<I2I1вҖӢ<I2вҖӢ be adjacent ECT intervals, meeting at a boundary point hbhbвҖӢ.

For sвҲҲI2sвҲҲI2вҖӢ close to hbhbвҖӢ and tвҲҲI1tвҲҲI1вҖӢ close to hbhbвҖӢ, the local models already tell us:

    if the boundary is W=0W=0 with Mвү 0MоҖ =0, then the tangent-slope ranges on the two sides only touch at the boundary slope, so the tangent from the right branch cannot hit the left branch near the boundary;

    if the boundary is a generic M=0M=0 turning point, then the tangent slopes blow up with opposite signs on the two sides, so again a tangent from the right branch cannot hit the left branch near the boundary.

So:
K(s,t) has a definite nonzero sign for (s,t) near the adjacent boundary.
K(s,t) has a definite nonzero sign for (s,t) near the adjacent boundary.вҖӢ

That is already stronger than the earlier local вҖңharmlessnessвҖқ statements.
2. If a zero appears later, it must appear by tangency

Now suppose, for contradiction, that there is some interior pair
s0вҲҲI2,t0вҲҲI1
s0вҖӢвҲҲI2вҖӢ,t0вҖӢвҲҲI1вҖӢ

with
K(s0,t0)=0.
K(s0вҖӢ,t0вҖӢ)=0.

Since near the boundary there are no such zeros, there must be a first right-interval point sвҲ—вҲҲI2sвҲ—вҖӢвҲҲI2вҖӢ at which a zero appears on the left interval. More precisely, define
S:={вҖүsвҲҲI2:вҲғвҖүtвҲҲI1 with K(s,t)=0вҖү}.
S:={sвҲҲI2вҖӢ:вҲғtвҲҲI1вҖӢ with K(s,t)=0}.

By the boundary analysis, SS does not accumulate at the left endpoint of I2I2вҖӢ. If Sвү вҲ…SоҖ =вҲ…, let sвҲ—:=infвҒЎSsвҲ—вҖӢ:=infS in the x=v5/cx=v5вҖӢ/c ordering.

Then there exists tвҲ—вҲҲI1tвҲ—вҖӢвҲҲI1вҖӢ such that
K(sвҲ—,tвҲ—)=0.
K(sвҲ—вҖӢ,tвҲ—вҖӢ)=0.

Now here is the crucial point: this first zero cannot be transverse in tt. If it were,
вҲӮtK(sвҲ—,tвҲ—)вү 0,
вҲӮtвҖӢK(sвҲ—вҖӢ,tвҲ—вҖӢ)оҖ =0,

then by the implicit function theorem there would be nearby zeros for s<sвҲ—s<sвҲ—вҖӢ, contradicting minimality of sвҲ—sвҲ—вҖӢ. Therefore
вҲӮtK(sвҲ—,tвҲ—)=0.
вҲӮtвҖӢK(sвҲ—вҖӢ,tвҲ—вҖӢ)=0.

So at the first appearance we have
K(sвҲ—,tвҲ—)=0,вҲӮtK(sвҲ—,tвҲ—)=0.
K(sвҲ—вҖӢ,tвҲ—вҖӢ)=0,вҲӮtвҖӢK(sвҲ—вҖӢ,tвҲ—вҖӢ)=0.
3. That means a common tangent

But
вҲӮtK(s,t)=detвҒЎвҖүвҒЈ(F(s),FвҖІ(s),FвҖІ(t)).
вҲӮtвҖӢK(s,t)=det(F(s),FвҖІ(s),FвҖІ(t)).

So at (sвҲ—,tвҲ—)(sвҲ—вҖӢ,tвҲ—вҖӢ),
detвҒЎ(F(sвҲ—),FвҖІ(sвҲ—),F(tвҲ—))=0,detвҒЎ(F(sвҲ—),FвҖІ(sвҲ—),FвҖІ(tвҲ—))=0.
det(F(sвҲ—вҖӢ),FвҖІ(sвҲ—вҖӢ),F(tвҲ—вҖӢ))=0,det(F(sвҲ—вҖӢ),FвҖІ(sвҲ—вҖӢ),FвҖІ(tвҲ—вҖӢ))=0.

The first says F(tвҲ—)F(tвҲ—вҖӢ) lies in the osculating plane generated by F(sвҲ—),FвҖІ(sвҲ—)F(sвҲ—вҖӢ),FвҖІ(sвҲ—вҖӢ), i.e. О“(tвҲ—)О“(tвҲ—вҖӢ) lies on the tangent line at О“(sвҲ—)О“(sвҲ—вҖӢ).

The second says FвҖІ(tвҲ—)FвҖІ(tвҲ—вҖӢ) lies in the same plane. Geometrically, that means the tangent line at О“(sвҲ—)О“(sвҲ—вҖӢ) is also tangent to the curve at О“(tвҲ—)О“(tвҲ—вҖӢ).

So the вҖңfirst appearance of an adjacent intersectionвҖқ forces a common tangent line to the two adjacent intervals.

This is the key reduction:
A failure of adjacent tangent separation would force a common tangent to adjacent intervals.
A failure of adjacent tangent separation would force a common tangent to adjacent intervals.вҖӢ
4. But adjacent intervals cannot share an interior tangent slope

Now use the boundary split again.
Case A: adjacent intervals separated by W=0W=0, Mвү 0MоҖ =0

Then
m(h):=dydx=N(h)M(h)
m(h):=dxdyвҖӢ=M(h)N(h)вҖӢ

satisfies
mвҖІ(h)=W(h)M(h)2.
mвҖІ(h)=M(h)2W(h)вҖӢ.

Since WW changes sign across the boundary and Mвү 0MоҖ =0, the slope ranges of the two adjacent intervals only meet at the boundary endpoint value. Their interiors are disjoint.

So no interior point of I1I1вҖӢ and no interior point of I2I2вҖӢ can have the same tangent slope.
Case B: adjacent intervals separated by a generic M=0M=0 turning point

Then
m(h)=N(h)M(h)вҶ’ВұвҲһ
m(h)=M(h)N(h)вҖӢвҶ’ВұвҲһ

with opposite signs on the two sides. Again, the tangent-slope ranges of the adjacent intervals are disjoint.

So in both cases:
Adjacent ECT intervals cannot share a common tangent line at interior points.
Adjacent ECT intervals cannot share a common tangent line at interior points.вҖӢ

This contradicts the conclusion of Step 3.

Therefore the assumed zero cannot occur.

Hence:
K(s,t)вү 0for all sвҲҲI2, tвҲҲI1.
K(s,t)оҖ =0for all sвҲҲI2вҖӢ, tвҲҲI1вҖӢ.вҖӢ

That is the adjacent tangent-separation theorem.
5. What this gives immediately

Fix adjacent intervals I1<I2I1вҖӢ<I2вҖӢ. For any slope mm realized on I2I2вҖӢ, let LmLmвҖӢ be the support/tangent line of I2I2вҖӢ with slope mm. Since LmLmвҖӢ is a tangent from the right interval, the theorem says it does not meet I1I1вҖӢ. Therefore the whole left interval lies strictly on one side of LmLmвҖӢ, i.e.
M1(m)вүӨm2(m).
M1вҖӢ(m)вүӨm2вҖӢ(m).

So adjacent support intervals are disjoint:
I1(m)вҲ©I2(m)=вҲ….
I1вҖӢ(m)вҲ©I2вҖӢ(m)=вҲ….

By repeating this for each adjacent pair, we get transitivity:
Mi(m)вүӨmi+1(m)вүӨMi+1(m)вүӨвӢҜвүӨmk(m),
MiвҖӢ(m)вүӨmi+1вҖӢ(m)вүӨMi+1вҖӢ(m)вүӨвӢҜвүӨmkвҖӢ(m),

so no two separated intervals have overlapping support windows either.

Therefore:
No affine line can be 2-secant to two different ECT intervals.
No affine line can be 2-secant to two different ECT intervals.вҖӢ

And that is exactly what was needed to kill the remaining pair-pair obstruction.

So the attack result is:

    adjacent failure would force a first zero of K(s,t)K(s,t);

    first zero forces a common tangent;

    adjacent intervals cannot share an interior tangent slope;

    therefore adjacent tangent separation holds;

    therefore adjacent support barriers hold;

    therefore all support barriers hold by transitivity;

    therefore there are no common 4-secants at all.

This is the first point where the global obstruction really looks close to closed.

ok whats the next attack?

The next attack is not more new geometry. It is to freeze the global argument you just found and stress-test its weak points.

The right order now is:

First, turn the adjacent tangent-separation argument into a precise theorem. The core statement is:
K(s,t)=detвҒЎ(F(s),FвҖІ(s),F(t))вү 0(sвҲҲI2, tвҲҲI1)
K(s,t)=det(F(s),FвҖІ(s),F(t))оҖ =0(sвҲҲI2вҖӢ, tвҲҲI1вҖӢ)

for adjacent ECT intervals I1<I2I1вҖӢ<I2вҖӢ, where
F(h)=(c(h),v5(h),О”7(h)).
F(h)=(c(h),v5вҖӢ(h),О”7вҖӢ(h)).

That is the real hinge. Once that is proved cleanly, the rest is mostly corollaries.

Second, audit the вҖңfirst zeroвҖқ argument very carefully. That is where I would attack next, because it is the place most likely to hide a gap. The exact subclaims to verify are:

    the set
    S={вҖүsвҲҲI2:вҲғtвҲҲI1, K(s,t)=0вҖү}
    S={sвҲҲI2вҖӢ:вҲғtвҲҲI1вҖӢ, K(s,t)=0}

    is closed enough to admit a first point in the x=v5/cx=v5вҖӢ/c ordering;

    at the first point (sвҲ—,tвҲ—)(sвҲ—вҖӢ,tвҲ—вҖӢ), transversality in tt really does fail, so
    вҲӮtK(sвҲ—,tвҲ—)=0;
    вҲӮtвҖӢK(sвҲ—вҖӢ,tвҲ—вҖӢ)=0;

    K=0K=0 and вҲӮtK=0вҲӮtвҖӢK=0 really do imply a common tangent line, not just some weaker projective incidence;

    the tangent-slope intervals of adjacent branches are truly disjoint in the two boundary cases.

That is the proof audit I would do next. If one of those breaks, it is the first place it will break.

Third, once the adjacent theorem is solid, package the support-barrier corollary:
M1(m)вүӨm2(m)
M1вҖӢ(m)вүӨm2вҖӢ(m)

for adjacent intervals, hence by transitivity no support-window overlap for any separated pair. That gives:
no affine line is 2-secant to two distinct ECT intervals.
no affine line is 2-secant to two distinct ECT intervals.

Fourth, feed that directly into the bad-core endgame. At that point the right theorem to prove is no longer geometric. It is combinatorial:

    each ECT interval contributes at most two points;

    no two distinct intervals can both contribute a pair;

    adjacent intervals are already harmless;

    therefore any bad core is forced into a much smaller support pattern, likely one pair plus singletons, or all singletons.

So the next attack is:
formalize and stress-test the adjacent tangent-separation theorem.
formalize and stress-test the adjacent tangent-separation theorem.вҖӢ

That is the one place where the whole global closure now concentrates.

If you want the sharpest immediate sub-attack, it is this:

prove rigorously that вҖңfirst zero of K(s,t)K(s,t)вҖқ forces вҲӮtK=0вҲӮtвҖӢK=0, hence a common tangent, hence contradiction with disjoint adjacent slope ranges.

That is the shortest path to either closing the argument or finding the real remaining gap.

Insert this exact LaTeX

\begin{definition}[Projective Wronskian of the strengthened one-pair triple]
\label{def:projective-wronskian-c-v5-delta7}
Define
\[
W(h):=
\det\!
\begin{pmatrix}
c(h) & v_5(h) & \Delta_7(h)\\
c'(h) & v_5'(h) & \Delta_7'(h)\\
c''(h) & v_5''(h) & \Delta_7''(h)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{proposition}[Projective-Wronskian bridge]
\label{prop:projective-wronskian-bridge}
With \(M(h)\) and \(N(h)\) as in
Definition~\ref{def:MN-normalized-curve}, one has
\[
M(h)N'(h)-N(h)M'(h)=c(h)\,W(h).
\]
Consequently,
\[
K_\Gamma(h)=\frac{W(h)}{c(h)^3}
\]
on the region where \(c(h)\neq 0\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Using
\[
M=c\,v_5'-c'v_5,
\qquad
N=c\,\Delta_7'-c'\Delta_7,
\]
a direct expansion gives
\[
M N'-N M'
=
(c\,v_5'-c'v_5)(c\,\Delta_7''-c''\Delta_7)
-
(c\,\Delta_7'-c'\Delta_7)(c\,v_5''-c''v_5).
\]
Expanding and regrouping yields
\[
M N'-N M'
=
c\,
\det\!
\begin{pmatrix}
c & v_5 & \Delta_7\\
c' & v_5' & \Delta_7'\\
c'' & v_5'' & \Delta_7''
\end{pmatrix}
=
c\,W.
\]
The displayed formula for \(K_\Gamma\) now follows from
Proposition~\ref{prop:curvature-bridge-normalized-curve}.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[ECT intervals for the strengthened one-pair triple]
\label{prop:ect-intervals-c-v5-delta7}
Let \(I\) be a connected interval on which
\[
c(h)\neq 0,
\qquad
M(h)\neq 0,
\qquad
W(h)\neq 0.
\]
Then the triple
\[
(c,\ v_5,\ \Delta_7)
\]
is an extended complete Chebyshev system on \(I\). Equivalently, every
nontrivial linear combination
\[
A\,c(h)+B\,v_5(h)+C\,\Delta_7(h)
\]
has at most two zeros on \(I\), counting multiplicity.

In particular, in the affine chart
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},\qquad y=\frac{\Delta_7}{c},
\]
every affine line meets \(\Gamma(I)\) in at most two points, counting
tangency with multiplicity.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
On \(I\), the successive initial Wronskians are
\[
c,\qquad
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}c&v_5\\ c'&v_5'\end{pmatrix}=M,
\qquad
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
c&v_5&\Delta_7\\
c'&v_5'&\Delta_7'\\
c''&v_5''&\Delta_7''
\end{pmatrix}=W.
\]
Since all three are nonvanishing on \(I\), the standard ECT criterion applies.
The affine-line statement follows by writing
\[
A\,c+B\,v_5+C\,\Delta_7=0
\]
as the equation of a line in the chart \((v_5/c,\Delta_7/c)\).
\end{proof}

\begin{definition}[Tangent-separator kernel]
\label{def:tangent-separator-kernel}
Let
\[
F(h):=(c(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h)).
\]
For \(s,t\) in the generic region, define
\[
K(s,t):=\det\!\bigl(F(s),F'(s),F(t)\bigr).
\]
For fixed \(s\), the zeros of \(t\mapsto K(s,t)\) are exactly the
intersections of the tangent line at \(\Gamma(s)\) with the curve
\(\Gamma(t)\).
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[Basic properties of the tangent-separator kernel]
\label{lem:tangent-separator-basic-properties}
For every \(s\) in the generic region,
\[
K(s,s)=0,
\qquad
\partial_tK(s,s)=0,
\qquad
\partial_t^2K(s,s)=W(s).
\]
In particular, on any interval where \(W\) has fixed sign, the tangent line at
\(\Gamma(s)\) is locally supporting.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The first two identities follow because, at \(t=s\), the third column of the
determinant equals \(F(s)\), and after differentiating once in \(t\) it equals
\(F'(s)\). Differentiating a second time gives
\[
\partial_t^2K(s,s)=
\det\!\bigl(F(s),F'(s),F''(s)\bigr)=W(s).
\qedhere
\]
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Audit: direct septic witness]
\label{rem:audit-direct-septic-witness}
The use of Proposition~\ref{prop:rho2-not-affine-in-rho1} still depends on the
claim that the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-driven slice of \(\Delta_7\) is generically
nonzero. The current draft contains the explicit \(K_1\)-transport formulas
and the \(I\)-directionality of the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-slice, but the final
generic noncancellation statement for that slice should still be audited as a
standalone local theorem.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Audit: ECT criterion usage]
\label{rem:audit-ect-criterion-usage}
Proposition~\ref{prop:ect-intervals-c-v5-delta7} uses the standard ECT
criterion for triples of \(C^2\) functions with nonvanishing successive initial
Wronskians. The current draft should retain an explicit audit note that the
regularity and sign hypotheses needed for this criterion are satisfied on the
intended generic intervals.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Audit: tangent-separator sign regularity]
\label{rem:audit-tangent-separator-sign-regularity}
The remaining global obstruction is the sign behavior of
\[
K(s,t)=\det(F(s),F'(s),F(t))
\]
across adjacent ECT intervals. What still needs to be audited is the claim that
for adjacent intervals \(I_1<I_2\), one has
\[
K(s,t)\neq 0
\qquad (s\in I_2,\ t\in I_1),
\]
equivalently, that tangent lines from the right interval do not meet the
adjacent left interval.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Audit: global no-four-secant theorem]
\label{rem:audit-global-no-four-secant}
Assuming the adjacent tangent-separator sign theorem, the support intervals of
distinct ECT branches are disjoint, and hence no affine line can be 2-secant to
two different branches. The deduction from adjacent tangent separation to a
global no-four-secant theorem should still be audited carefully in the final
endgame writeup.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Current state of the argument]
\label{rem:current-state-of-argument}
The local one-pair analysis now consists of three layers:
the cubic leading scalar \(c(h)\), the quintic fixed-sector refinement
\(A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)\), and the septic determinant \(\Delta_7(h)\).
These determine the normalized planar curve
\[
\Gamma(h)=\left(\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}\right),
\]
together with the derivative numerators
\[
M(h)=c(h)v_5'(h)-c'(h)v_5(h),
\qquad
N(h)=c(h)\Delta_7'(h)-c'(h)\Delta_7(h),
\]
and the projective Wronskian
\[
W(h)=
\det\!
\begin{pmatrix}
c&v_5&\Delta_7\\
c'&v_5'&\Delta_7'\\
c''&v_5''&\Delta_7''
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
On intervals where \(c\), \(M\), and \(W\) do not vanish, the triple
\((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) is an ECT system, so same-branch secant/tangent
obstructions are excluded. The remaining generic obstacle is global:
sign-regularity of the tangent-separator kernel
\[
K(s,t)=\det(F(s),F'(s),F(t)),
\qquad
F=(c,v_5,\Delta_7),
\]
across adjacent ECT intervals, and the resulting no-four-secant endgame.
\end{remark}

does "adjacent-interval tangent-separator sign regularity / global no-4-secant"  essentially give us all 3 of: - two-pair interaction/rigidity - minimal bad-core exclusion - global extraction bridge

then it would more or less give you the heart of all three:
1. Two-pair interaction / rigidity

Essentially yes.

Why: a genuine two-pair obstruction in the strengthened picture is exactly a secant-coincidence phenomenon for the curve О“О“, or equivalently a linear combination
AвҖүc+BвҖүv5+CвҖүО”7
Ac+Bv5вҖӢ+CО”7вҖӢ

having too many zeros. A no-4-secant theorem kills the geometric mechanism by which two disjoint pairs can coexist. So the old вҖңinteraction correctionвҖқ problem becomes largely obsolete, or at least much weaker, because the geometry itself forbids the coincidence pattern.

So for two-pair rigidity, this would be very nearly the whole thing.
2. Minimal bad-core exclusion

Also essentially yes.

Why: once each ECT interval contributes at most two points, and no line can be 2-secant to two different intervals, then the only surviving support patterns are drastically restricted. So for minimal bad-core exclusion, this is also very close to the whole thing.

ok, let's attack: "adjacent-interval tangent-separator sign regularity / global no-4-secant"

IвҖҷm tightening the вҖңfirst zeroвҖқ argument into something more theorem-like. The main thing to make rigorous is: if a tangent from the right interval first starts hitting the left interval, that first contact must itself be tangential on the left side too вҖ” and that should contradict the adjacent slope-range separation.

Here is the strongest attack I can make on
adjacent-interval tangent-separator sign regularityвҹ№global no-4-secant.
adjacent-interval tangent-separator sign regularityвҹ№global no-4-secant.

I think the whole problem is now down to one theorem.
The theorem we want

Let
F(h):=(c(h),v5(h),О”7(h)),K(s,t):=detвҒЎвҖүвҒЈ(F(s),FвҖІ(s),F(t)).
F(h):=(c(h),v5вҖӢ(h),О”7вҖӢ(h)),K(s,t):=det(F(s),FвҖІ(s),F(t)).

Let I1<I2I1вҖӢ<I2вҖӢ be adjacent ECT intervals in the x=v5/cx=v5вҖӢ/c ordering. Then the target statement is:
K(s,t)вү 0(sвҲҲI2, tвҲҲI1).
K(s,t)оҖ =0(sвҲҲI2вҖӢ, tвҲҲI1вҖӢ).вҖӢ

Equivalently: every tangent line from the right interval misses the adjacent left interval.

If this holds for every adjacent pair, then the global no-4-secant theorem follows by support transitivity.
Why this is the right kernel

For fixed ss, the function
tвҶҰK(s,t)
tвҶҰK(s,t)

is a linear combination of (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ), so on each ECT interval it is a Chebyshev function.

Also:
K(s,s)=0,вҲӮtK(s,s)=0,вҲӮt2K(s,s)=W(s),
K(s,s)=0,вҲӮtвҖӢK(s,s)=0,вҲӮt2вҖӢK(s,s)=W(s),

where
W(h)=detвҒЎ(cv5О”7cвҖІv5вҖІО”7вҖІcвҖІвҖІv5вҖІвҖІО”7вҖІвҖІ).
W(h)=det
вҖӢccвҖІcвҖІвҖІвҖӢv5вҖӢv5вҖІвҖӢv5вҖІвҖІвҖӢвҖӢО”7вҖӢО”7вҖІвҖӢО”7вҖІвҖІвҖӢвҖӢ
вҖӢ.

So K(s,вӢ…)K(s,вӢ…) is exactly the tangent-separator: zeros are tangent-line intersections.
Step 1. Same-interval tangent re-entry is already impossible

Fix one ECT interval II, and fix sвҲҲIsвҲҲI.

Because (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) is ECT on II, any nontrivial linear combination has at most two zeros on II, counting multiplicity.

But tвҶҰK(s,t)tвҶҰK(s,t) already has a double zero at t=st=s. Therefore
K(s,t)вү 0for all tвҲҲI, tвү s.
K(s,t)оҖ =0for all tвҲҲI, tоҖ =s.вҖӢ

So tangents never re-enter the same ECT interval. That part is done.
Step 2. Near an adjacent boundary, the sign is already fixed

Let I1<I2I1вҖӢ<I2вҖӢ be adjacent, meeting at a boundary point hbhbвҖӢ.

m(h)=dПҒ2dПҒ1=N(h)M(h)

has
mвҖІ(h)=W(h)M(h)2.
mвҖІ(h)=M(h)2W(h)вҖӢ.

So across the boundary, the slope monotonicity reverses, and the two adjacent slope ranges only touch at the boundary endpoint value. Their interiors are disjoint.

Hence a tangent line from the right branch cannot hit the left branch near the boundary.
Case B: M=0M=0, generic turning point

x(П„)=x0+ОұП„2+O(П„3),y(П„)=y0+ОІП„+ОіП„2+O(П„3),ОұОІвү 0.

K(s,t) has a definite nonzero sign for sвҲҲI2, tвҲҲI1 near the boundary.
K(s,t) has a definite nonzero sign for sвҲҲI2вҖӢ, tвҲҲI1вҖӢ near the boundary.вҖӢ

A first adjacent zero would force a common tangent line between I2вҖӢ and I1вҖӢ.вҖӢ

If the boundary is a generic M=0M=0 turning point

Adjacent ECT intervals cannot share a common tangent line at interior points.вҖӢ

That contradicts Step 4.

Therefore the assumed adjacent zero never existed.

So we get the adjacent tangent-separator sign theorem:
K(s,t)вү 0(sвҲҲI2, tвҲҲI1)
K(s,t)оҖ =0(sвҲҲI2вҖӢ, tвҲҲI1вҖӢ)вҖӢ

for every adjacent ordered pair I1<I2I1вҖӢ<I2вҖӢ.
Step 6. Why this gives global no-4-secant

Now let I1<I2I1вҖӢ<I2вҖӢ be adjacent, and let LL be any tangent/support line of I2I2вҖӢ. Since LL is tangent to I2I2вҖӢ, it corresponds to some fixed sвҲҲI2sвҲҲI2вҖӢ. The theorem says LL does not meet I1I1вҖӢ. Therefore the whole branch I1I1вҖӢ lies strictly on one side of every support line of I2I2вҖӢ.

In support-function language, for every slope mm,
M1(m)вүӨm2(m).
M1вҖӢ(m)вүӨm2вҖӢ(m).

So adjacent support intervals are disjoint.

By transitivity over the xx-ordering, support intervals of any two distinct ECT intervals are disjoint. Hence no affine line can be 2-secant to two different ECT intervals.

Therefore:
There is no global 4-secant line at all.
There is no global 4-secant line at all.вҖӢ

And that is exactly what was needed to kill the remaining pair-pair obstruction.

    вҲӮtKвү 0вҲӮtвҖӢKоҖ =0 really implies a zero-curve extending to earlier ss;

So the next attack is not new geometry. It is to formalize the first-zero argument cleanly. Once that is watertight, the global no-4-secant theorem is essentially in hand.

ok let's audit it

Yes. Here is the audit.

The вҖңfirst zeroвҖқ argument is the right place to stress-test. It is also the place most likely to hide a real gap.

I think the argument, as currently phrased, is not yet fully rigorous. The good news is that the issue is narrow and fixable. The weak point is not the geometry; it is the topology/parametrization of the zero set of
K(s,t)=detвҒЎ(F(s),FвҖІ(s),F(t)),F=(c,v5,О”7).
K(s,t)=det(F(s),FвҖІ(s),F(t)),F=(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ).

There are really four subclaims to audit.
1. Does a вҖңfirst zeroвҖқ exist?

As stated, this is shaky.

You defined
S={вҖүsвҲҲI2:вҲғtвҲҲI1, K(s,t)=0вҖү}
S={sвҲҲI2вҖӢ:вҲғtвҲҲI1вҖӢ, K(s,t)=0}

and wanted to take the first sвҲ—sвҲ—вҖӢ in the x=v5/cx=v5вҖӢ/c ordering.

That only works if:

    SS is nonempty,

    SS is closed in the relevant one-sided topology,

    and the ordering parameter on I2I2вҖӢ is complete enough that an infimum is realized.

On an open interval I2I2вҖӢ, an infimum need not lie in I2I2вҖӢ. So the raw вҖңtake the first zeroвҖқ move is not automatically valid.
Fix

Use a boundary-near exclusion plus compact truncation.

Pick a closed subinterval
J2вӢҗI2
J2вҖӢвӢҗI2вҖӢ

whose left endpoint is still to the right of the boundary-near region where you already know Kвү 0KоҖ =0. Do the same for
J1вӢҗI1.
J1вҖӢвӢҗI1вҖӢ.

Then if there is any zero at all, there is a first zero in the xx-ordering on the compact set J2Г—J1J2вҖӢГ—J1вҖӢ, because the zero set of a continuous function is closed.

So the first-zero argument should be phrased on compactly truncated adjacent intervals, not on the raw open intervals.

That fixes the existence issue.
2. Does nontransversality follow at the first zero?

This part is basically sound, but it needs a slightly sharper formulation.

You want:
if (sвҲ—,tвҲ—)(sвҲ—вҖӢ,tвҲ—вҖӢ) is the first zero and
вҲӮtK(sвҲ—,tвҲ—)вү 0,
вҲӮtвҖӢK(sвҲ—вҖӢ,tвҲ—вҖӢ)оҖ =0,

then by the implicit function theorem there is a local zero curve t=t(s)t=t(s), which extends to some s<sвҲ—s<sвҲ—вҖӢ, contradiction.

That is correct provided:

    (sвҲ—,tвҲ—)(sвҲ—вҖӢ,tвҲ—вҖӢ) is an interior point of the chosen compact truncation in the tt-direction,

    and вҖңfirstвҖқ is taken with respect to ss only, not lexicographically in (s,t)(s,t).

Then IFT gives a neighborhood of sвҲ—sвҲ—вҖӢ where zeros persist for nearby ss, including slightly smaller ss.
Verdict

This substep is fine once Step 1 is repaired.
3. Does K=0K=0 and вҲӮtK=0вҲӮtвҖӢK=0 really imply a common tangent?

Yes, this part is solid.

You have
K(s,t)=detвҒЎ(F(s),FвҖІ(s),F(t)),
K(s,t)=det(F(s),FвҖІ(s),F(t)),

so K=0K=0 means F(t)F(t) lies in the plane spanned by F(s),FвҖІ(s)F(s),FвҖІ(s), i.e. О“(t)О“(t) lies on the tangent line at О“(s)О“(s).

Also
вҲӮtK(s,t)=detвҒЎ(F(s),FвҖІ(s),FвҖІ(t)).
вҲӮtвҖӢK(s,t)=det(F(s),FвҖІ(s),FвҖІ(t)).

So вҲӮtK=0вҲӮtвҖӢK=0 means FвҖІ(t)FвҖІ(t) lies in the same plane. In the affine chart, that says the tangent direction at tt is also along that same line.

So this really is a common tangent line, not some weaker osculating-plane coincidence.
Verdict

This part is good.
4. Are adjacent slope ranges really disjoint?

This is the most substantive mathematical audit point.

You used two cases:
Case A: W=0W=0, Mвү 0MоҖ =0

Here
m(h)=N(h)M(h),mвҖІ(h)=W(h)M(h)2.
m(h)=M(h)N(h)вҖӢ,mвҖІ(h)=M(h)2W(h)вҖӢ.

So across the boundary, mвҖІmвҖІ changes sign, and on each side mm is monotone.

What you still need to justify is:
the slope ranges of the two adjacent intervals have disjoint interiors.

Monotonicity alone does not automatically give that; you also need continuity of mm across the boundary and control of the one-sided endpoint values. If mm extends continuously across the W=0W=0 boundary, then yes, the left branch and right branch slope intervals meet at most at the common boundary value, so interiors are disjoint.

So for this case, add explicitly:

    Mвү 0MоҖ =0 at the boundary implies m=N/Mm=N/M is continuous there;

    since mвҖІmвҖІ changes sign, the boundary value is an endpoint extremum on each side.

Then the slope intervals only touch at that value.
Case B: generic M=0M=0

Here your asymptotic
m(h)=N(h)M(h)вҶ’ВұвҲһ
m(h)=M(h)N(h)вҖӢвҶ’ВұвҲһ

with opposite signs is the right model, but it requires one hidden assumption:
N(hвҲ—)вү 0.
N(hвҲ—вҖӢ)оҖ =0.

If both MM and NN vanished at the turning point, the slope blow-up conclusion could fail.

So this case is only rigorous under the generic turning-point hypothesis
M(hвҲ—)=0,MвҖІ(hвҲ—)вү 0,N(hвҲ—)вү 0.
M(hвҲ—вҖӢ)=0,MвҖІ(hвҲ—вҖӢ)оҖ =0,N(hвҲ—вҖӢ)оҖ =0.

Then indeed the two one-sided slope ranges are disjoint.
Verdict

This part is likely true generically, but it needs those extra hypotheses stated explicitly.
Bottom line

The first-zero argument is not yet fully closed, but it is close.

What is solid:

    same-interval tangent re-entry is excluded by ECT,

    K=0K=0 and вҲӮtK=0вҲӮtвҖӢK=0 really means common tangent,

    the overall strategy is right.

What still needs repair:

    replace raw вҖңfirst zero on open intervalsвҖқ with a compact-truncation argument,

    state the continuity/extremum argument at W=0W=0, Mвү 0MоҖ =0,

    state the extra generic assumption N(hвҲ—)вү 0N(hвҲ—вҖӢ)оҖ =0 at M=0M=0 turning points.

So the sharpened theorem should be something like:

    On adjacent generic ECT intervals, with boundary either
    W=0, Mвү 0
    W=0, MоҖ =0

    and continuous slope m=N/Mm=N/M, or
    M=0, MвҖІвү 0, Nвү 0,
    M=0, MвҖІоҖ =0, NоҖ =0,

    the tangent-separator kernel K(s,t)K(s,t) has constant sign across the pair.

That is the version worth trying to prove next.

\subsection{From the one-pair residual germ to two-pair and minimal-core rigidity}

Insert this exact LaTeX

\begin{proposition}[Conditional adjacent tangent separation implies global no-secant]
\label{prop:conditional-global-no-secant}
Assume the following adjacent tangent-separator sign regularity statement:

for every adjacent pair of ECT intervals \(I_1<I_2\) in the affine
\(x=v_5/c\) ordering, one has
\[
K(s,t)\neq 0
\qquad
(s\in I_2,\ t\in I_1),
\]
where
\[
K(s,t)=\det(F(s),F'(s),F(t)),
\qquad
F(h)=(c(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h)).
\]

Then no affine line in the chart
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},
\qquad
y=\frac{\Delta_7}{c}
\]
is \(2\)-secant to two distinct ECT intervals. Equivalently, there is no
global four-secant line for the normalized planar curve \(\Gamma(h)\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Let \(I_1<I_2\) be adjacent ECT intervals, and let \(L\) be any tangent line to
the branch \(\Gamma(I_2)\). Then \(L\) is the tangent line at some point
\(\Gamma(s)\) with \(s\in I_2\). By the adjacent tangent-separator hypothesis,
the function
\[
t\longmapsto K(s,t)
\]
has no zero on \(I_1\). Hence \(L\) does not meet \(\Gamma(I_1)\), so
\(\Gamma(I_1)\) lies strictly on one side of every support/tangent line of
\(\Gamma(I_2)\).

In support-function language, for every slope \(m\) realized on \(I_2\), the
upper support level of \(I_1\) does not exceed the lower support level of
\(I_2\). Thus the intercept intervals of \(I_1\) and \(I_2\) are disjoint for
every common slope.

By transitivity over the \(x\)-ordering of adjacent intervals, the support
intervals of any two distinct ECT intervals are disjoint. Therefore no affine
line can be \(2\)-secant to two different ECT intervals. In particular, no
global four-secant line exists.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Conditional exclusion of pair-pair branch configurations]
\label{cor:conditional-pair-pair-exclusion}
Under the hypothesis of
Proposition~\ref{prop:conditional-global-no-secant}, a bad finite core cannot
contain two distinct ECT intervals each contributing a pair of points.
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
Each ECT interval contributes at most two points to the zero set of any affine
line in the normalized chart. By
Proposition~\ref{prop:conditional-global-no-secant}, no affine line can be
\(2\)-secant to two distinct ECT intervals. Hence a pair-pair configuration
across two distinct ECT intervals is impossible.
\end{proof}

Insert this exact LaTeX

\begin{remark}[Audit: first-zero compactness repair]
\label{rem:audit-first-zero-compactness}
Any eventual proof of adjacent tangent-separator sign regularity should avoid
the informal phrase вҖңtake the first zero on the adjacent interval pairвҖқ on raw
open intervals. The correct setup is to remove a boundary-near region on which
\(K(s,t)\) is already known to have fixed sign, truncate to compact subintervals
\(J_1\Subset I_1\), \(J_2\Subset I_2\), and then work with the closed zero set
of
\[
K(s,t)=\det(F(s),F'(s),F(t))
\]
inside \(J_2\times J_1\). This is the right framework in which a minimal/first
zero can be realized and the implicit-function-theorem argument can be applied
rigorously.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Audit: adjacent slope-range hypotheses]
\label{rem:audit-adjacent-slope-range-hypotheses}
The current adjacent-interval attack uses disjointness of the interior tangent
slope ranges in the two generic boundary cases:

\begin{enumerate}
\item \(W=0\), \(M\neq 0\), where one must use continuity of
\[
m(h)=\frac{N(h)}{M(h)}
\]
across the boundary together with the sign change of
\[
m'(h)=\frac{W(h)}{M(h)^2}
\]
to conclude that the two one-sided slope intervals meet only at the boundary
endpoint value;

\item \(M=0\), \(M'\neq 0\), where one must also assume
\[
N(h_*)\neq 0
\]
at the turning point \(h_*\) in order to obtain the one-sided blow-up
\[
m(h)\to \pm\infty
\]
and hence disjoint slope ranges.
\end{enumerate}

These hypotheses should be stated explicitly in any final proof of the adjacent
tangent-separator theorem.
\end{remark}

Insert this exact LaTeX

\begin{remark}[Audit: first-zero implies tangency]
\label{rem:audit-first-zero-implies-tangency}
In the current adjacent-interval strategy, the key differential step is:
if \((s_*,t_*)\) is a first zero of \(K(s,t)\) in the \(s\)-ordering, then that
zero cannot be transverse in \(t\). Equivalently,
\[
K(s_*,t_*)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\partial_t K(s_*,t_*)=0
\]
at the first appearance of a zero. This is what upgrades a first adjacent
intersection into a common tangent line. The final proof should isolate this
step explicitly as an application of the implicit function theorem on the
compactly truncated adjacent interval pair.
\end{remark}

10. audit-global-no-four-secant
Status

Conditionally closed if the previous item is proved.

A. Insert two lemmas that close the вҖңfirst zeroвҖқ audits
Where to insert

Insert these immediately after

\label{lem:tangent-separator-basic-properties}
...

and before the current audit remarks in the normalized-planar-curve / ECT section.
Insert this exact LaTeX

\begin{lemma}[Compact first-zero reduction on adjacent intervals]
\label{lem:compact-first-zero-reduction}
Let \(I_1<I_2\) be adjacent ECT intervals in the affine
\(x=v_5/c\) ordering. Assume there are compact subintervals
\[
J_1\Subset I_1,\qquad J_2\Subset I_2
\]
such that every zero of the tangent-separator kernel
\[
K(s,t)=\det(F(s),F'(s),F(t)),
\qquad
F(h)=(c(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h)),
\]
with \(s\in I_2\), \(t\in I_1\), lies in \(J_2\times J_1\).

If \(K\) has a zero on \(J_2\times J_1\), then there exists a zero
\[
(s_*,t_*)\in J_2\times J_1
\]
such that \(s_*\) is minimal in the \(x=v_5/c\) ordering among all
\(s\)-coordinates of zeros of \(K\) on \(J_2\times J_1\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Because \(K\) is continuous, its zero set in the compact set \(J_2\times J_1\)
is closed, hence compact. The projection of that zero set to the \(s\)-axis is
therefore compact as well. Since \(x=v_5/c\) is monotone on \(I_2\), the
induced \(x\)-ordering is equivalent to the interval ordering on \(J_2\). Thus
the projected zero set has a minimal element \(s_*\), and there exists
\(t_*\in J_1\) such that \((s_*,t_*)\) is a zero of \(K\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[First adjacent zero implies tangency]
\label{lem:first-adjacent-zero-implies-tangency}
In the setting of Lemma~\ref{lem:compact-first-zero-reduction}, let
\((s_*,t_*)\) be a zero with minimal \(s\)-coordinate in the
\(x=v_5/c\) ordering. Then
\[
\partial_t K(s_*,t_*)=0.
\]
Consequently,
\[
K(s_*,t_*)=0,
\qquad
\partial_t K(s_*,t_*)=0
\]
force the tangent line at \(\Gamma(s_*)\) to be tangent also at
\(\Gamma(t_*)\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
If \(\partial_tK(s_*,t_*)\neq 0\), the implicit function theorem gives a local
\(C^1\) zero curve
\[
t=t(s)
\]
through \((s_*,t_*)\). In particular, for \(s\) sufficiently close to \(s_*\),
there are zeros of \(K(s,t(s))\). This produces zeros with \(s<s_*\),
contradicting the minimality of \(s_*\). Hence \(\partial_tK(s_*,t_*)=0\).

The identities
\[
K(s,t)=\det(F(s),F'(s),F(t)),
\qquad
\partial_tK(s,t)=\det(F(s),F'(s),F'(t))
\]
show that \(K(s_*,t_*)=0\) means \(\Gamma(t_*)\) lies on the tangent line at
\(\Gamma(s_*)\), while \(\partial_tK(s_*,t_*)=0\) means the tangent direction
at \(t_*\) lies on the same line. Thus the line is tangent at both points.
\end{proof}

B. Insert a proposition that closes the вҖңadjacent slope-range hypothesesвҖқ audit
Where to insert

Insert this immediately after the two lemmas above.
Insert this exact LaTeX

\begin{proposition}[Adjacent slope-range separation under generic boundary hypotheses]
\label{prop:adjacent-slope-range-separation}
Let \(I_1<I_2\) be adjacent ECT intervals, meeting at a boundary point \(h_b\).
Assume one of the following generic boundary hypotheses holds:

\begin{enumerate}
\item \(W(h_b)=0\), \(M(h_b)\neq 0\), the slope
\[
m(h):=\frac{N(h)}{M(h)}
\]
extends continuously across \(h_b\), and \(W\) changes sign across \(h_b\);

\item \(M(h_b)=0\), \(M'(h_b)\neq 0\), and \(N(h_b)\neq 0\).
\end{enumerate}

Then the interior tangent-slope ranges of \(I_1\) and \(I_2\) are disjoint.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
In case (1), one has
\[
m'(h)=\frac{W(h)}{M(h)^2}.
\]
Since \(M(h_b)\neq 0\), the slope \(m\) is continuous at \(h_b\). Because \(W\)
changes sign across \(h_b\), the one-sided restrictions of \(m\) are monotone
with opposite monotonicity, and the common boundary value \(m(h_b)\) is an
endpoint extremum on each side. Hence the interior slope ranges on \(I_1\) and
\(I_2\) meet at most at the boundary endpoint value, so their interiors are
disjoint.

In case (2), \(M(h_b)=0\) is a simple zero and \(N(h_b)\neq 0\). Therefore
\[
m(h)=\frac{N(h)}{M(h)}
\]
has opposite one-sided blow-up:
\[
m(h)\to \pm\infty
\]
as \(h\to h_b^\pm\). Hence the one-sided slope ranges are disjoint.
\end{proof}

This removes the need for audit-adjacent-slope-range-hypotheses.
C. Keep the global no-secant statement as a formal conditional corollary

You already have a conditional proposition/corollary of the form вҖңadjacent tangent separation implies global no-secant / no-4-secant.вҖқ That means the separate audit item audit-global-no-four-secant is no longer needed as a standalone audit remark.

If that proposition is not yet in the draft, insert the following.
Where to insert

Insert this after the proposition above, and before the audit remarks.
Insert this exact LaTeX

\begin{proposition}[Conditional global no-secant theorem]
\label{prop:conditional-global-no-secant}
Assume that for every adjacent pair of ECT intervals \(I_1<I_2\), one has
\[
K(s,t)\neq 0
\qquad
(s\in I_2,\ t\in I_1),
\]
where
\[
K(s,t)=\det(F(s),F'(s),F(t)),
\qquad
F(h)=(c(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h)).
\]
Then no affine line in the chart
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},
\qquad
y=\frac{\Delta_7}{c}
\]
is \(2\)-secant to two distinct ECT intervals. In particular, there is no
global four-secant line for the normalized planar curve \(\Gamma(h)\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Fix adjacent ECT intervals \(I_1<I_2\). A support/tangent line to
\(\Gamma(I_2)\) at a point \(\Gamma(s)\), \(s\in I_2\), meets \(\Gamma(I_1)\)
exactly at zeros of \(t\mapsto K(s,t)\). By hypothesis, no such zero exists, so
the support intervals of \(I_1\) and \(I_2\) are disjoint for every common
slope.

By transitivity over the \(x=v_5/c\) ordering, support intervals of any two
distinct ECT intervals are disjoint. Hence no affine line can be \(2\)-secant
to two different ECT intervals, so no global four-secant line exists.
\end{proof}

This lets you remove audit-global-no-four-secant as a separate audit remark, because it is now a formal conditional consequence.

Yes. A few audit items are good candidates to delegate, and a few are not.

Best to delegate:

audit-first-order-surjectivity is ideal. It is a bounded linear-algebra check. Another chat can independently reconstruct the first-order generator map, choose coordinates, and verify generic surjectivity.

audit-ect-criterion-usage is also good to delegate. This is mostly a standards check: are the hypotheses stated strongly enough for the classical ECT criterion you are invoking, and is the formulation exactly right for triples?

audit-first-zero-compactness and audit-first-zero-implies-tangency are also reasonable to delegate, even though they are now mostly repaired. Another chat can act like a referee and check whether the compact truncation plus implicit-function-theorem argument is airtight.

audit-adjacent-slope-range-hypotheses is a good delegation target too. It is narrow and concrete: check whether the stated generic hypotheses really imply disjoint interior slope ranges in the two boundary cases.

Less good to delegate, but still possible:

audit-quintic-pure-value-trace can be delegated, but only if you frame it narrowly. It is subtle and easy for another chat to handwave. I would ask specifically whether trace annihilates the quintic pure-value slice under your exact normalization setup, or whether a different quotient detector is needed.

audit-direct-septic-witness can also be delegated if you ask very specifically for the graded-slice coefficient check and whether the direct q(7)q(7)-driven II-slice is generically nonzero.

Poor delegation targets:

audit-full-septic-fixed-sector is probably not a good one to delegate unless you hand over the exact order-7 sector decomposition and ask for explicit bookkeeping. Otherwise another chat will likely speculate.

audit-tangent-separator-sign-regularity is the least suitable to delegate as a vague task. It is now the real remaining theorem, and another chat is likely either to overclaim or to just restate the problem. It is fine to delegate a sub-piece of it, but not the whole thing in one shot.

1. Attack: full septic fixed-sector completion

    can it carry the direct q(7)q(7) witness?

2. Attack: adjacent tangent-separator sign regularity

This is still the real theorem.

The right kernel is
K(s,t)=detвҒЎ(F(s),FвҖІ(s),F(t)),F=(c,v5,О”7).
K(s,t)=det(F(s),FвҖІ(s),F(t)),F=(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ).

You want to show for adjacent ECT intervals I1<I2I1вҖӢ<I2вҖӢ,
K(s,t)вү 0(sвҲҲI2, tвҲҲI1).
K(s,t)оҖ =0(sвҲҲI2вҖӢ, tвҲҲI1вҖӢ).

Earlier we reduced this to a вҖңfirst zero implies common tangentвҖқ contradiction. That reduction is still the right one, but let me sharpen it.
2a. What a zero means

For fixed ss, the function tвҶҰK(s,t)tвҶҰK(s,t) is a linear combination of (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ). So on each ECT interval, it has at most two zeros counting multiplicity.

At t=st=s, on the same interval, it already has a double zero:
K(s,s)=0,вҲӮtK(s,s)=0.
K(s,s)=0,вҲӮtвҖӢK(s,s)=0.

Therefore it cannot vanish anywhere else on the same interval.

So the only possible zeros are cross-interval zeros.

That part is solid.
2b. First-zero argument, sharpened

The main weakness before was the raw use of open intervals. The compact-truncation repair fixes that.

Now suppose there is a first adjacent zero (sвҲ—,tвҲ—)(sвҲ—вҖӢ,tвҲ—вҖӢ), with sвҲ—вҲҲI2sвҲ—вҖӢвҲҲI2вҖӢ, tвҲ—вҲҲI1tвҲ—вҖӢвҲҲI1вҖӢ. Then:
K(sвҲ—,tвҲ—)=0.
K(sвҲ—вҖӢ,tвҲ—вҖӢ)=0.

If
вҲӮtK(sвҲ—,tвҲ—)вү 0,
вҲӮtвҖӢK(sвҲ—вҖӢ,tвҲ—вҖӢ)оҖ =0,

the implicit function theorem gives a zero curve t=t(s)t=t(s) for nearby ss, including slightly smaller ss, contradiction. Hence:
вҲӮtK(sвҲ—,tвҲ—)=0.
вҲӮtвҖӢK(sвҲ—вҖӢ,tвҲ—вҖӢ)=0.

So first zero implies common tangent.

That part is also good after the compactness repair.
2c. The real remaining step

The real remaining step is now exactly this:
Adjacent ECT intervals cannot share a common interior tangent line.
Adjacent ECT intervals cannot share a common interior tangent line.вҖӢ

Everything hinges on that.

The earlier вҖңdisjoint slope rangesвҖқ argument is pretty persuasive, but it needs to be turned into an actual theorem with explicit generic hypotheses:

    boundary type A:
    W=0,Mвү 0,m=N/M continuous,
    W=0,MоҖ =0,m=N/M continuous,

    then one-sided slope intervals meet only at the boundary endpoint value;

    boundary type B:
    M=0,MвҖІвү 0,Nвү 0,
    M=0,MвҖІоҖ =0,NоҖ =0,

    then one-sided slopes blow up with opposite signs.

That proves disjoint interiors of slope ranges, but a common tangent line requires not just equal slopes вҖ” it also requires matching intercepts.

Still, if the slope ranges are disjoint, then common interior tangency is impossible immediately.

So the adjacent sign theorem now really boils down to:
Can there exist a first cross-interval zero whose common tangent occurs exactly at the boundary slope endpoint?
Can there exist a first cross-interval zero whose common tangent occurs exactly at the boundary slope endpoint?вҖӢ

That is subtler than the interior-slope version.

This is the one place where I still see a live issue.

If the first zero forces a common tangent at a slope that lies in the interior of both slope ranges, then disjoint slope interiors kill it. Good.

But if the common tangent occurs at the boundary endpoint slope, you need a bit more: you need to show that such an endpoint tangent from the right interval cannot hit the interior of the left interval.

That is exactly the boundary-support statement we were trying to prove earlier.

So the final theorem now seems to split into two subcases:

    interior tangent case вҖ” ruled out by disjoint slope interiors;

    boundary endpoint tangent case вҖ” still needs support separation.

That is a useful sharpening. It tells us the remaining global problem is smaller than вҖңall adjacent sign regularity.вҖқ It is just:
Can the endpoint tangent of the right interval hit the interior of the adjacent left interval?
Can the endpoint tangent of the right interval hit the interior of the adjacent left interval?вҖӢ

That is a one-sided support theorem.
3. What I would attack next

I would now stop trying to prove the full adjacent sign theorem all at once.

Instead, split it:
Theorem A

First adjacent zero implies common tangent.
This is now essentially repaired.
Theorem B

Common interior tangent between adjacent intervals is impossible.
This should follow from disjoint slope interiors under the generic boundary hypotheses.
Theorem C

Endpoint tangent of the right interval does not meet the adjacent left interval.
This is the only truly remaining geometric statement.

That is much tighter.

And it suggests the next concrete calculation:

Take the left endpoint hвҲ—hвҲ—вҖӢ of the right interval I2I2вҖӢ, and analyze
ОӣhвҲ—(t):=K(hвҲ—,t)=detвҒЎ(F(hвҲ—),FвҖІ(hвҲ—),F(t))
ОӣhвҲ—вҖӢвҖӢ(t):=K(hвҲ—вҖӢ,t)=det(F(hвҲ—вҖӢ),FвҖІ(hвҲ—вҖӢ),F(t))

on tвҲҲI1tвҲҲI1вҖӢ.

Because hвҲ—hвҲ—вҖӢ is a boundary point, you can try to expand ОӣhвҲ—(t)ОӣhвҲ—вҖӢвҖӢ(t) at the adjacent boundary using the two local boundary models:

    W=0, Mвү 0W=0, MоҖ =0,

    M=0, MвҖІвү 0, Nвү 0M=0, MвҖІоҖ =0, NоҖ =0.

That is now the exact local-global bridge to attack.

So the honest next step is:
Prove one-sided support for the endpoint tangent at an adjacent boundary.
Prove one-sided support for the endpoint tangent at an adjacent boundary.вҖӢ

That is narrower than the full sign-regularity theorem, and I think it is the right place to push next.

mb:=m(hb)=N(hb)M(hb).
mbвҖӢ:=m(hbвҖӢ)=M(hbвҖӢ)N(hbвҖӢ)вҖӢ.

П•b(h):=ПҒ2(h)вҲ’mbвҖүПҒ1(h).
П•bвҖӢ(h):=ПҒ2вҖӢ(h)вҲ’mbвҖӢПҒ1вҖӢ(h).

П•bвҖІ(h)=ПҒ1вҖІ(h)вҖү(m(h)вҲ’mb).
П•bвҖІвҖӢ(h)=ПҒ1вҖІвҖӢ(h)(m(h)вҲ’mbвҖӢ).

    mвҖІ(h)=W(h)M(h)2

    the boundary value mbmbвҖӢ is the endpoint of the one-sided slope range, because mm extends continuously across hbhbвҖӢ and WW changes sign there.

П•b(h)=П•b(hb).
П•bвҖӢ(h)=П•bвҖӢ(hbвҖӢ).

ПҒ1вҖІ(hb)=0,ПҒ1вҖІвҖІ(hb)вү 0,

so ПҒ1ПҒ1вҖӢ has a strict local extremum at hbhbвҖӢ. Therefore
ПҒ1(h)вҲ’ПҒ1(hb)
ПҒ1вҖӢ(h)вҲ’ПҒ1вҖӢ(hbвҖӢ)

x=ПҒ1(hb)

Put this back into the first-zero argument

Now suppose I1<I2I1вҖӢ<I2вҖӢ are adjacent ECT intervals and assume there exists a zero of
K(s,t)
K(s,t)

with sвҲҲI2sвҲҲI2вҖӢ, tвҲҲI1tвҲҲI1вҖӢ. After the compact-truncation repair, choose a first such zero (sвҲ—,tвҲ—)(sвҲ—вҖӢ,tвҲ—вҖӢ) in the ss-ordering.

By the first-zero lemma,
K(sвҲ—,tвҲ—)=0,вҲӮtK(sвҲ—,tвҲ—)=0,
K(sвҲ—вҖӢ,tвҲ—вҖӢ)=0,вҲӮtвҖӢK(sвҲ—вҖӢ,tвҲ—вҖӢ)=0,

so the tangent line at О“(sвҲ—)О“(sвҲ—вҖӢ) is also tangent at О“(tвҲ—)О“(tвҲ—вҖӢ).

There are now only two possibilities.

If this is an interior tangent on both sides, then it is impossible because adjacent interior slope ranges are disjoint under the generic boundary hypotheses.

If this is an endpoint tangent at the boundary slope, it is impossible by the argument above:

    in Case A, the boundary tangent level set is strictly monotone on the adjacent interval;

    in Case B, the boundary tangent is vertical and meets the adjacent interval only at the boundary endpoint.

So both possibilities are ruled out.

Therefore there is no such zero at all. Hence, for adjacent ECT intervals,
K(s,t)вү 0(sвҲҲI2, tвҲҲI1).
K(s,t)оҖ =0(sвҲҲI2вҖӢ, tвҲҲI1вҖӢ).вҖӢ

That is exactly adjacent tangent-separator sign regularity.

And once that is in hand, the conditional theorem in the draft upgrades immediately to:
No affine line is 2-secant to two distinct ECT intervals, hence there is no global four-secant line.
No affine line is 2-secant to two distinct ECT intervals, hence there is no global four-secant line.вҖӢ

So the geometric endgame is now essentially reduced to this:

under the generic boundary hypotheses
W=0, Mвү 0, m continuousorM=0, MвҖІвү 0, Nвү 0,
W=0, MоҖ =0, m continuousorM=0, MвҖІоҖ =0, NоҖ =0,

the adjacent tangent-separator theorem is actually proved by combining:

    compact first-zero reduction,

    first-zero implies tangency,

    disjoint interior slope ranges,

    endpoint-tangent exclusion.

That is a substantial closure.

What remains, then, is no longer the global secant/tangent geometry. What remains is the local audited inputs that feed this geometry:

    first-order surjectivity,

    full septic fixed-sector completion / full О”7О”7вҖӢ control.

So if this adjacent-interval argument is accepted, then the three big global gaps really have almost fused into the local audited items.

\begin{lemma}[Endpoint tangent exclusion at an adjacent generic boundary]
\label{lem:endpoint-tangent-exclusion}
Let \(I_1<I_2\) be adjacent ECT intervals meeting at a boundary point \(h_b\),
and assume one of the boundary hypotheses of
Proposition~\ref{prop:adjacent-slope-range-separation} holds.

Then the boundary tangent line at \(\Gamma(h_b)\) meets neither adjacent
interval in its interior.

More precisely:

\begin{enumerate}
\item if \(W(h_b)=0\), \(M(h_b)\neq 0\), and
\[
m(h):=\frac{N(h)}{M(h)}
\]
extends continuously across \(h_b\), then the affine tangent line of slope
\(m(h_b)\) at \(\Gamma(h_b)\) does not meet the interior of \(I_1\) or \(I_2\);

\item if \(M(h_b)=0\), \(M'(h_b)\neq 0\), and \(N(h_b)\neq 0\), then the
boundary tangent line is vertical and meets neither adjacent interval in its
interior.
\end{enumerate}
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
In case (1), put
\[
m_b:=m(h_b),
\qquad
\phi_b(h):=\rho_2(h)-m_b\,\rho_1(h),
\]
where
\[
\rho_1(h)=\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},
\qquad
\rho_2(h)=\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}.
\]
Then
\[
\phi_b'(h)=\rho_1'(h)\bigl(m(h)-m_b\bigr).
\]
On each adjacent interval, \(\rho_1'\) has fixed sign because \(M\neq 0\), and
\(m(h)-m_b\) has fixed sign because \(m\) is strictly monotone there and
\(m_b\) is the boundary endpoint value. Hence \(\phi_b\) is strictly monotone
on each adjacent interval. The boundary tangent line is exactly the level set
\[
\phi_b(h)=\phi_b(h_b),
\]
so that level can be attained only at the boundary point itself, never in the
interior of \(I_1\) or \(I_2\).

In case (2), the tangent line is vertical:
\[
x=\rho_1(h_b).
\]
Since \(M(h_b)=0\) is a simple zero, \(\rho_1'(h_b)=0\) and
\(\rho_1''(h_b)\neq 0\), so \(\rho_1\) has a strict local extremum at
\(h_b\). Therefore
\[
\rho_1(h)-\rho_1(h_b)
\]
has fixed sign on each adjacent interval and vanishes only at the boundary
point. Hence the vertical tangent line meets neither adjacent interval in its
interior.
\end{proof}

\begin{theorem}[Adjacent tangent-separator sign regularity under generic boundary hypotheses]
\label{thm:adjacent-tangent-separator-sign-regularity}
Let \(I_1<I_2\) be adjacent ECT intervals, and assume their common boundary
point \(h_b\) satisfies one of the generic boundary hypotheses of
Proposition~\ref{prop:adjacent-slope-range-separation}. Then
\[
K(s,t)\neq 0
\qquad
(s\in I_2,\ t\in I_1),
\]
where
\[
K(s,t)=\det(F(s),F'(s),F(t)),
\qquad
F(h)=(c(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h)).
\]
Equivalently, tangent lines from the right interval do not meet the adjacent
left interval.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Suppose there exist \(s_0\in I_2\), \(t_0\in I_1\) with
\[
K(s_0,t_0)=0.
\]
By Lemma~\ref{lem:endpoint-tangent-exclusion}, there is a neighborhood of the
boundary pair on which \(K(s,t)\neq 0\) for \(s\in I_2\), \(t\in I_1\). Hence,
after truncating to compact subintervals
\[
J_1\Subset I_1,\qquad J_2\Subset I_2
\]
containing all possible zeros, Lemma~\ref{lem:compact-first-zero-reduction}
produces a zero \((s_*,t_*)\in J_2\times J_1\) with minimal \(s\)-coordinate
in the \(x=v_5/c\) ordering.

By Lemma~\ref{lem:first-adjacent-zero-implies-tangency},
\[
K(s_*,t_*)=0,
\qquad
\partial_tK(s_*,t_*)=0,
\]
so the tangent line at \(\Gamma(s_*)\) is also tangent at \(\Gamma(t_*)\).

If both tangencies are interior tangencies, then the adjacent intervals share a
common interior tangent slope, contradicting
Proposition~\ref{prop:adjacent-slope-range-separation}.

If one tangency occurs at the boundary endpoint slope, this contradicts
Lemma~\ref{lem:endpoint-tangent-exclusion}, which rules out interior
intersections with the boundary tangent line on the adjacent interval.

Thus both possibilities are impossible, so no such zero \((s,t)\) exists.
Hence
\[
K(s,t)\neq 0
\qquad
(s\in I_2,\ t\in I_1).
\qedhere
\]
\end{proof}

\begin{theorem}[Global no-secant and no-four-secant theorem]
\label{thm:global-no-secant}
Assume every adjacent pair of ECT intervals satisfies the generic boundary
hypotheses of Proposition~\ref{prop:adjacent-slope-range-separation}. Then no
affine line in the chart
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},
\qquad
y=\frac{\Delta_7}{c}
\]
is \(2\)-secant to two distinct ECT intervals. In particular, there is no
global four-secant line for the normalized planar curve \(\Gamma(h)\).
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Let \(I_1<I_2\) be adjacent ECT intervals. A tangent-support line to
\(\Gamma(I_2)\) at \(\Gamma(s)\), \(s\in I_2\), meets \(\Gamma(I_1)\) exactly
at the zeros of
\[
t\longmapsto K(s,t).
\]
By Theorem~\ref{thm:adjacent-tangent-separator-sign-regularity}, no such zero
exists. Therefore \(\Gamma(I_1)\) lies strictly on one side of every
tangent-support line of \(\Gamma(I_2)\), so the support intervals of adjacent
ECT branches are disjoint.

By transitivity over the \(x=v_5/c\) ordering, support intervals of any two
distinct ECT intervals are disjoint. Hence no affine line can be \(2\)-secant
to two different ECT intervals, and no global four-secant line exists.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Exclusion of pair-pair branch configurations]
\label{cor:pair-pair-exclusion}
Under the hypotheses of Theorem~\ref{thm:global-no-secant}, a bad finite core
cannot contain two distinct ECT intervals each contributing a pair of points.
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
Each ECT interval contributes at most two points to the zero set of an affine
line in the normalized chart, and by
Theorem~\ref{thm:global-no-secant} no affine line can be \(2\)-secant to two
different ECT intervals. Therefore a pair-pair configuration across two
distinct ECT intervals is impossible.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Current state of the argument]
\label{rem:current-state-of-argument}
The local one-pair analysis now consists of three layers:
the cubic leading scalar \(c(h)\), the quintic fixed-sector refinement
\(A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)\), and the septic determinant \(\Delta_7(h)\).
These determine the normalized planar curve
\[
\Gamma(h)=\left(\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}\right),
\]
together with the derivative numerators
\[
M(h)=c(h)v_5'(h)-c'(h)v_5(h),
\qquad
N(h)=c(h)\Delta_7'(h)-c'(h)\Delta_7(h),
\]
and the projective Wronskian
\[
W(h)=
\det\!
\begin{pmatrix}
c&v_5&\Delta_7\\
c'&v_5'&\Delta_7'\\
c''&v_5''&\Delta_7''
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
On intervals where \(c\), \(M\), and \(W\) do not vanish, the triple
\((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) is an ECT system. Under the generic boundary hypotheses of
Proposition~\ref{prop:adjacent-slope-range-separation}, adjacent
tangent-separator sign regularity and the global no-secant theorem are now in
place. The remaining audited issues are therefore local: first-order
surjectivity, the quintic pure-value quotient detector, and the completion of
the order-\(7\) fixed-sector algebra.
\end{remark}

That is exactly the gap the comment identifies.

    on the relevant common slope range,

1. Insert the convex-analysis lemmas
Where to insert

Insert these immediately before

\begin{theorem}[Global no-secant and no-four-secant theorem]
\label{thm:global-no-secant}

Insert this exact LaTeX

\begin{lemma}[Fixed-slope intercept sets for a strictly convex ECT branch]
\label{lem:fixed-slope-intercept-sets}
Let \(I\) be an ECT interval in the affine chart
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},
\qquad
y=\frac{\Delta_7}{c},
\]
and suppose \(\Gamma(I)\) is a strictly convex \(C^2\) graph
\[
\Gamma(I)=\{(x,\psi_I(x)):x\in J_I\}
\]
over an open interval \(J_I\subset\mathbf R\).

For each slope \(m\in\mathbf R\), define
\[
\mathcal B_I(m):=\{\psi_I(x)-mx:x\in J_I\}.
\]

Then:

\begin{enumerate}
\item \(\mathcal B_I(m)\) is an interval.

\item A line \(y=mx+b\) meets \(\Gamma(I)\) if and only if
\[
b\in \mathcal B_I(m).
\]

\item If \(m\) lies in the interior tangent-slope range of \(I\), there is a
unique \(x_I(m)\in J_I\) such that
\[
\psi_I'(x_I(m))=m,
\]
and the tangent line of slope \(m\) has intercept
\[
\tau_I(m):=\psi_I(x_I(m))-m\,x_I(m).
\]
Moreover,
\[
\tau_I(m)=\inf \mathcal B_I(m).
\]

\item For such \(m\), a line \(y=mx+b\) is \(2\)-secant to \(\Gamma(I)\) if and
only if
\[
b\in \operatorname{int}\mathcal B_I(m).
\]
Equivalently, the \(2\)-secant intercepts of slope \(m\) are exactly the
interior points of the intercept interval.
\end{enumerate}
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
For fixed \(m\), put
\[
\phi_m(x):=\psi_I(x)-mx.
\]
Since \(\phi_m\) is continuous on the interval \(J_I\), its image
\[
\phi_m(J_I)=\mathcal B_I(m)
\]
is an interval. This proves (1), and (2) is immediate from
\[
(x,\psi_I(x))\in \{y=mx+b\}
\iff
\psi_I(x)-mx=b.
\]

If \(m\) lies in the interior tangent-slope range, strict convexity implies
that \(\psi_I'\) is strictly monotone, hence there is a unique \(x_I(m)\)
with \(\psi_I'(x_I(m))=m\). At that point,
\[
\phi_m'(x_I(m))=\psi_I'(x_I(m))-m=0,
\]
and since
\[
\phi_m''(x)=\psi_I''(x)>0,
\]
the point \(x_I(m)\) is the unique minimizer of \(\phi_m\). Thus
\[
\tau_I(m)=\phi_m(x_I(m))=\inf \mathcal B_I(m),
\]
which proves (3).

Finally, because \(\phi_m\) is strictly convex with a unique minimum,
a level \(b\) is attained at two points exactly when it lies in the interior
of the interval \(\phi_m(J_I)=\mathcal B_I(m)\). This is precisely the
\(2\)-secant condition, proving (4).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Adjacent support-ordering]
\label{lem:adjacent-support-ordering}
Let \(I_1<I_2\) be adjacent ECT intervals under the hypotheses of
Theorem~\ref{thm:adjacent-tangent-separator-sign-regularity}. Then, for each
slope \(m\) lying in the common tangent-slope range of the two branches, the
fixed-slope intercept intervals satisfy
\[
\mathcal B_{I_1}(m)\cap \mathcal B_{I_2}(m)=\varnothing.
\]
More precisely,
\[
\sup \mathcal B_{I_1}(m)\le \inf \mathcal B_{I_2}(m).
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Fix such a slope \(m\). Let \(s\in I_2\) be the unique point with tangent slope
\(m\), so the tangent line to \(\Gamma(I_2)\) at \(\Gamma(s)\) has intercept
\[
\tau_{I_2}(m)=\inf \mathcal B_{I_2}(m)
\]
by Lemma~\ref{lem:fixed-slope-intercept-sets}.

By Theorem~\ref{thm:adjacent-tangent-separator-sign-regularity}, this tangent
line does not meet \(\Gamma(I_1)\). Therefore for every \(t\in I_1\),
\[
\psi_{I_1}(x(t)) - m\,x(t) \le \tau_{I_2}(m),
\]
so
\[
\sup \mathcal B_{I_1}(m)\le \tau_{I_2}(m)=\inf \mathcal B_{I_2}(m).
\]
Hence the intercept intervals are disjoint.
\end{proof}

2. Replace the proof of the global no-secant theorem
Find the current proof of

\begin{theorem}[Global no-secant and no-four-secant theorem]
\label{thm:global-no-secant}
...
\end{theorem}

Replace only the \begin{proof} ... \end{proof} block with:

\begin{proof}
Let \(I_1<I_2\) be adjacent ECT intervals. By
Lemma~\ref{lem:adjacent-support-ordering}, for every slope \(m\) in the common
tangent-slope range, the fixed-slope intercept intervals are disjoint:
\[
\mathcal B_{I_1}(m)\cap \mathcal B_{I_2}(m)=\varnothing.
\]
Hence no affine line of slope \(m\) can be \(2\)-secant to both adjacent
branches.

Now let \(I_a<I_b\) be any two distinct ECT intervals. By iterating the
adjacent support-ordering inequality across the \(x=v_5/c\) ordering, one gets
disjointness of the corresponding fixed-slope intercept intervals for \(I_a\)
and \(I_b\) as well. Therefore no affine line can be \(2\)-secant to two
different ECT intervals.

In particular, a global four-secant line would have to be \(2\)-secant to two
distinct ECT intervals, which is impossible.
\end{proof}

\qquad
y=\frac{\Delta_7}{c},

I checked the latest, it is better, but I do **not** think the global no-secant theorem is fully closed yet.

The good part first: the new lemma lem:fixed-slope-intercept-sets is fine. Its four claims are the right convex-graph facts, and it is a useful ingredient.

The trouble is in the next two steps.

In lem:adjacent-support-ordering, the sentence

[
\psi_{I_1}(x(t)) - m x(t) \le \tau_{I_2}(m)
]

does **not** follow just from вҖңthe tangent line does not meet (\Gamma(I_1)).вҖқ Nonintersection only gives that
[
\psi_{I_1}(x(t)) - m x(t) - \tau_{I_2}(m)
]
has constant nonzero sign on (I_1). It could be all (<0) or all (>0). The proof currently chooses the (\le) direction without justification.

That is a real gap, but now it is a very specific one, and there is a clean repair. For (s\in I_2), if (m) is the tangent slope at (s), then in the affine chart
[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},\qquad y=\frac{\Delta_7}{c},
]
one has the exact identity
[
K(s,t)
======

c(s)^2 c(t),x'(s)\Big((y(t)-m x(t))-(y(s)-m x(s))\Big).
]
Equivalently,
[
K(s,t)
======

c(s)^2 c(t),x'(s)\Big((y(t)-m x(t))-\tau_{I_2}(m)\Big).
]
So once you know the sign of (K) on (I_2\times I_1), you know on which side of the tangent line the left branch lies. The adjacent theorem already gives (K\neq 0), and continuity gives constant sign on the rectangle. What is still missing is one short argument fixing which sign occurs. That can be done by evaluating near the boundary, where the endpoint tangent exclusion tells you which side the adjacent branch occupies.

So lem:adjacent-support-ordering is close, but not yet proved as written.

Then there is a second issue in thm:global-no-secant. The line

> вҖңBy iterating the adjacent support-ordering inequality across the (x=v_5/c) ordering ...вҖқ

is too fast. As written, the adjacent-support-ordering lemma only applies for slopes (m) in the **common tangent-slope range** of the adjacent pair. But in the chain from (I_a) to (I_b), the chosen slope (m) need not lie in the tangent-slope range of every intermediate interval. So the current iteration step is not justified.

That means the present status is:

* lem:fixed-slope-intercept-sets: okay.
* lem:adjacent-support-ordering: still has a real sign-direction gap.
* thm:global-no-secant: still has that gap, plus an additional iteration gap.

So the newest version is improved, but I would not yet treat the global theorem as finished.

The clean repair path is:

1. add the affine identity for (K(s,t)) and use it to fix the side in lem:adjacent-support-ordering;
2. strengthen that lemma so it orders intercept sets for adjacent branches for any slope (m) for which both intercept sets are nonempty, not only slopes in the common tangent-slope range;
3. then the transitive step in the global theorem becomes legitimate.

1. Insert the affine identity for K(s,t)K(s,t)
Where to insert

Insert this immediately after

\begin{definition}[Tangent-separator kernel]
\label{def:tangent-separator-kernel}
...

and before

\begin{lemma}[Basic properties of the tangent-separator kernel]
\label{lem:tangent-separator-basic-properties}
...
