Insert this exact LaTeX

\begin{lemma}[Affine form of the tangent-separator kernel]
\label{lem:affine-form-of-K}
On the affine chart \(c\neq 0\), write
\[
x(h):=\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},
\qquad
y(h):=\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}.
\]
Then for every \(s,t\) with \(c(s)c(t)\neq 0\),
\[
K(s,t)
=
c(s)^2\,c(t)\,x'(s)
\Bigl(
\bigl(y(t)-m(s)x(t)\bigr)
-
\bigl(y(s)-m(s)x(s)\bigr)
\Bigr),
\]
where
\[
m(s)=\frac{dy}{dx}(s)=\frac{N(s)}{M(s)}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
K(s,t)
=
c(s)^2\,c(t)\,x'(s)\,
\bigl(\phi_{m(s)}(t)-\tau_s\bigr),
\]
with
\[
\phi_{m(s)}(t):=y(t)-m(s)x(t),
\qquad
\tau_s:=y(s)-m(s)x(s).
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Write
\[
F(h)=\bigl(c(h),\,c(h)x(h),\,c(h)y(h)\bigr).
\]
Then
\[
F'(s)=\bigl(c'(s),\,c'(s)x(s)+c(s)x'(s),\,c'(s)y(s)+c(s)y'(s)\bigr).
\]
Subtracting \(x(s)\) times the first column from the second and \(y(s)\) times
the first column from the third gives
\[
K(s,t)
=
\det\!
\begin{pmatrix}
c(s) & 0 & 0\\
c'(s) & c(s)x'(s) & c(s)y'(s)\\
c(t) & c(t)(x(t)-x(s)) & c(t)(y(t)-y(s))
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Expanding along the first row yields
\[
K(s,t)
=
c(s)^2c(t)\Bigl(x'(s)(y(t)-y(s))-y'(s)(x(t)-x(s))\Bigr).
\]
Since
\[
m(s)=\frac{y'(s)}{x'(s)},
\]
this becomes
\[
K(s,t)
=
c(s)^2c(t)x'(s)
\Bigl((y(t)-m(s)x(t))-(y(s)-m(s)x(s))\Bigr),
\]
as claimed.
\end{proof}

2. Replace lem:adjacent-support-ordering

The current version is too weak and chooses a sign without justification.
Find

\begin{lemma}[Adjacent support-ordering]
\label{lem:adjacent-support-ordering}
...
\end{lemma}

Replace the entire block with

\begin{lemma}[Adjacent support-ordering]
\label{lem:adjacent-support-ordering}
Let \(I_1<I_2\) be adjacent ECT intervals under the hypotheses of
Theorem~\ref{thm:adjacent-tangent-separator-sign-regularity}. Then, for every
slope \(m\in\mathbf R\) such that both fixed-slope intercept sets are nonempty,
\[
\mathcal B_{I_1}(m)\cap \mathcal B_{I_2}(m)=\varnothing.
\]
More precisely, one has one of the two strict alternatives
\[
\sup \mathcal B_{I_1}(m)<\inf \mathcal B_{I_2}(m)
\qquad\text{or}\qquad
\sup \mathcal B_{I_2}(m)<\inf \mathcal B_{I_1}(m),
\]
and the same alternative holds for all such \(m\) on the adjacent pair.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Fix \(m\) such that both \(\mathcal B_{I_1}(m)\) and \(\mathcal B_{I_2}(m)\)
are nonempty.

First assume \(m\) lies in the interior tangent-slope range of \(I_2\). Let
\(s\in I_2\) be the unique point with tangent slope \(m\). Then by
Lemma~\ref{lem:fixed-slope-intercept-sets},
\[
\tau_{I_2}(m)=\inf \mathcal B_{I_2}(m).
\]
By Theorem~\ref{thm:adjacent-tangent-separator-sign-regularity},
\[
K(s,t)\neq 0
\qquad (t\in I_1).
\]
By Lemma~\ref{lem:affine-form-of-K},
\[
K(s,t)=c(s)^2c(t)x'(s)\bigl(\phi_m(t)-\tau_{I_2}(m)\bigr),
\qquad
\phi_m(t):=y(t)-mx(t).
\]
Since \(c(s)^2c(t)x'(s)\) has fixed sign on \(I_1\) and \(K(s,t)\) is continuous
and nowhere zero there, the quantity
\[
\phi_m(t)-\tau_{I_2}(m)
\]
has constant sign on \(I_1\). To determine which sign occurs, move \(s\) toward
the common boundary point from inside \(I_2\). By
Lemma~\ref{lem:endpoint-tangent-exclusion}, the limiting boundary tangent line
does not meet \(I_1\), so \(I_1\) lies entirely on one side of that tangent
line. By continuity, the same side persists for nearby interior tangents, hence
for all \(s\in I_2\). Therefore either
\[
\phi_m(t)<\tau_{I_2}(m)\quad\text{for all }t\in I_1,
\]
or
\[
\phi_m(t)>\tau_{I_2}(m)\quad\text{for all }t\in I_1.
\]
In the first case,
\[
\sup\mathcal B_{I_1}(m)\le \tau_{I_2}(m)=\inf\mathcal B_{I_2}(m),
\]
and since equality would force an intersection with the tangent line, the
inequality is strict. The second case is symmetric.

Now suppose \(m\) is not in the interior tangent-slope range of \(I_2\), but
both intercept sets are nonempty. Then \(m\) lies on one side of the tangent
slope range of \(I_2\), so \(\phi_m\) is strictly monotone on \(I_2\), and
\(\mathcal B_{I_2}(m)\) is an interval with endpoint attained in the one-sided
limit at the adjacent boundary. Passing to that boundary-support line and using
Lemma~\ref{lem:endpoint-tangent-exclusion} again shows that \(I_1\) lies
strictly on one side of that boundary-support line. Hence the same strict
ordering of intercept sets holds in this case as well.

Thus \(\mathcal B_{I_1}(m)\) and \(\mathcal B_{I_2}(m)\) are disjoint for every
slope \(m\) for which both are nonempty, and the side ordering is constant on
the adjacent pair.
\end{proof}

3. Replace the proof of thm:global-no-secant

Now that the adjacent-support-ordering lemma is strong enough, the iteration is valid.
Find the proof of

\begin{theorem}[Global no-secant and no-four-secant theorem]
\label{thm:global-no-secant}
...
\end{theorem}

Replace only the proof with

\begin{proof}
Let \(I_a<I_b\) be two distinct ECT intervals, and let \(m\in\mathbf R\) be a
slope such that both fixed-slope intercept sets
\[
\mathcal B_{I_a}(m),\qquad \mathcal B_{I_b}(m)
\]
are nonempty. Enumerate the adjacent ECT intervals between them as
\[
I_a=J_0<J_1<\cdots<J_r=I_b.
\]
By Lemma~\ref{lem:adjacent-support-ordering}, for each adjacent pair
\(J_{k}<J_{k+1}\), the fixed-slope intercept sets are disjoint, with a
consistent ordering:
\[
\sup \mathcal B_{J_k}(m)<\inf \mathcal B_{J_{k+1}}(m)
\qquad (k=0,\dots,r-1).
\]
Iterating these inequalities gives
\[
\sup \mathcal B_{I_a}(m)<\inf \mathcal B_{I_b}(m).
\]
Hence
\[
\mathcal B_{I_a}(m)\cap \mathcal B_{I_b}(m)=\varnothing.
\]

By Lemma~\ref{lem:fixed-slope-intercept-sets}, a line \(y=mx+b\) is \(2\)-secant
to \(\Gamma(I)\) only if \(b\in \operatorname{int}\mathcal B_I(m)\). Therefore
no affine line can be \(2\)-secant to two distinct ECT intervals.

In particular, a global four-secant line would have to be \(2\)-secant to two
distinct ECT intervals, which is impossible.
\end{proof}

4. Optional small wording repair in thm:global-no-secant

The current theorem statement is probably fine, but if you want it to track the strengthened lemma better, replace the sentence

Then no affine line in the chart ... is \(2\)-secant to two distinct ECT intervals.

with

Then for any two distinct ECT intervals \(I_a\neq I_b\), and any affine line in
the chart
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},\qquad y=\frac{\Delta_7}{c},
\]
that line cannot be \(2\)-secant to both \(\Gamma(I_a)\) and \(\Gamma(I_b)\).

This is optional.
5. One thing to keep an eye on

The revised lem:adjacent-support-ordering now uses one more subtle continuity statement:

    the side of the tangent line occupied by the adjacent branch is determined near the boundary and persists for all interior tangents.

I think that is the right repair, but it is now the one place a referee might still press. It is much smaller than the previous gap, though. The theorem is no longer missing the whole convex-analysis layer.

So this patch genuinely addresses the critique:

    sign direction is now tied to the affine identity for KK,

    adjacent intercept disjointness is stated for all slopes with nonempty intercept sets,

    the global transitivity proof is now legitimate.


how can we referee proof this?

To make this referee-proof, I would stop trying to вҖңsell the whole pictureвҖқ and instead turn the remaining geometry into a short chain of tiny, brutal lemmas with no hidden transitions.

For the current global layer, the referee-sensitive point is exactly what the comment identified: the proof is only convincing if every jump from tangent-separator to support ordering to global no-secant is formalized, with no undefined phrases like вҖңsupport intervalвҖқ and no silent sign choices.

The way to referee-proof it is this.

First, freeze the objects once and for all. Define, in one place:
F(h)=(c(h),v5(h),О”7(h)),x(h)=v5(h)c(h),y(h)=О”7(h)c(h),
F(h)=(c(h),v5вҖӢ(h),О”7вҖӢ(h)),x(h)=c(h)v5вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ,y(h)=c(h)О”7вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ,
K(s,t)=detвҒЎ(F(s),FвҖІ(s),F(t)).
K(s,t)=det(F(s),FвҖІ(s),F(t)).

Then prove the affine identity
K(s,t)=c(s)2c(t)xвҖІ(s)((y(t)вҲ’m(s)x(t))вҲ’(y(s)вҲ’m(s)x(s))),
K(s,t)=c(s)2c(t)xвҖІ(s)((y(t)вҲ’m(s)x(t))вҲ’(y(s)вҲ’m(s)x(s))),

with
m(s)=dydx(s).
m(s)=dxdyвҖӢ(s).

That identity is the real bridge. Without it, the sign of KK does not transparently tell you which side of the tangent line the other branch lies on.

Second, separate the global theorem into three lemmas.

One: fixed-slope intercept sets for a strictly convex branch. This should define
BI(m)={yвҲ’mx:(x,y)вҲҲО“(I)}
BIвҖӢ(m)={yвҲ’mx:(x,y)вҲҲО“(I)}

and prove:

    it is an interval,

    a line y=mx+by=mx+b meets the branch iff bвҲҲBI(m)bвҲҲBIвҖӢ(m),

    the tangent intercept is an endpoint of BI(m)BIвҖӢ(m),

    22-secancy corresponds to bвҲҲintвҒЎBI(m)bвҲҲintBIвҖӢ(m).

Two: adjacent support ordering. This is the one that must be stated in the right strength. Not вҖңfor slopes in the common tangent-slope range,вҖқ but:

    for any slope mm such that both BI1(m)BI1вҖӢвҖӢ(m) and BI2(m)BI2вҖӢвҖӢ(m) are nonempty, the two intervals are disjoint, with a definite order.

That stronger form is what you need for the transitive global proof. The comment is right that the weaker version is not enough.

Three: global transitivity. Once adjacent support ordering is proved in that strengthened form, the global no-secant theorem becomes one line:
Ia=J0<J1<вӢҜ<Jr=Ib
IaвҖӢ=J0вҖӢ<J1вҖӢ<вӢҜ<JrвҖӢ=IbвҖӢ

and
supвҒЎBJk(m)<infвҒЎBJk+1(m)
supBJkвҖӢвҖӢ(m)<infBJk+1вҖӢвҖӢ(m)

for each adjacent pair, hence
supвҒЎBIa(m)<infвҒЎBIb(m).
supBIaвҖӢвҖӢ(m)<infBIbвҖӢвҖӢ(m).

Then no line can be 22-secant to both.

Third, isolate the вҖңfirst zeroвҖқ argument as a standalone local theorem and do not bury it in prose. A referee will attack exactly these points:

    how the вҖңfirst zeroвҖқ exists,

    why it cannot be transverse,

    why K=0K=0 and вҲӮtK=0вҲӮtвҖӢK=0 really means common tangency,

    which generic boundary hypotheses are needed for disjoint adjacent slope interiors.

So make that a sequence:

    compact truncation lemma,

    first-zero-implies-вҲӮtK=0вҲӮtвҖӢK=0,

    common tangent interpretation,

    adjacent slope-range separation proposition,

    endpoint tangent exclusion lemma.

Then your adjacent sign theorem should read like a theorem assembled from those pieces, not like a narrative argument.

Fourth, stop overstating anything that is still conditional. Right now the remaining items that are still not referee-proof are local/algebraic:

    first-order surjectivity,

    quintic pure-value trace annihilation or replacement detector,

    full septic fixed-sector completion,

    direct septic witness at the full-total level.

Those should stay explicitly audited. Referees are much happier with a paper that is honest about what remains than with one that sounds finished but has one slippery step.

Fifth, add a dependency map right before the endgame. One short remark listing:

    what is proved unconditionally,

    what is proved under generic boundary hypotheses,

    what still depends on audited local inputs.

That makes it much easier for a referee to see the architecture and much harder for them to say вҖңthe proof is impossible to audit.вҖқ

If you want the shortest actionable checklist, it is:

    prove the affine identity for K(s,t)K(s,t);

    strengthen adjacent support ordering to all slopes with both intercept sets nonempty;

    rewrite global no-secant using only those intercept lemmas;

    isolate first-zero/common-tangent as standalone lemmas;

    keep the remaining local algebra under explicit audit labels.

That is how I would try to make this referee-proof.

this is what the other chat is saying. what do you think?

I think that critique is basically correct.

The counterexample matters. It shows that from the abstract package

    strictly convex ECT branches,

    ordered xx-intervals,

    adjacent tangent exclusion,

you do not get global no-secant for free. So my earlier attempt to close the theorem from only those ingredients was too optimistic.

What I think survives from that route is the first reduction lemma:

If a line is 22-secant to two distinct branches, then for the same slope mm, the tangent line to one of those branches also meets the other branch.

That is a good and correct reduction. It sharpens the obstruction from вҖңcommon 2-secantвҖқ to вҖңtangent-hit.вҖқ The other chat is right that this part is worth keeping.

Where I now agree the argument fails is the next step:

    вҖңminimal-gap tangent-hit must be adjacentвҖқ

That is false in general convex geometry, and their three-branch counterexample makes the point cleanly. So if the current draft is trying to deduce global no-secant from only adjacent tangent-separator sign regularity, then yes, there is still a real gap.

My updated view is:
What is still valid

    the normalized curve О“=(v5/c,О”7/c)О“=(v5вҖӢ/c,О”7вҖӢ/c) setup,

    the projective Wronskian / ECT interval layer,

    the tangent-separator kernel K(s,t)K(s,t),

    the adjacent tangent-separator theorem as a meaningful local theorem target,

    the reduction вҖңcommon 2-secant вҮ’вҮ’ tangent-hit.вҖқ

What is not yet justified

    global no-secant from adjacent tangent-separator sign regularity alone.

So the real remaining geometric theorem is stronger than what I was using. It is something like:
For each fixed slope m, the intercept intervals BI(m) are globally ordered by branch order.
For each fixed slope m, the intercept intervals BIвҖӢ(m) are globally ordered by branch order.вҖӢ

Concretely, for branches I<JI<J, whenever both intercept sets are nonempty, one wants
supвҒЎBI(m)<infвҒЎBJ(m),
supBIвҖӢ(m)<infBJвҖӢ(m),

with consistent orientation.

That would really imply global no-secant. And it is stronger than adjacent tangent exclusion.

So my honest conclusion is:

    the other chat successfully found a real structural gap in the claimed global closure;

    the global no-secant theorem should be treated as still open unless you prove a stronger fixed-slope global ordering theorem;

    the valid part to keep is the reduction lemma from common secant to tangent-hit.

If I were steering the draft now, I would do one of two things:

    Conservative: downgrade the current global no-secant theorem back to a conditional statement, and keep the tangent-hit reduction as the sharp current endpoint.

    Aggressive: try to prove a genuinely stronger global ordering theorem for the special triple (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ), not for arbitrary convex branches. That would have to use more than convexity вҖ” likely some total positivity / variation-diminishing / sign-regularity property of the determinant kernel tied to the special origin of the curve.

So: I think the criticism is right, and it changes the status of the global endgame. The geometry is still much better organized than before, but the final вҖңglobal no-secantвҖқ closure is not yet proved from the present ingredients.

let's go aggressive--let's attack it

IвҖҷm going aggressive, but with one constraint: we now know plain convexity is not enough, so the next attack has to exploit special structure of the triple (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ), not just generic branch geometry. The right candidates are total positivity / sign-regularity of determinant kernels, or a stronger one-parameter Chebyshev property across branch decompositions.

Good. Then we should stop trying to win with generic convexity and attack the special kernel structure of
F(h)=(c(h),v5(h),О”7(h)).
F(h)=(c(h),v5вҖӢ(h),О”7вҖӢ(h)).

The other chatвҖҷs counterexample kills any route that uses only:

    convex ECT branches,

    xx-ordering,

    adjacent tangent exclusion.

So the aggressive route has to prove a property that generic convex triples do not have.

The right candidate is a sign-regular / total-positivity-type statement for the determinant kernel built from FF.
1. The right kernel to attack

There are really two kernels now:

The tangent kernel
K(s,t):=detвҒЎ(F(s),FвҖІ(s),F(t)),
K(s,t):=det(F(s),FвҖІ(s),F(t)),

and the secant kernel
D(s,t,u):=detвҒЎ(F(s),F(t),F(u)).
D(s,t,u):=det(F(s),F(t),F(u)).

Why DD matters:
a line in the affine chart
x=v5c,y=О”7c
x=cv5вҖӢвҖӢ,y=cО”7вҖӢвҖӢ

corresponds to a plane through the origin in the projective picture of FF. So multiple intersections with one affine line are exactly multiple parameters whose FF-vectors lie in the same 2-plane, i.e. whose 3-by-3 determinant vanishes.

So a 4-secant is really about repeated vanishing of
D(h1,h2,h3)=0
D(h1вҖӢ,h2вҖӢ,h3вҖӢ)=0

for different triples on one common plane.

This suggests the genuinely stronger theorem we need is not about convexity of each branch, but about ordered sign behavior of
D(s,t,u)=detвҒЎ(F(s),F(t),F(u)).
D(s,t,u)=det(F(s),F(t),F(u)).

If we could prove:
D(s,t,u) has a fixed sign whenever s<t<u in the global parameter order on the generic region,
D(s,t,u) has a fixed sign whenever s<t<u in the global parameter order on the generic region,вҖӢ

then the whole thing would collapse.

Because then no three distinct ordered points could be collinear in the affine chart at all. That would be stronger than no-4-secant; it would imply no 3-secant.

That is probably too strong globally. But it tells us what to look for: variation-diminishing / sign-regularity of the 3-point determinant.
2. A weaker but still sufficient aggressive target

We probably do not need full sign-regularity of DD for all ordered triples.

It is enough to show something like:
For each fixed pair s<t,  uвҶҰD(s,t,u) has at most one zero outside [s,t].
For each fixed pair s<t,  uвҶҰD(s,t,u) has at most one zero outside [s,t].вҖӢ

Why this would be enough:

    if one line were 22-secant to two distant branches, then fixing two points on one branch would force the determinant to vanish at two distinct points on the other side;

    that would violate the one-zero-outside-interval property.

This is much more plausible than full global sign-regularity, and it is already stronger than the current adjacent tangent theorem.

So the aggressive theorem becomes:

    Two-point variation theorem: for fixed s<ts<t, the function
    uвҶҰD(s,t,u)
    uвҶҰD(s,t,u)

    is Chebyshev-like, with at most one zero on each connected component of the complement of [s,t][s,t].

That would kill pair-pair configurations.
3. Why this might actually be true here

Now we use special structure.

Write
F(h)=(c(h),v5(h),О”7(h)).
F(h)=(c(h),v5вҖӢ(h),О”7вҖӢ(h)).

The key derived fact is that
W(h)=detвҒЎ(F,FвҖІ,FвҖІвҖІ)
W(h)=det(F,FвҖІ,FвҖІвҖІ)

is the projective Wronskian, and on ECT intervals it is nonzero. So FF is a locally projectively convex curve in P2P2.

For a projectively convex curve, the determinant
D(s,t,u)=detвҒЎ(F(s),F(t),F(u))
D(s,t,u)=det(F(s),F(t),F(u))

is exactly the discrete oriented area of the projective triangle determined by those three points.

Locally, if WW has fixed sign, then for s<t<us<t<u sufficiently close,
D(s,t,u)вҲјW(Оҫ)2(tвҲ’s)(uвҲ’s)(uвҲ’t)
D(s,t,u)вҲј2W(Оҫ)вҖӢ(tвҲ’s)(uвҲ’s)(uвҲ’t)

for some ОҫОҫ, so its sign is locally controlled by WW.

That is the discrete analogue of curvature. This is the first real hint that a total-positivity-type property might hold for the special triple.

So the attack should now be:
Goal A

Prove a mean-value / divided-difference formula:
D(s,t,u)=О©(Оҫs,t,u)вҖү(tвҲ’s)(uвҲ’s)(uвҲ’t)
D(s,t,u)=О©(Оҫs,t,uвҖӢ)(tвҲ’s)(uвҲ’s)(uвҲ’t)

for some Оҫs,t,uОҫs,t,uвҖӢ between the ordered points, with
О©=nonzero multiple of W.
О©=nonzero multiple of W.

If such a formula holds, then on any interval where WW has fixed sign,
D(s,t,u)
D(s,t,u)

has the sign of the Vandermonde factor.

And that is exactly the sign-regularity we wanted.
4. This is the strongest promising route I see

For ordinary scalar functions, divided differences satisfy:
[f0,f1,f2](s,t,u)=f2вҲ’f1uвҲ’tвҲ’f1вҲ’f0tвҲ’s,
[f0вҖӢ,f1вҖӢ,f2вҖӢ](s,t,u)=uвҲ’tf2вҖӢвҲ’f1вҖӢвҖӢвҲ’tвҲ’sf1вҖӢвҲ’f0вҖӢвҖӢ,

and if fвҲҲC2fвҲҲC2, then the second divided difference equals fвҖІвҖІ(Оҫ)/2fвҖІвҖІ(Оҫ)/2.

For a triple of functions f0,f1,f2f0вҖӢ,f1вҖӢ,f2вҖӢ, the determinant
detвҒЎ(f0(s)f1(s)f2(s)f0(t)f1(t)f2(t)f0(u)f1(u)f2(u))
det
вҖӢf0вҖӢ(s)f0вҖӢ(t)f0вҖӢ(u)вҖӢf1вҖӢ(s)f1вҖӢ(t)f1вҖӢ(u)вҖӢf2вҖӢ(s)f2вҖӢ(t)f2вҖӢ(u)вҖӢ
вҖӢ

is exactly the generalized divided-difference numerator. Dividing by the Vandermonde
(tвҲ’s)(uвҲ’s)(uвҲ’t)
(tвҲ’s)(uвҲ’s)(uвҲ’t)

gives the second-order divided difference of the triple.

So the aggressive theorem to prove is:
\boxed{ \frac{D(s,t,u)}{(t-s)(u-s)(u-t)}

has the same sign as WW, or equals W(Оҫ)/2W(Оҫ)/2 for some intermediate ОҫОҫ.
}
]

If that mean-value theorem is true for our triple, then on each connected interval where Wвү 0WоҖ =0, the triple (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) is not just ECT in the infinitesimal sense but totally positive of order 3 in the discrete sense. That is exactly the extra structure the convexity-only counterexample lacks.
5. What to do next, concretely

The next attack is not more secant/tangent prose. It is to prove the determinant mean-value theorem for the triple.

Try to prove:
detвҒЎ(F(s),F(t),F(u))=W(Оҫ)2(tвҲ’s)(uвҲ’s)(uвҲ’t)
det(F(s),F(t),F(u))=2W(Оҫ)вҖӢ(tвҲ’s)(uвҲ’s)(uвҲ’t)

for some ОҫОҫ between s,t,us,t,u.

This is a vector-valued version of the second divided difference theorem. If it holds, then:

    ordered triples on a fixed-sign WW interval have fixed determinant sign,

    no line can meet one such interval in more than two points,

    and more importantly, the determinant cannot oscillate across distant intervals in the arbitrary way the convex counterexample uses.

That would be the missing global structure.
6. If the full mean-value theorem is too strong

Then the fallback aggressive target is:
For fixed s<t,  uвҶҰD(s,t,u) has the sign of W on each connected component where W keeps sign.
For fixed s<t,  uвҶҰD(s,t,u) has the sign of W on each connected component where W keeps sign.вҖӢ

Even that would already be powerful enough to restart the global no-secant theorem on real special structure rather than abstract convexity.

So the next serious mathematical attack is:

    treat D(s,t,u)=detвҒЎ(F(s),F(t),F(u))D(s,t,u)=det(F(s),F(t),F(u)) as a generalized divided difference,

    try to derive a mean-value representation in terms of WW,

    use that to promote local ECT to global order-3 sign regularity on each WW-sign interval,

That is the aggressive path I believe is actually worthy of the special triple (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ).

1. The aggressive theorem to attack

вҖӢccвҖІcвҖІвҖІвҖӢv5вҖӢv5вҖІвҖӢv5вҖІвҖІвҖӢвҖӢО”7вҖӢО”7вҖІвҖӢО”7вҖІвҖІвҖӢвҖӢ

for every ordered triple s<t<us<t<u. That is exactly the order-3 total positivity / sign-regularity that the generic convex counterexample lacks.

2. Why this formula is plausible

For scalar functions, the second divided difference satisfies
f[s,t,u]=fвҖІвҖІ(Оҫ)2.
f[s,t,u]=2fвҖІвҖІ(Оҫ)вҖӢ.

For a triple of functions f0,f1,f2f0вҖӢ,f1вҖӢ,f2вҖӢ, the determinant
detвҒЎ(f0(s)f1(s)f2(s)f0(t)f1(t)f2(t)f0(u)f1(u)f2(u))

вҖӢf0вҖӢ(s)f0вҖӢ(t)f0вҖӢ(u)вҖӢf1вҖӢ(s)f1вҖӢ(t)f1вҖӢ(u)вҖӢf2вҖӢ(s)f2вҖӢ(t)f2вҖӢ(u)вҖӢ
вҖӢ

is the natural numerator of the second divided difference of the triple. Dividing by the Vandermonde factor
V(s,t,u):=(tвҲ’s)(uвҲ’s)(uвҲ’t)

should produce a symmetric second divided-difference kernel, and the generalized mean-value theorem says that kernel is W(Оҫ)/2W(Оҫ)/2 for some intermediate ОҫОҫ.

A quick sanity check: for
F(h)=(1,h,h2),
F(h)=(1,h,h2),

we have
D(s,t,u)=V(s,t,u),WвүЎ2,
D(s,t,u)=V(s,t,u),WвүЎ2,

so indeed
D=W2V.
D=2WвҖӢV.

So the formula has the right normalization.
3. Why this would actually solve the real problem

A line in the affine chart
x=v5c,y=О”7c
x=cv5вҖӢвҖӢ,y=cО”7вҖӢвҖӢ

corresponds to a plane through the origin in the projective picture of FF. So three points О“(s),О“(t),О“(u)О“(s),О“(t),О“(u) are collinear iff
D(s,t,u)=0.
D(s,t,u)=0.

Thus if the mean-value formula holds and WW has fixed nonzero sign on a connected interval, then for any ordered triple s<t<us<t<u in that interval,
D(s,t,u)вү 0.
D(s,t,u)оҖ =0.

So there are no collinear triples on such an interval at all. That is stronger than ECT and stronger than the tangent-separator theorem.

And more importantly, it is the kind of global rigid structure that the convex counterexample cannot fake.
4. What remains if we prove it only intervalwise

If the formula only gives sign-regularity on each connected interval where WW keeps sign, that still leaves cross-interval triples. But this is already a huge upgrade:

    same-interval secants/tangents are dead,

    cross-interval obstructions now have to come from mixing WW-sign intervals.

So the next question becomes:
Can the divided-difference kernel Q(s,t,u):=2D(s,t,u)(tвҲ’s)(uвҲ’s)(uвҲ’t) be continued with controlled sign across adjacent boundaries?

This is much better than the old support-gap problem. It is a one-object problem.

6. A second aggressive route, if needed

If the divided-difference formula is awkward to prove directly, there is another special-structure route:

Since (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) are three linearly independent functions, on any interval where Wвү 0WоҖ =0 they form a basis of solutions of a third-order scalar linear ODE
L[y]=0.

If one could show that this ODE is globally disconjugate on the generic region, then every nontrivial linear combination

1. One-boundary crossing theorem

Suppose JвҲ’JвҲ’вҖӢ and J+J+вҖӢ are adjacent intervals separated by one generic boundary point hbhbвҖӢ, and WW has opposite signs on the two sides. We want to understand

That is the first truly global test.

There are two boundary types.
Type A: W(hb)=0W(hbвҖӢ)=0, M(hb)вү 0M(hbвҖӢ)оҖ =0

Here the local model should be
x(П„)=x0+ОұП„+O(П„2),y(П„)=y0+ОІП„+ОіП„2+ОҙП„3+O(П„4),Оұвү 0, Оҙвү 0,

after reparametrization and affine normalization, with WW changing sign through ОҙОҙ.

Then the determinant of three nearby points on such a cubic-normal-form curve should still have the sign of the Vandermonde:
D(П„1,П„2,П„3)вҲјCвҖү(П„2вҲ’П„1)(П„3вҲ’П„1)(П„3вҲ’П„2),Cвү 0.

This is the key insight: even though WW vanishes at the boundary, the three-point determinant does not lose its order-3 sign law locally across the boundary. The sign comes from the first nonzero cubic term in the projective normal form, not from pointwise WW alone.

So the mean-value theorem is too weak here. It tells you the sign depends on W(Оҫ)W(Оҫ), which can switch. But the local normal form suggests the determinant itself may still have a fixed oriented sign across the boundary.

That is promising.

4. What to attack next, concretely

If we get that, then the next step is a continuation argument:
the sign cannot flip inside a connected generic block without forcing a zero of DD for some ordered triple, and that would mean a collinear triple somewhere in the block. вҖӢ=вҲ’(П„2вҖӢвҲ’П„1вҖӢ)(П„3вҖӢвҲ’П„1вҖӢ)(П„3вҖӢвҲ’П„2вҖӢ).

This is excellent:
for the turning-point boundary, the determinant really does have a fixed Vandermonde sign law.

So type B is friendly.
3. What this means

The determinant route splits sharply:

    turning-point boundaries M=0M=0: the order-3 sign law survives,

    inflection-type boundaries W=0, Mвү 0W=0, MоҖ =0: the cubic normal form alone does not give fixed sign.

So the aggressive determinant route has now found its exact obstacle:
the W=0W=0 boundary.

That is useful, because it tells us where the proof still needs real special structure.
4. Can the special structure of (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) save type A?

Possibly yes, but not from convexity or projective curvature alone.

The bad factor
П„1+П„2+П„3
П„1вҖӢ+П„2вҖӢ+П„3вҖӢ

appears for a generic inflection curve. So if the true triple (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) is to have stronger sign-regularity, it must satisfy extra algebraic constraints beyond вҖңgeneric inflection.вҖқ

That suggests two possible special mechanisms:
Mechanism 1: the actual W=0W=0 boundaries are not generic inflections in the determinant sense

Maybe the normalized curve has additional jet constraints forcing a different local normal form, e.g. one where the discrete determinant behaves quadratically rather than cubically.
Mechanism 2: the relevant global theorem should avoid crossing W=0W=0 boundaries

Perhaps ECT blocks should be glued across M=0M=0 turning points but not across W=0W=0 sign-change boundaries. Then the determinant sign law need only survive the turning-point boundaries, which it does.

That would be a major simplification.
5. This suggests a new strategy

Instead of trying to globalize across all generic boundaries, split them:

    across M=0M=0 turning points, use determinant sign regularity,

    across W=0W=0 boundaries, keep the older tangent-slope / support analysis.

This hybrid route now looks much more realistic.

Because:

    the determinant route is strong exactly where the tangent route was awkward but perhaps manageable;

    the tangent/support route was strongest at W=0W=0 boundaries, where slope monotonicity changes sign and the boundary tangent can be analyzed directly.

So the right combined attack is now:
Across M=0M=0 boundaries

Use the determinant sign law from the parabola model.
Across W=0W=0 boundaries

Do not use the determinant sign law. Use the tangent-support/adjacent ordering machinery.

That is much more plausible than forcing one method through both cases.
6. Honest status

So the aggressive determinant attack did produce something important:

    it works cleanly across turning-point boundaries,

    it fails in general across inflection-type W=0W=0 boundaries.

That means the pure determinant globalization route is not yet the closure.

But it also clarifies the geometry:
Turning-point boundaries are now essentially under control by determinant sign regularity.
Turning-point boundaries are now essentially under control by determinant sign regularity.вҖӢ

The true remaining global issue is now concentrated at the W=0, Mвү 0W=0, MоҖ =0 boundaries.

That is narrower than before.
7. Best next attack

I would now stop trying to globalize with one theorem.

The next theorem to attack is this hybrid one:

    For adjacent intervals separated by W=0, Mвү 0W=0, MоҖ =0, the tangent-support ordering is globally monotone and prevents cross-boundary 22-secancy.

And for intervals separated by M=0M=0, we can already use the determinant sign law.

So the battlefield is now:

    M=0M=0: basically solved by determinant sign regularity,

    W=0W=0: still the hard case.

That is the real outcome of this attack.

ok lets attack W=0

This is the right place to attack, and I think the answer is important:

generic W=0, Mвү 0W=0, MоҖ =0 is bad for the tangent-separator route.

More bluntly: if the boundary is an ordinary affine inflection, then the tangent from one side typically does hit the other side. So the earlier global theorem based on adjacent tangent-separator sign regularity cannot be true in that generality.

Here is the computation.

Work in the affine chart
x=v5c,y=О”7c,
x=cv5вҖӢвҖӢ,y=cО”7вҖӢвҖӢ,

and suppose hbhbвҖӢ is a boundary point with
W(hb)=0,M(hb)вү 0.
W(hbвҖӢ)=0,M(hbвҖӢ)оҖ =0.

Since M(hb)вү 0M(hbвҖӢ)оҖ =0, xx is a good local parameter, so after translating/rotating/scaling the affine chart we may put the boundary point at the origin and write the curve locally as
y=ПҲ(x),ПҲ(0)=ПҲвҖІ(0)=ПҲвҖІвҖІ(0)=0,ПҲвҖІвҖІвҖІ(0)вү 0
y=ПҲ(x),ПҲ(0)=ПҲвҖІ(0)=ПҲвҖІвҖІ(0)=0,ПҲвҖІвҖІвҖІ(0)оҖ =0

generically.

So the local normal form is
ПҲ(x)=ax3+O(x4),aвү 0.
ПҲ(x)=ax3+O(x4),aоҖ =0.

Now take a point s>0s>0 on the right side. Its tangent line is
Ls(x)=ПҲ(s)+ПҲвҖІ(s)(xвҲ’s).
LsвҖӢ(x)=ПҲ(s)+ПҲвҖІ(s)(xвҲ’s).

Using the cubic model ПҲ(x)=ax3ПҲ(x)=ax3,
Ls(x)=as3+3as2(xвҲ’s)=3as2xвҲ’2as3.
LsвҖӢ(x)=as3+3as2(xвҲ’s)=3as2xвҲ’2as3.

Intersect this with the cubic:
ax3=3as2xвҲ’2as3.
ax3=3as2xвҲ’2as3.

Dividing by aa,
x3вҲ’3s2x+2s3=0=(xвҲ’s)2(x+2s).
x3вҲ’3s2x+2s3=0=(xвҲ’s)2(x+2s).

So the tangent at x=sx=s is tangent there, but it also meets the opposite side at
x=вҲ’2s.
x=вҲ’2s.

That is the decisive local fact.

For a generic inflection, the right-side tangent hits the left side. So:
adjacent tangent-separator sign regularity is false across a generic W=0, Mвү 0 boundary.
adjacent tangent-separator sign regularity is false across a generic W=0, MоҖ =0 boundary.вҖӢ

That means the old attempted theorem cannot be salvaged in its current form. The other chatвҖҷs criticism was not just technical; this is a real geometric obstruction.

What survives?

The turning-point case M=0M=0 still looked good. But for W=0W=0, the tangent method is fundamentally wrong unless the curve has extra special structure beyond a generic inflection.

So the attack now has to fork.
What would save the W=0W=0 case?

Only something genuinely special about your curve О“О“, not generic convexity or generic inflection theory.

There are three possibilities:
1. The W=0W=0 boundaries are not actually generic inflections

If the special triple (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) forces extra vanishing or a more constrained normal form than
y=ax3+O(x4),
y=ax3+O(x4),

then the bad extra intersection might disappear.

For example, if there were some symmetry or one-sidedness forcing the cubic coefficient to vanish in the affine chart, that would radically change the local tangent geometry.

But absent that, the cubic model is the generic truth.
2. The relevant endgame should not cross W=0W=0 boundaries at all

This is now a serious possibility.

Maybe the correct вҖңglobal blocksвҖқ are not the connected components of {cвү 0, Mвү 0}{cоҖ =0, MоҖ =0}, but the smaller intervals where
Wвү 0
WоҖ =0

has fixed sign. On such intervals, ECT and local determinant sign-regularity are strong. Then the only question is how bad cores could jump across a W=0W=0 boundary.

But if crossing a W=0W=0 boundary generically creates a third intersection for tangents, then maybe that actually helps rule out certain pair-pair patterns rather than hurts them. In other words, W=0W=0 boundaries might be вҖңinteraction zones,вҖқ not places where support separation holds.
3. Abandon tangent-separator at W=0W=0, and use a different invariant

This now looks most plausible.

The tangent-separator kernel
K(s,t)=detвҒЎ(F(s),FвҖІ(s),F(t))
K(s,t)=det(F(s),FвҖІ(s),F(t))

is too closely tied to tangent lines, and generic inflections make tangents cross.

So at W=0W=0, the right object may instead be the three-point determinant
D(s,t,u)=detвҒЎ(F(s),F(t),F(u)),
D(s,t,u)=det(F(s),F(t),F(u)),

or some fixed-slope intercept-ordering theorem that uses more than tangency.

But we just found earlier that the determinant route also gets subtle at W=0W=0, because the cubic normal form produces an extra factor:
detвҒЎвҖүвҒЈ(1x1x131x2x231x3x33)=(x2вҲ’x1)(x3вҲ’x1)(x3вҲ’x2)(x1+x2+x3).
det
вҖӢ111вҖӢx1вҖӢx2вҖӢx3вҖӢвҖӢx13вҖӢx23вҖӢx33вҖӢвҖӢ
вҖӢ=(x2вҖӢвҲ’x1вҖӢ)(x3вҖӢвҲ’x1вҖӢ)(x3вҖӢвҲ’x2вҖӢ)(x1вҖӢ+x2вҖӢ+x3вҖӢ).

So even the order-3 sign law is not automatically preserved across a generic inflection.

That means W=0W=0 is the real hard case, period.
What I think this means strategically

This is actually useful news, because it narrows the true unresolved point:

    M=0M=0 turning-point boundaries look compatible with the determinant route.

    W=0W=0 inflection-type boundaries are the real obstacle.

So the next honest theorem to attack is not вҖңadjacent tangent-separator sign regularity.вҖқ That theorem is dead in general.

The right next theorem is something like:
What special extra structure does the actual triple (c,v5,О”7) have at W=0 boundaries?
What special extra structure does the actual triple (c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) have at W=0 boundaries?вҖӢ

Concretely, I would now attack the affine jet of О“О“ at a W=0W=0 boundary:

    compute the local expansions of
    x(h)=v5(h)c(h),y(h)=О”7(h)c(h)
    x(h)=c(h)v5вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ,y(h)=c(h)О”7вҖӢ(h)вҖӢ

    up to the first nontrivial orders around a point with W=0, Mвү 0W=0, MоҖ =0;

    determine whether the cubic coefficient in the affine normal form is genuinely generic, or whether the special origin of c,v5,О”7c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ imposes a relation;

    if it is generic, then the tangent route must be abandoned at W=0W=0 boundaries;

    if it is not generic, then that extra relation is the real hidden structure you need for the global endgame.

So the honest result of attacking W=0W=0 is:
generic inflection geometry defeats the tangent-separator theorem across W=0.
generic inflection geometry defeats the tangent-separator theorem across W=0.вҖӢ

That is not failure. It is a very valuable correction of course.

The next aggressive step is to compute the actual affine jet of О“О“ at a W=0W=0 boundary in your special variables and see whether it is truly a generic cubic inflection or something more rigid.

1. Replace the global no-secant theorem stack with conditional / partial statements
Find this theorem

\begin{theorem}[Global no-secant and no-four-secant theorem]
\label{thm:global-no-secant}

Replace the entire theorem with this

\begin{theorem}[Partial global no-secant theorem away from \(W=0\) boundaries]
\label{thm:partial-global-no-secant}
Assume every adjacent pair of ECT intervals is separated by a generic turning
boundary
\[
M(h_b)=0,\qquad M'(h_b)\neq 0,\qquad N(h_b)\neq 0,
\]
and that no adjacent pair is separated by a boundary of type
\[
W(h_b)=0,\qquad M(h_b)\neq 0.
\]
Then no affine line in the chart
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},
\qquad
y=\frac{\Delta_7}{c}
\]
is \(2\)-secant to two distinct ECT intervals. In particular, there is no
global four-secant line for the normalized planar curve \(\Gamma(h)\).
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Under the stated hypotheses, every adjacent boundary is of turning-point type.
For such adjacent pairs, Theorem~\ref{thm:adjacent-tangent-separator-sign-regularity}
and Lemma~\ref{lem:adjacent-support-ordering} apply without encountering the
generic \(W=0\) inflection obstruction. Hence adjacent fixed-slope intercept
sets are strictly ordered. Iterating this ordering over the \(x=v_5/c\)
ordering of ECT intervals shows that no affine line can be \(2\)-secant to two
distinct ECT intervals. Therefore no global four-secant line exists.
\end{proof}

Find this corollary

\begin{corollary}[Exclusion of pair-pair branch configurations]
\label{cor:pair-pair-exclusion}
...
\end{corollary}

Replace it with

\begin{corollary}[Partial exclusion of pair-pair branch configurations]
\label{cor:pair-pair-exclusion}
Under the hypotheses of Theorem~\ref{thm:partial-global-no-secant}, a bad
finite core cannot contain two distinct ECT intervals each contributing a pair
of points.
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
Each ECT interval contributes at most two points to the zero set of an affine
line in the normalized chart, and by
Theorem~\ref{thm:partial-global-no-secant} no affine line can be \(2\)-secant
to two different ECT intervals. Therefore a pair-pair configuration across two
distinct ECT intervals is impossible.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Failure of the tangent-separator route at generic \(W=0\) boundaries]
\label{rem:w0-inflection-obstruction}
The preceding adjacent tangent-separator argument is robust at generic turning
boundaries
\[
M(h_b)=0,\qquad M'(h_b)\neq 0,\qquad N(h_b)\neq 0,
\]
but does not automatically extend across generic boundaries of type
\[
W(h_b)=0,\qquad M(h_b)\neq 0.
\]
In the affine chart
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},\qquad y=\frac{\Delta_7}{c},
\]
such a boundary is generically modeled by an ordinary affine inflection
\[
y=ax^3+O(x^4),\qquad a\neq 0,
\]
and the tangent line at a point \(x=s\) on one side of the inflection meets the
opposite side again at \(x=-2s\) in the cubic model. Thus adjacent
tangent-separator sign regularity, and hence the global no-secant theorem, are
not presently justified across generic \(W=0\), \(M\neq 0\) boundaries without
additional special structure of the actual triple \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\).
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Current state of the argument]
\label{rem:current-state-of-argument}
The local one-pair analysis now consists of three layers:
the cubic leading scalar \(c(h)\), the quintic fixed-sector refinement

These determine the normalized planar curve
\[
\Gamma(h)=\left(\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}\right),

together with the derivative numerators

\qquad

\[
W(h)=

\begin{pmatrix}
c&v_5&\Delta_7\\

pure-value quotient detector, the completion of the order-\(7\) fixed-sector

read and respond to naive chats' answers and paper consequences

I read both naive-chat outputs carefully. My reaction is: they are quite useful, and the core message is sound.

The most important part is this:

The W=0, Mвү 0W=0, MоҖ =0 case really does look like a generic affine inflection in local affine coordinates, and in that model the tangent from one side hits the other side again. The explicit cubic calculation
y=ax3,Ls(x)=3as2xвҲ’2as3,ax3=Ls(x)вҖ…вҖҠвҹәвҖ…вҖҠ(xвҲ’s)2(x+2s)=0
y=ax3,LsвҖӢ(x)=3as2xвҲ’2as3,ax3=LsвҖӢ(x)вҹә(xвҲ’s)2(x+2s)=0

is exactly the right warning sign. That means the earlier adjacent tangent-separator route is not just вҖңmissing a lemmaвҖқ; it is aiming at a statement that is generically false at W=0W=0. That is a major correction, and I agree with it.

I also agree with the second major point:

The naive determinant-sign globalization across W=0W=0 fails too. The computation
detвҒЎ(1x1f(x1)1x2f(x2)1x3f(x3))=V(x1,x2,x3)вҖүf[x1,x2,x3]
det
вҖӢ111вҖӢx1вҖӢx2вҖӢx3вҖӢвҖӢf(x1вҖӢ)f(x2вҖӢ)f(x3вҖӢ)вҖӢ
вҖӢ=V(x1вҖӢ,x2вҖӢ,x3вҖӢ)f[x1вҖӢ,x2вҖӢ,x3вҖӢ]

and, for
f(x)=ax3+bx4+O(x5),
f(x)=ax3+bx4+O(x5),

the leading factor
a(x1+x2+x3)
a(x1вҖӢ+x2вҖӢ+x3вҖӢ)

shows exactly why a fixed Vandermonde-sign law breaks across an ordinary inflection. So the aggressive determinant route, in its naive form, is also dead at generic W=0W=0. I think that conclusion is right too.

The third important point is more encouraging, and I also agree with it:

A generic affine inflection does not create local 4-secants. The line-intersection count argument using
g(x)=f(x)вҲ’mxвҲ’b,gвҖІвҖІ(x)=fвҖІвҖІ(x)
g(x)=f(x)вҲ’mxвҲ’b,gвҖІвҖІ(x)=fвҖІвҖІ(x)

and the fact that fвҖІвҖІfвҖІвҖІ changes sign once implies gg has at most three zeros locally is exactly the kind of sanity check we needed. So the situation is not вҖңthe RH endgame is false locallyвҖқ; it is вҖңour current proof mechanism for the W=0W=0 case is wrong.вҖқ That is an important distinction.

So, stepping back, I think the вҖңpaper consequencesвҖқ summary is basically correct too. In particular, I agree with these consequences:

The current generic W=0W=0 tangent-separator theorem should not remain in the draft as an unconditional theorem.

The pure determinant globalization should also not be presented as if it solves W=0W=0 generically.

The endgame should now be split explicitly into:
M=0 turning boundariesvs.W=0, Mвү 0 inflection boundaries.
M=0 turning boundariesvs.W=0, MоҖ =0 inflection boundaries.

That split now feels mandatory, not stylistic.

Where I would add a little caution is here:

The naive chats show that the generic affine geometry at W=0W=0 is hostile to the old route. But they do not yet show that the actual normalized triple
(c,v5,О”7)
(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ)

has no extra structure there. So I would not conclude вҖңthe determinant route is deadвҖқ in the strongest possible sense. I would conclude:

    the generic tangent route is dead at W=0W=0;

    the generic fixed-sign determinant route is dead at W=0W=0;

    any surviving W=0W=0 strategy must use special structure of the actual triple, not abstract convexity or abstract inflection theory.

That is the main refinement I would add.

So my overall response is:
What I think the naive chats successfully established

    Generic W=0, Mвү 0W=0, MоҖ =0 behaves like an affine inflection.

    Tangent-separator sign regularity fails generically there.

    Naive determinant sign-regularity also fails generically there.

    Local no-4-secant can still be true there.

What this means for the paper

    Downgrade or remove any unconditional global theorem that uses generic W=0W=0 tangent exclusion.

    Split the endgame by boundary type.

    Reframe the W=0W=0 case as the genuine remaining geometric gap.

    State clearly that the unresolved question is whether the actual triple (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) has extra jet constraints at W=0W=0 that beat the generic affine-inflection model.

Not вҖңprove adjacent tangent-separator harder.вҖқ

Find the correct W=0-specific replacement theorem for the actual normalized triple (c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ).вҖӢ

That could be:

    a special affine-jet constraint forcing nongeneric inflection behavior,

    a restricted determinant-variation law stronger than generic affine theory,

    or some third-order Chebyshev/disconjugacy property that is special to the actual triple.

So yes: I think the naive chats gave a real correction, and I would treat their conclusions as strong evidence that the paper should now be revised around the M=0M=0 vs. W=0W=0 split.

What I would still phrase cautiously

1. Replace the adjacent tangent-separator theorem with a turning-boundary version only
Find this theorem block

It starts at

\begin{theorem}[Adjacent tangent-separator sign regularity under generic boundary hypotheses]
\label{thm:adjacent-tangent-separator-sign-regularity}

and ends at the matching \end{theorem}.
Replace the entire block with this

\begin{theorem}[Adjacent tangent-separator sign regularity across turning boundaries]
\label{thm:adjacent-tangent-separator-sign-regularity}
Let \(I_1<I_2\) be adjacent ECT intervals, and assume their common boundary
point \(h_b\) is a generic turning boundary:
\[
M(h_b)=0,\qquad M'(h_b)\neq 0,\qquad N(h_b)\neq 0.
\]
Then
\[
K(s,t)\neq 0
\qquad
(s\in I_2,\ t\in I_1),
\]
where
\[
K(s,t)=\det(F(s),F'(s),F(t)),
\qquad
F(h)=(c(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h)).
\]
Equivalently, tangent lines from the right interval do not meet the adjacent
left interval across a generic turning boundary.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
By Lemma~\ref{lem:endpoint-tangent-exclusion}, for a generic turning boundary
there is a neighborhood of the boundary pair on which \(K(s,t)\neq 0\) for
\(s\in I_2\), \(t\in I_1\). Assume for contradiction that some zero
\((s_0,t_0)\in I_2\times I_1\) exists. Choose compact subintervals
\[
J_1\Subset I_1,\qquad J_2\Subset I_2
\]
such that \((s_0,t_0)\in J_2\times J_1\) and \(J_2\times J_1\) is disjoint from
the boundary-near region where \(K\neq 0\). Then the zero set of \(K\) in the
compact set \(J_2\times J_1\) is nonempty and closed, so by
Lemma~\ref{lem:compact-first-zero-reduction} there exists a zero
\((s_*,t_*)\in J_2\times J_1\) whose \(s\)-coordinate is minimal in the
\(x=v_5/c\) ordering among all zeros in \(J_2\times J_1\).

By Lemma~\ref{lem:first-adjacent-zero-implies-tangency},
\[
K(s_*,t_*)=0,
\qquad
\partial_tK(s_*,t_*)=0,
\]
so the tangent line at \(\Gamma(s_*)\) is also tangent at \(\Gamma(t_*)\).

Across a generic turning boundary, Proposition~\ref{prop:adjacent-slope-range-separation}
shows that the interior tangent-slope ranges of the two adjacent intervals are
disjoint. Hence no common interior tangent slope can occur. This contradicts
the existence of \((s_*,t_*)\). Therefore no such zero exists, and
\[
K(s,t)\neq 0
\qquad
(s\in I_2,\ t\in I_1).
\qedhere
\]
\end{proof}

2. Replace the adjacent slope-range proposition so it no longer claims the W=0W=0 case is equally usable
Find this proposition block

It starts at

\begin{proposition}[Adjacent slope-range separation]
\label{prop:adjacent-slope-range-separation}

Replace the entire block with this

\begin{proposition}[Adjacent slope-range separation]
\label{prop:adjacent-slope-range-separation}
Let \(I_1<I_2\) be adjacent ECT intervals, meeting at a boundary point \(h_b\).

\begin{enumerate}
\item If
\[
M(h_b)=0,\qquad M'(h_b)\neq 0,\qquad N(h_b)\neq 0,
\]
then the interior tangent-slope ranges of \(I_1\) and \(I_2\) are disjoint.

\item If
\[
W(h_b)=0,\qquad M(h_b)\neq 0,
\]
then \(x=v_5/c\) remains a valid local parameter, but no adjacent tangent
separation statement is asserted here. In that case the local affine jet is
governed by
\[
\left.\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}\right|_{h_b}=0,
\qquad
\left.\frac{d^3y}{dx^3}\right|_{h_b}
=
\frac{c(h_b)^5}{M(h_b)^4}W'(h_b),
\]
so the generic behavior is an ordinary affine inflection unless further
special structure forces \(W'(h_b)=0\).
\end{enumerate}
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
In case (1), \(M(h_b)=0\) is a simple zero and \(N(h_b)\neq 0\). Therefore
\[
m(h)=\frac{N(h)}{M(h)}
\]
has opposite one-sided blow-up:
\[
m(h)\to \pm\infty
\]
as \(h\to h_b^\pm\). Hence the interior tangent-slope ranges on the two sides
are disjoint.

Case (2) is the local affine-jet computation recorded in
Remark~\ref{rem:w0-inflection-obstruction}; it identifies the cubic-inflection
coefficient but does not produce a tangent-separation theorem.
\end{proof}

3. Replace the endpoint tangent exclusion lemma so it only claims the turning-boundary case

It starts at

\begin{lemma}[Endpoint tangent exclusion at an adjacent generic boundary]
\label{lem:endpoint-tangent-exclusion}

Replace the entire block with this

\begin{lemma}[Endpoint tangent exclusion at a generic turning boundary]
\label{lem:endpoint-tangent-exclusion}
Let \(I_1<I_2\) be adjacent ECT intervals meeting at a boundary point \(h_b\),
and assume
\[
M(h_b)=0,\qquad M'(h_b)\neq 0,\qquad N(h_b)\neq 0.
\]
Then the boundary tangent line at \(\Gamma(h_b)\) is vertical and meets
neither adjacent interval in its interior.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The boundary tangent line is
\[
x=\rho_1(h_b),
\qquad
\rho_1(h)=\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)}.
\]
Since \(M(h_b)=0\) is a simple zero,
\[
\rho_1'(h_b)=0,\qquad \rho_1''(h_b)\neq 0,
\]
so \(\rho_1\) has a strict local extremum at \(h_b\). Therefore
\[
\rho_1(h)-\rho_1(h_b)
\]
has fixed sign on each adjacent interval and vanishes only at the boundary
point. Hence the vertical tangent line meets neither adjacent interval in its
interior.
\end{proof}

Replace it with

\begin{remark}[The \(W=0\) obstruction and the cubic-inflection coefficient]
\label{rem:w0-inflection-obstruction}
Let
\[
x(h):=\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},
\qquad
y(h):=\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}.
\]
At a boundary point \(h_b\) with
\[
W(h_b)=0,\qquad M(h_b)\neq 0,
\]
the coordinate \(x\) remains a valid local parameter. Since
\[
\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}=\frac{c(h)^3W(h)}{M(h)^3},
\]
one has
\[
\left.\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}\right|_{h_b}=0.
\]
Differentiating once more with respect to \(x\) gives
\[
\left.\frac{d^3y}{dx^3}\right|_{h_b}
=
\frac{c(h_b)^5}{M(h_b)^4}W'(h_b).
\]
Hence, after translating the point to the origin and subtracting the tangent
line, the normalized curve has local expansion
\[
y
=
\frac{c(h_b)^5W'(h_b)}{6M(h_b)^4}\,x^3+O(x^4).
\]

Therefore a generic \(W=0,\ M\neq 0\) boundary is an ordinary affine inflection
unless additional special structure forces
\[
W'(h_b)=0.
\]
In particular, the tangent-separator route is not presently justified across
such boundaries, and the real remaining geometric question is whether the
actual triple \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) imposes any non-generic vanishing of
\(W'(h_b)\).
\end{remark}

5. Replace the partial global no-secant theorem so it explicitly depends only on turning boundaries
Find this theorem block

\begin{theorem}[Partial global no-secant theorem away from $W=0$ boundaries]
\label{thm:partial-global-no-secant}

Replace it with

\begin{theorem}[Partial global no-secant theorem on turning-boundary generic blocks]
\label{thm:partial-global-no-secant}
Assume every adjacent pair of ECT intervals is separated by a generic turning
boundary
\[
M(h_b)=0,\qquad M'(h_b)\neq 0,\qquad N(h_b)\neq 0.
\]
Then no affine line in the chart
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},
\qquad
y=\frac{\Delta_7}{c}
\]
is \(2\)-secant to two distinct ECT intervals. In particular, there is no
global four-secant line for the normalized planar curve \(\Gamma(h)\) on such
a turning-boundary generic block.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Under the stated hypotheses, every adjacent boundary is of turning type.
For such adjacent pairs,
Theorem~\ref{thm:adjacent-tangent-separator-sign-regularity} and
Lemma~\ref{lem:adjacent-support-ordering} apply. Hence adjacent fixed-slope
intercept sets are strictly ordered. Iterating this ordering over the
\(x=v_5/c\) ordering of ECT intervals shows that no affine line can be
\(2\)-secant to two distinct ECT intervals. Therefore no global four-secant
line exists on the block.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Partial exclusion of pair-pair branch configurations]
\label{cor:pair-pair-exclusion}
Under the hypotheses of Theorem~\ref{thm:partial-global-no-secant}, a bad
finite core cannot contain two distinct ECT intervals each contributing a pair
of points on a turning-boundary generic block.
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
Each ECT interval contributes at most two points to the zero set of an affine
line in the normalized chart, and by
Theorem~\ref{thm:partial-global-no-secant} no affine line can be \(2\)-secant
to two different ECT intervals on such a block. Therefore a pair-pair
configuration across two distinct ECT intervals is impossible there.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Current state of the argument]
\label{rem:current-state-of-argument}
The local one-pair analysis now consists of three layers:
the cubic leading scalar \(c(h)\), the quintic fixed-sector refinement
\(A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)\), and the septic determinant \(\Delta_7(h)\).
These determine the normalized planar curve
\[
\Gamma(h)=\left(\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}\right),
\]
together with the derivative numerators
\[
M(h)=c(h)v_5'(h)-c'(h)v_5(h),
\qquad
N(h)=c(h)\Delta_7'(h)-c'(h)\Delta_7(h),
\]
and the projective Wronskian
\[
W(h)=
\det\!
\begin{pmatrix}
c&v_5&\Delta_7\\
c'&v_5'&\Delta_7'\\
c''&v_5''&\Delta_7''
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

On intervals where \(c\), \(M\), and \(W\) do not vanish, the triple
\((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) is an ECT system. The turning-boundary case
\[
M=0,\qquad M'\neq 0,\qquad N\neq 0
\]
is comparatively well behaved. The remaining nonlocal geometric issue is the
\(W=0,\ M\neq 0\) case, where the cubic-inflection coefficient is controlled by
\(W'(h_b)\) and the current architecture does not yet force nongeneric
vanishing. The remaining audited issues are therefore both local and global:
first-order surjectivity, the quintic pure-value quotient detector, the
completion of the order-\(7\) fixed-sector algebra, and the special
\(W=0\)-boundary structure of the actual triple \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\).
\end{remark}

ok lets pause for a second and think about the big picture. we seem to be finding that we have to go continually to ever-higher orders. at each order we find some cases that resolve neatly like M=0 did at order-7, but others like W=0 do not. Is there an overall pattern here that is order independent that we are missing?

Yes вҖ” I think there probably is an order-independent pattern hiding here, and the repeated need to go to higher odd orders is a strong sign of it.

What your current path is doing, in effect, is this:

At each stage you build a new scalar or residual invariant that survives after quotienting out everything the lower orders failed to see.

Schematically:

    order 3 gives the first leading line cc,

    order 5 gives a first residual scalar like v5v5вҖӢ,

    order 7 gives a second residual scalar like О”7О”7вҖӢ,

    then at W=0W=0 you discover that even (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) is not yet enough to control the local geometry globally.

That is exactly the pattern of an iterated moving-frame / Wilczynski-type projective normalization process. Each time a lower invariant vanishes, you are forced to prolong and look one order higher for the next genuinely projective obstruction.

So the вҖңgo to higher ordersвҖқ phenomenon may not be an accident or a weakness. It may be telling you that the real object is not any particular finite-order invariant, but an entire projective jet flag of the normalized curve or germ.

In other words, the hidden order-independent object may be:
F(h)=(c(h),v5(h),О”7(h),вҖҰвҖү)
F(h)=(c(h),v5вҖӢ(h),О”7вҖӢ(h),вҖҰ)

not as three arbitrary scalars, but as the beginning of a canonical jet-adapted sequence, where each new entry is the first quantity not killed by the previous normalizations.

That suggests a few big-picture possibilities.
1. You may be chasing a projective moving-frame hierarchy

The pattern вҖңlower invariant vanishes вҮ’вҮ’ go to the next odd orderвҖқ is exactly what happens when one is implicitly constructing canonical invariants of a curve under reparametrization / projective transformation.

At order 3 you detect first nontrivial projective curvature-type data.
At order 5 you detect a first residual after modding out the cubic line.
At order 7 you detect a second residual determinant.
At W=0W=0, you are really discovering that the triple (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ) is still not the full projective flag.

So one missing conceptual step may be:
Stop treating c,v5,О”7,вҖҰ as ad hoc witnesses, and identify the canonical full residual tower.
Stop treating c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ,вҖҰ as ad hoc witnesses, and identify the canonical full residual tower.вҖӢ

If such a tower exists cleanly, then the right theorem might be an order-independent statement like:

    A bad core would force all invariants in the residual tower to vanish in a way incompatible with the normalized family.

That would be much better than repeatedly inventing the next witness.
2. The real invariant may be вҖңminimal nonzero odd orderвҖқ

Right now you keep discovering that when one residual vanishes, the geometry is controlled by the next odd term.

That suggests the global classification may depend only on:
r(h):=the first odd order at which the normalized residual invariant is nonzero.
r(h):=the first odd order at which the normalized residual invariant is nonzero.

Then instead of proving separate ad hoc lemmas at orders 5, 7, 9, вҖҰ, you might aim for a structural theorem:

    If the first nonzero residual occurs at odd order 2k+12k+1, then the local affine/projective normal form is of a specific type, and either:

        it is benign (like the M=0M=0 turning-point case), or

        it forces a canonical next invariant at order 2k+32k+3, or

        it falls into a small finite list of model geometries.

That would explain why you keep seeing the same pattern.
3. W=0W=0 is probably not вҖңthe next isolated bad case,вҖқ but evidence that the finite-order picture is incomplete

The key signal from W=0W=0 is that even after building (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ), the local affine geometry can still look generic. That means the finite package you currently have is still not the true canonical closure.

So the big-picture lesson is probably not вҖңwe need order 9 tooвҖқ in a naive way.

It is more likely:
We need the rule that produces the next invariant automatically, not one more manually computed invariant.
We need the rule that produces the next invariant automatically, not one more manually computed invariant.вҖӢ

That is the conceptual difference between an exhaustion argument and a structural theory.
4. There may be a dichotomy between вҖңfinite-type benignвҖқ and вҖңprolongingвҖқ cases

Your examples already suggest this:

    M=0M=0 at order 7 turned out to be benign: the local model gave enough structure.

    W=0W=0 did not: it behaved like a generic affine inflection and demanded more.

That suggests a general dichotomy:
Type A: benign singularities

Vanishing of a current invariant leads to a local model that already rules out the bad configuration.
Type B: prolonging singularities

Vanishing of a current invariant does not resolve the geometry, but instead reveals the next residual degree of freedom and forces you up one more odd order.

If this is right, the real theorem is not about order 7 or 9. It is something like:

    Every singularity in the normalized curve/germ is either benign or prolonging, and prolonging singularities generate a canonical next invariant.

That would finally make the process feel principled rather than endless.
5. The вҖңcorrectвҖқ object may be a scalar linear ODE or disconjugacy class

There is another big-picture pattern here.

Once you have three functions (c,v5,О”7)(c,v5вҖӢ,О”7вҖӢ), they determine a third-order scalar ODE locally. If you kept going and built a canonical full sequence, you might really be constructing a distinguished linear differential equation whose solution space is spanned by these invariants.

Then the actual order-independent theorem might be about:

    disconjugacy,

    total positivity,

    oscillation bounds,

    or a projective curve in PnPn associated to the solution space.

That would naturally explain why each new odd order matters: you are uncovering more of the effective linear system. The true statement might live at the ODE / total positivity level, not at the finite-jet level.

    and ambiguity terms in the fixed-sector quotient.

3. Why the K1K1вҖӢ slice is the only plausible source of [О”7][1][О”7вҖӢ][1]

At the level of the odd two-point germ, the genuinely new q(7)q(7)-slice is same-point/even. To enter an odd pair invariant at septic order, it must be coupled with an odd separation factor.

The first odd separation factor is exactly the K1K1вҖӢ-transport slice.

That is why the direct septic witness appeared in the K1K1вҖӢ computation:
A7,K1f=u7,K1I+v7,K1S,
A7,K1вҖӢfвҖӢ=u7,K1вҖӢвҖӢI+v7,K1вҖӢвҖӢS,

with
u7,K1[1]=Оә((x2вҲ’x1)ОҪ6[1]+v(Оі5вҲ’Оұ5)[1]),v7,K1[1]=0.
u7,K1вҖӢ[1]вҖӢ=Оә((x2вҖӢвҲ’x1вҖӢ)ОҪ6[1]вҖӢ+v(Оі5вҖӢвҲ’Оұ5вҖӢ)[1]),v7,K1вҖӢ[1]вҖӢ=0.

So structurally:
[A7f][1] must come from the odd-separation coupling of X5[1], i.e. the direct K1 slice.
[A7fвҖӢ][1] must come from the odd-separation coupling of X5[1]вҖӢ, i.e. the direct K1вҖӢ slice.вҖӢ

That is the core idea.
4. Sector elimination, carefully

Here is the proof skeleton I would try to promote into a lemma.
(a) K3K3вҖӢ and K5K5вҖӢ septic sectors

These only involve lower same-point orders. Since Xj[1]=0Xj[1]вҖӢ=0 for j<5j<5, they have no weight-1 part:
[A7,K3f][1]=0,[A7,K5f][1]=0.
[A7,K3вҖӢfвҖӢ][1]=0,[A7,K5вҖӢfвҖӢ][1]=0.

That part is clean.
(b) Pure same-point order-7 contributions

These are more delicate, but here is the right structural point:

A pure same-point order-7 term by itself is exchange-even. The object О”7О”7вҖӢ is not just any septic coefficient; it is the odd two-point residual determinant
О”7=u7v5вҲ’u5v7
О”7вҖӢ=u7вҖӢv5вҖӢвҲ’u5вҖӢv7вҖӢ

in the fixed-sector quotient.

So a pure same-point term can only affect [О”7][1][О”7вҖӢ][1] if it survives both:

    passage to the odd pair germ,

    passage to the quotient modulo the affine ambiguity
    A7fвҶҰA7f+c2A5f.
    A7fвҖӢвҶҰA7fвҖӢ+c2вҖӢA5fвҖӢ.

The natural expectation is that pure same-point order-7 terms either:

    never enter the odd pair germ at weight 1,

    or enter only through lower/mixed pieces that do not affect the top new coefficient.

This is the weakest point in the argument, but it is also the one most likely to be true structurally.
(c) Ambiguity terms

Since
О”7=u7v5вҲ’u5v7
О”7вҖӢ=u7вҖӢv5вҖӢвҲ’u5вҖӢv7вҖӢ

is invariant under
A7fвҶҰA7f+c2A5f,
A7fвҖӢвҶҰA7fвҖӢ+c2вҖӢA5fвҖӢ,

any contribution that is purely along A5fA5fвҖӢ cannot affect [О”7][1][О”7вҖӢ][1].

So even if some non-K1K1вҖӢ weight-1 piece survives at the level of A7fA7fвҖӢ, if it lies in the ambiguity direction it drops out of О»7О»7вҖӢ.

That is exactly why О”7О”7вҖӢ is the right scalar to analyze.
5. What this gives for О»7О»7вҖӢ

Assuming the above grading lemma, the weight-1 part of О”7О”7вҖӢ is just the direct K1K1вҖӢ slice:
О»7=[О”7][1]=v5вҖүu7,K1[1]вҲ’u5вҖүv7,K1[1]=v5вҖүu7,K1[1],
О»7вҖӢ=[О”7вҖӢ][1]=v5вҖӢu7,K1вҖӢ[1]вҖӢвҲ’u5вҖӢv7,K1вҖӢ[1]вҖӢ=v5вҖӢu7,K1вҖӢ[1]вҖӢ,

because
v7,K1[1]=0.
v7,K1вҖӢ[1]вҖӢ=0.

So
О»7=ОәвҖүv5((x2вҲ’x1)ОҪ6[1]+v(Оі5вҲ’Оұ5)[1]).
О»7вҖӢ=Оәv5вҖӢ((x2вҖӢвҲ’x1вҖӢ)ОҪ6[1]вҖӢ+v(Оі5вҖӢвҲ’Оұ5вҖӢ)[1]).вҖӢ

And on the minimal graded model, that coefficient was already computed as
(x2вҲ’x1)ОҪ6[1]+v(Оі5вҲ’Оұ5)[1]=eu(a2+abвҲ’b2)a2b4(a+b)2,
(x2вҖӢвҲ’x1вҖӢ)ОҪ6[1]вҖӢ+v(Оі5вҖӢвҲ’Оұ5вҖӢ)[1]=a2b4(a+b)2eu(a2+abвҲ’b2)вҖӢ,

so, schematically,
О»7=ОәвҖүv5вҖүeu(a2+abвҲ’b2)a2b4(a+b)2
О»7вҖӢ=Оәv5вҖӢa2b4(a+b)2eu(a2+abвҲ’b2)вҖӢвҖӢ

up to normalization conventions.

That is the exact coefficient controlling the highest new part of WW and WвҖІWвҖІ.
6. Why this matters for the W=0W=0 problem

Because the exact identities
W=MвҖүО”7вҖІвҖІ+(v5cвҖІвҖІвҲ’cv5вҖІвҖІ)вҖүО”7вҖІ+(cвҖІv5вҖІвҖІвҲ’v5вҖІcвҖІвҖІ)вҖүО”7,
W=MО”7вҖІвҖІвҖӢ+(v5вҖӢcвҖІвҖІвҲ’cv5вҖІвҖІвҖӢ)О”7вҖІвҖӢ+(cвҖІv5вҖІвҖІвҖӢвҲ’v5вҖІвҖӢcвҖІвҖІ)О”7вҖӢ,
WвҖІ=MвҖүО”7вҖІвҖІвҖІ+(v5cвҖІвҖІвҖІвҲ’cv5вҖІвҖІвҖІ)вҖүО”7вҖІ+(cвҖІv5вҖІвҖІвҖІвҲ’v5вҖІcвҖІвҖІвҖІ)вҖүО”7
WвҖІ=MО”7вҖІвҖІвҖІвҖӢ+(v5вҖӢcвҖІвҖІвҖІвҲ’cv5вҖІвҖІвҖІвҖӢ)О”7вҖІвҖӢ+(cвҖІv5вҖІвҖІвҖІвҖӢвҲ’v5вҖІвҖӢcвҖІвҖІвҖІ)О”7вҖӢ

show that the highest genuinely new parts are
[q(9)]вҖүWвҲјMвҖүО»7,[q(10)]вҖүWвҖІвҲјMвҖүО»7.
[q(9)]WвҲјMО»7вҖӢ,[q(10)]WвҖІвҲјMО»7вҖӢ.

So if О»7О»7вҖӢ is really already determined by the direct K1K1вҖӢ slice, then the W=0W=0 obstruction is decided at order 7, not by a new primitive order-9 invariant.

That is the big payoff.
7. What is still missing to make this referee-proof

I think there is now one exact missing lemma:
Non-K1 septic sectors do not contribute to [О”7][1].
Non-K1вҖӢ septic sectors do not contribute to [О”7вҖӢ][1].вҖӢ

To prove that cleanly, the paper needs a precise grading statement like:

    The weight-1 part of the odd septic fixed-sector quotient comes only from the odd-separation coupling of the order-5 same-point block.

If you can formalize that, then the rest really does collapse.
Bottom line

So the attack result is:

    I do not think you need to compute all of order 9 as a new primitive layer to address the current W=0W=0 problem.

    The exact identities for WW and WвҖІWвҖІ strongly suggest the top new behavior is already encoded in the full septic coefficient О»7О»7вҖӢ.

    The only remaining conceptual gap is to formalize why non-K1K1вҖӢ septic sectors cannot alter that top new coefficient.

That is the right next lemma to write down and prove.

ok attack

Here is the hardest push I can make on the key lemma.

The statement to attack is:
[О”7][1] comes only from the direct K1 septic transport slice.
[О”7вҖӢ][1] comes only from the direct K1вҖӢ septic transport slice.вҖӢ

I do not think this is fully proved yet, but I think there is a pretty strong structural argument for it.

Let me formalize the right grading.

Define вҖңnew-jet weightвҖқ by declaring the first genuinely new same-point septic input to have weight one:
wt(q(7))=1,wt(all lower same-point jets)=0.
wt(q(7))=1,wt(all lower same-point jets)=0.

Then for any quantity AA, write
A=A[0]+A[1]+A[вүҘ2],
A=A[0]+A[1]+A[вүҘ2],

where A[1]A[1] is the part linear in the genuinely new q(7)q(7)-slice.

The object we want is
О»7:=[О”7][1].
О»7вҖӢ:=[О”7вҖӢ][1].

So the question is not вҖңwhat is all of A7fA7fвҖӢ?вҖқ It is only:
what can contribute to the weight-1 part of О”7?
what can contribute to the weight-1 part of О”7вҖӢ?вҖӢ

Now the crucial structural fact is:

The first place the genuinely new q(7)q(7)-slice enters the same-point expansion is the order-5 block. In the current notation, that means
Xj[1]=0(j<5),
Xj[1]вҖӢ=0(j<5),

and
X5[1]вү 0
X5[1]вҖӢоҖ =0

is where the new slice first appears.

That already kills a lot.

At total septic order, any sector built only from X0,вҖҰ,X4X0вҖӢ,вҖҰ,X4вҖӢ is automatically weight zero. So:
[A7,K3f][1]=0,[A7,K5f][1]=0.
[A7,K3вҖӢfвҖӢ][1]=0,[A7,K5вҖӢfвҖӢ][1]=0.

That part is basically clean.

So the only possible weight-1 sources are:

    the K1K1вҖӢ transport slice that couples the new X5[1]X5[1]вҖӢ data into the odd septic pair term;

    pure same-point order-7 terms, if such terms survive into the odd pair fixed-sector quotient at weight one.

The first source is exactly the one you already computed:
u7,K1[1]=Оә((x2вҲ’x1)ОҪ6[1]+v(Оі5вҲ’Оұ5)[1]),v7,K1[1]=0,
u7,K1вҖӢ[1]вҖӢ=Оә((x2вҖӢвҲ’x1вҖӢ)ОҪ6[1]вҖӢ+v(Оі5вҖӢвҲ’Оұ5вҖӢ)[1]),v7,K1вҖӢ[1]вҖӢ=0,

hence
[О”7][1]=v5вҖүu7,K1[1]вҲ’u5вҖүv7,K1[1]=v5вҖүu7,K1[1]
[О”7вҖӢ][1]=v5вҖӢu7,K1вҖӢ[1]вҖӢвҲ’u5вҖӢv7,K1вҖӢ[1]вҖӢ=v5вҖӢu7,K1вҖӢ[1]вҖӢ

provided no other sector contributes to weight one.

So the entire problem has now shrunk to the second source:
Can a pure same-point septic term contribute to [О”7][1]?
Can a pure same-point septic term contribute to [О”7вҖӢ][1]?вҖӢ

This is where the right structural argument is:

A pure same-point order-7 term is exchange-even before transport. But О”7О”7вҖӢ is not an arbitrary septic scalar; it is the odd two-point residual determinant
О”7=u7v5вҲ’u5v7
О”7вҖӢ=u7вҖӢv5вҖӢвҲ’u5вҖӢv7вҖӢ

in the fixed-sector quotient. So for a pure same-point order-7 contribution to survive into [О”7][1][О”7вҖӢ][1], it would have to do three things simultaneously:

    survive passage from same-point data to the odd pair germ,

    survive projection to the fixed sector,

    survive quotienting by the affine ambiguity
    A7fвҶҰA7f+c2A5f.
    A7fвҖӢвҶҰA7fвҖӢ+c2вҖӢA5fвҖӢ.

That is a very stiff constraint.

The natural expectation is that pure same-point septic terms either:

    never enter the odd pair germ at weight one at all, because they lack the odd-separation factor that the K1K1вҖӢ channel supplies, or

    if they do enter A7fA7fвҖӢ, they do so only in the ambiguity direction and therefore drop out of О”7О”7вҖӢ.

This is the one point I would still call an audit item, but it is now a very focused one.

If that expectation is right, then the proof is almost done:

    K3K3вҖӢ and K5K5вҖӢ sectors are weight zero because they cannot see X5[1]X5[1]вҖӢ,

    pure same-point septic terms do not contribute to the weight-1 odd fixed-sector quotient,

    therefore
    [О”7][1]=[О”7,K1][1].
    [О”7вҖӢ][1]=[О”7,K1вҖӢвҖӢ][1].

Then the coefficient you already computed on the minimal graded model is in fact the full top new coefficient:
О»7=[О”7][1]=ОәвҖүv5((x2вҲ’x1)ОҪ6[1]+v(Оі5вҲ’Оұ5)[1]),
О»7вҖӢ=[О”7вҖӢ][1]=Оәv5вҖӢ((x2вҖӢвҲ’x1вҖӢ)ОҪ6[1]вҖӢ+v(Оі5вҖӢвҲ’Оұ5вҖӢ)[1]),

and on the explicit model,
(x2вҲ’x1)ОҪ6[1]+v(Оі5вҲ’Оұ5)[1]=eu(a2+abвҲ’b2)a2b4(a+b)2,
(x2вҖӢвҲ’x1вҖӢ)ОҪ6[1]вҖӢ+v(Оі5вҖӢвҲ’Оұ5вҖӢ)[1]=a2b4(a+b)2eu(a2+abвҲ’b2)вҖӢ,

so schematically
О»7=ОәвҖүv5вҖүeu(a2+abвҲ’b2)a2b4(a+b)2.
О»7вҖӢ=Оәv5вҖӢa2b4(a+b)2eu(a2+abвҲ’b2)вҖӢ.

That is exactly what you want, because the exact identities
W=MвҖүО”7вҖІвҖІ+(v5cвҖІвҖІвҲ’cv5вҖІвҖІ)вҖүО”7вҖІ+(cвҖІv5вҖІвҖІвҲ’v5вҖІcвҖІвҖІ)вҖүО”7,
W=MО”7вҖІвҖІвҖӢ+(v5вҖӢcвҖІвҖІвҲ’cv5вҖІвҖІвҖӢ)О”7вҖІвҖӢ+(cвҖІv5вҖІвҖІвҖӢвҲ’v5вҖІвҖӢcвҖІвҖІ)О”7вҖӢ,
WвҖІ=MвҖүО”7вҖІвҖІвҖІ+(v5cвҖІвҖІвҖІвҲ’cv5вҖІвҖІвҖІ)вҖүО”7вҖІ+(cвҖІv5вҖІвҖІвҖІвҲ’v5вҖІcвҖІвҖІвҖІ)вҖүО”7
WвҖІ=MО”7вҖІвҖІвҖІвҖӢ+(v5вҖӢcвҖІвҖІвҖІвҲ’cv5вҖІвҖІвҖІвҖӢ)О”7вҖІвҖӢ+(cвҖІv5вҖІвҖІвҖІвҖӢвҲ’v5вҖІвҖӢcвҖІвҖІвҖІ)О”7вҖӢ

then imply
[q(9)]вҖүWвҲјMвҖүО»7,[q(10)]вҖүWвҖІвҲјMвҖүО»7.
[q(9)]WвҲјMО»7вҖӢ,[q(10)]WвҖІвҲјMО»7вҖӢ.

So the W=0W=0 inflection coefficient is already being decided by the full septic coefficient, not by a new primitive order-9 invariant.

So the clean next theorem to try to prove is:
Pure same-point septic terms do not contribute to the weight-1 odd fixed-sector quotient.
Pure same-point septic terms do not contribute to the weight-1 odd fixed-sector quotient.вҖӢ

That is now the exact missing lemma. If you get that, then the order-7 package really does decide the W=0W=0 issue at the top new-jet level.

attack

Here is the cleanest attack I can make, and I think it gets the missing lemma almost all the way.

The key is to separate two different gradings:

    new-jet weight: the first genuinely new same-point slice q(7)q(7) has weight 11;

    pair parity in the separation variable ss: the odd two-point germ only sees terms odd in ss.

That second grading is the one that kills the pure same-point septic terms.
1. The real lemma to prove

What we want is:
[О”7][1] comes only from the direct K1 septic transport slice.
[О”7вҖӢ][1] comes only from the direct K1вҖӢ septic transport slice.вҖӢ

Write the odd two-point family using symmetric placement
tВұ=hВұs2.
tВұвҖӢ=hВұ2sвҖӢ.

All the local coefficients in the paper are built from expansions around the midpoint hh, with ss the pair-separation variable.

Now О”7О”7вҖӢ is not an arbitrary septic scalar. It is extracted from the odd pair germ. So anything that is even in ss dies before you ever get to the fixed-sector quotient.

That is the key.
2. Pure same-point septic terms are even in ss

A вҖңpure same-pointвҖқ order-7 term means: it depends only on midpoint jet data at hh, not on an odd transport/separation kernel.

But midpoint jet data is invariant under swapping the pair:
t+вҶ”tвҲ’вҹәsвҶҰвҲ’s.
t+вҖӢвҶ”tвҲ’вҖӢвҹәsвҶҰвҲ’s.

So any contribution built only from midpoint jets is an even function of ss.

Therefore:
Pure same-point septic terms have even pair parity and vanish in the odd pair germ.
Pure same-point septic terms have even pair parity and vanish in the odd pair germ.вҖӢ

This is stronger than the earlier vague statement that they вҖңprobably drop out.вҖқ They should drop out for a basic symmetry reason.

So pure same-point order-7 terms do not contribute to [О”7][1][О”7вҖӢ][1].

That is the first big gain.
3. Which odd sectors remain?

Once pure same-point terms are gone, only odd-separation transport channels can survive in the odd pair germ.

At septic order, these are the transport channels carrying odd powers of separation:

    K1K1вҖӢ,

    K3K3вҖӢ,

    K5K5вҖӢ,
    and analogous odd transport kernels if present in the normalization.

Now apply the new-jet grading.

The first genuinely new same-point q(7)q(7)-slice enters the order-5 same-point block:
X5[1]вү 0,Xj[1]=0(j<5).
X5[1]вҖӢоҖ =0,Xj[1]вҖӢ=0(j<5).

So:

    any septic sector built only from X0,вҖҰ,X4X0вҖӢ,вҖҰ,X4вҖӢ is weight 00,

    therefore K3K3вҖӢ and K5K5вҖӢ septic sectors cannot contribute to [О”7][1][О”7вҖӢ][1],

    the only odd transport sector that can see the new X5[1]X5[1]вҖӢ data at septic order is the direct K1K1вҖӢ slice.

So now we have:
[K3][1]=[K5][1]=0,[pure same-point][1]=0,
[K3вҖӢ][1]=[K5вҖӢ][1]=0,[pure same-point][1]=0,вҖӢ

hence
[О”7][1]=[О”7,K1][1].
[О”7вҖӢ][1]=[О”7,K1вҖӢвҖӢ][1].вҖӢ

That is the missing structural lemma in essentially complete form.
4. Consequence for the coefficient О»7О»7вҖӢ

So the full highest new coefficient of О”7О”7вҖӢ is already the direct K1K1вҖӢ one:
О»7=[О”7][1]=[О”7,K1][1].
О»7вҖӢ=[О”7вҖӢ][1]=[О”7,K1вҖӢвҖӢ][1].

Earlier you computed on the minimal graded model:
u7,K1[1]=Оә((x2вҲ’x1)ОҪ6[1]+v(Оі5вҲ’Оұ5)[1]),v7,K1[1]=0.
u7,K1вҖӢ[1]вҖӢ=Оә((x2вҖӢвҲ’x1вҖӢ)ОҪ6[1]вҖӢ+v(Оі5вҖӢвҲ’Оұ5вҖӢ)[1]),v7,K1вҖӢ[1]вҖӢ=0.

Therefore
О»7=v5вҖүu7,K1[1]=ОәвҖүv5((x2вҲ’x1)ОҪ6[1]+v(Оі5вҲ’Оұ5)[1]).
О»7вҖӢ=v5вҖӢu7,K1вҖӢ[1]вҖӢ=Оәv5вҖӢ((x2вҖӢвҲ’x1вҖӢ)ОҪ6[1]вҖӢ+v(Оі5вҖӢвҲ’Оұ5вҖӢ)[1]).

And on the explicit minimal model,
(x2вҲ’x1)ОҪ6[1]+v(Оі5вҲ’Оұ5)[1]=eu(a2+abвҲ’b2)a2b4(a+b)2,
(x2вҖӢвҲ’x1вҖӢ)ОҪ6[1]вҖӢ+v(Оі5вҖӢвҲ’Оұ5вҖӢ)[1]=a2b4(a+b)2eu(a2+abвҲ’b2)вҖӢ,

so schematically
О»7=ОәвҖүv5вҖүeu(a2+abвҲ’b2)a2b4(a+b)2.
О»7вҖӢ=Оәv5вҖӢa2b4(a+b)2eu(a2+abвҲ’b2)вҖӢ.вҖӢ

That is now very plausibly the full top new coefficient, not just one contribution.
5. Why this matters for WW and WвҖІWвҖІ

Because the exact identities
W=MвҖүО”7вҖІвҖІ+(v5cвҖІвҖІвҲ’cv5вҖІвҖІ)вҖүО”7вҖІ+(cвҖІv5вҖІвҖІвҲ’v5вҖІcвҖІвҖІ)вҖүО”7,
W=MО”7вҖІвҖІвҖӢ+(v5вҖӢcвҖІвҖІвҲ’cv5вҖІвҖІвҖӢ)О”7вҖІвҖӢ+(cвҖІv5вҖІвҖІвҖӢвҲ’v5вҖІвҖӢcвҖІвҖІ)О”7вҖӢ,

imply
