ok do that last step

Use this as a new lemma plus a short corollary.

Place it in the fixed-sector / septic subsection, immediately after the explicit septic K1K1вҖӢ-transport formulas and before the remark that says the full septic coefficient is still under audit.
Insert this lemma

\begin{lemma}[Parity elimination of pure same-point septic terms]
\label{lem:parity-elimination-pure-septic}
Let \(t_\pm=h\pm s/2\), and let the odd two-point germ be defined as the odd
part under the involution
\[
s\longmapsto -s
\qquad
(\text{equivalently }t_+\leftrightarrow t_-).
\]
Then any pure same-point septic contribution is even in \(s\), hence vanishes in
the odd two-point germ.

In particular, pure same-point septic terms do not contribute to the weight-one
part \([\Delta_7]^{[1]}\) of the septic residual determinant.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
A pure same-point term depends only on midpoint jet data at \(h\), i.e. on the
local one-point expansion evaluated at the midpoint, and carries no transport
factor coming from the ordered pair \((t_+,t_-)\). Therefore it is invariant
under exchanging the two points:
\[
t_+\leftrightarrow t_-.
\]
Since this exchange is exactly the involution \(s\mapsto -s\), every pure
same-point septic term is an even function of \(s\).

By definition, the odd two-point germ is obtained by projecting to the odd part
under \(s\mapsto -s\). Hence every even contribution vanishes in that
projection. Therefore pure same-point septic terms do not survive into the odd
pair germ.

Finally, \(\Delta_7\) is extracted from the odd pair germ after fixed-sector
projection and quotient normalization. Since pure same-point septic terms are
already zero in the odd germ, they cannot contribute to \([\Delta_7]^{[1]}\).
\end{proof}

Insert this corollary immediately after

\begin{corollary}[Weight-one septic reduction to the direct \(K_1\) slice]
\label{cor:weight-one-septic-reduction}
Let \([\,\cdot\,]^{[1]}\) denote the part linear in the first genuinely new
same-point \(q^{(7)}\)-slice. Then
\[
[\Delta_7]^{[1]}=[\Delta_{7,K_1}]^{[1]}.
\]
Equivalently, the highest genuinely new \(q^{(7)}\)-coefficient of the septic
residual determinant comes only from the direct septic \(K_1\)-transport slice.
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
The first genuinely new \(q^{(7)}\)-slice enters the same-point expansion at
order \(5\), so
\[
X_j^{[1]}=0
\qquad (j<5).
\]
Hence every septic transport sector built only from lower same-point orders is
weight zero; in particular, the \(K_3\)- and \(K_5\)-transport sectors do not
contribute to \([\Delta_7]^{[1]}\).

By Lemma~\ref{lem:parity-elimination-pure-septic}, pure same-point septic terms
also do not contribute to \([\Delta_7]^{[1]}\), because they vanish already in
the odd two-point germ.

Thus the only septic sector that can see the weight-one \(q^{(7)}\)-slice is
the direct \(K_1\)-transport sector, and therefore
\[
[\Delta_7]^{[1]}=[\Delta_{7,K_1}]^{[1]}.
\qedhere
\]
\end{proof}

Then add one short sentence to the existing audit remark about full septic completion

In the remark that currently says the full septic fixed-sector coefficient is still under audit, add this sentence at the end:

What remains open is therefore not the weight-one \(q^{(7)}\)-coefficient of
\(\Delta_7\), which is reduced to the direct \(K_1\) slice by
Corollary~\ref{cor:weight-one-septic-reduction}, but the lower/mixed septic
contributions to the full total pair \((u_7,v_7)\).

That gives you the вҖңlast stepвҖқ in a clean, referee-readable form:

    parity kills pure same-point septic terms,

    lower transport sectors cannot see the new q(7)q(7)-slice,

    so the weight-one part of О”7О”7вҖӢ is exactly the direct K1K1вҖӢ slice.

\begin{lemma}[Parity elimination of pure same-point septic terms]
\label{lem:parity-elimination-pure-septic}
Let \(t_\pm=h\pm s/2\), and let the odd two-point germ be defined as the odd
part under the involution
\[
s\longmapsto -s
\qquad
(\text{equivalently }t_+\leftrightarrow t_-).
\]
Then any pure same-point septic contribution is even in \(s\), hence vanishes in
the odd two-point germ.

In particular, pure same-point septic terms do not contribute to the weight-one
part \([\Delta_7]^{[1]}\) of the septic residual determinant.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
A pure same-point term depends only on midpoint jet data at \(h\), i.e. on the
local one-point expansion evaluated at the midpoint, and carries no transport
factor coming from the ordered pair \((t_+,t_-)\). Therefore it is invariant
under exchanging the two points:
\[
t_+\leftrightarrow t_-.
\]
Since this exchange is exactly the involution \(s\mapsto -s\), every pure
same-point septic term is an even function of \(s\).

By definition, the odd two-point germ is obtained by projecting to the odd part
under \(s\mapsto -s\). Hence every even contribution vanishes in that
projection. Therefore pure same-point septic terms do not survive into the odd
pair germ.

Finally, \(\Delta_7\) is extracted from the odd pair germ after fixed-sector
projection and quotient normalization. Since pure same-point septic terms are
already zero in the odd germ, they cannot contribute to \([\Delta_7]^{[1]}\).
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Weight-one septic reduction to the direct \(K_1\) slice]
\label{cor:weight-one-septic-reduction}
Let \([\,\cdot\,]^{[1]}\) denote the part linear in the first genuinely new
same-point \(q^{(7)}\)-slice. Then
\[
[\Delta_7]^{[1]}=[\Delta_{7,K_1}]^{[1]}.
\]
Equivalently, the highest genuinely new \(q^{(7)}\)-coefficient of the septic
residual determinant comes only from the direct septic \(K_1\)-transport slice.
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
The first genuinely new \(q^{(7)}\)-slice enters the same-point expansion at
order \(5\), so
\[
X_j^{[1]}=0
\qquad (j<5).
\]
Hence every septic transport sector built only from lower same-point orders is
weight zero; in particular, the \(K_3\)- and \(K_5\)-transport sectors do not
contribute to \([\Delta_7]^{[1]}\).

By Lemma~\ref{lem:parity-elimination-pure-septic}, pure same-point septic terms
also do not contribute to \([\Delta_7]^{[1]}\), because they vanish already in
the odd two-point germ.

Thus the only septic sector that can see the weight-one \(q^{(7)}\)-slice is
the direct \(K_1\)-transport sector, and therefore
\[
[\Delta_7]^{[1]}=[\Delta_{7,K_1}]^{[1]}.
\qedhere
\]
\end{proof}

3. In В§12.4, replace the adjacent slope-range proposition

\begin{proposition}[Adjacent slope-range separation]
\label{prop:adjacent-slope-range-separation}
Let \(I_1<I_2\) be adjacent ECT intervals, meeting at a boundary point \(h_b\).

\begin{enumerate}
\item If
\[
M(h_b)=0,\qquad M'(h_b)\neq 0,\qquad N(h_b)\neq 0,
\]
then the interior tangent-slope ranges of \(I_1\) and \(I_2\) are disjoint.

\item If
\[
W(h_b)=0,\qquad M(h_b)\neq 0,
\]
then \(x=v_5/c\) remains a valid local parameter, but no adjacent tangent
separation statement is asserted here. In that case the local affine jet is
governed by
\[
\left.\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}\right|_{h_b}=0,
\qquad
\left.\frac{d^3y}{dx^3}\right|_{h_b}
=
\frac{c(h_b)^5}{M(h_b)^4}W'(h_b),
\]
so the generic behavior is an ordinary affine inflection unless further
special structure forces \(W'(h_b)=0\).
\end{enumerate}
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
In case (1), \(M(h_b)=0\) is a simple zero and \(N(h_b)\neq 0\). Therefore
\[
m(h)=\frac{N(h)}{M(h)}
\]
has opposite one-sided blow-up:
\[
m(h)\to \pm\infty
\]
as \(h\to h_b^\pm\). Hence the interior tangent-slope ranges on the two sides
are disjoint.

Case (2) is the local affine-jet computation recorded in
Remark~\ref{rem:w0-inflection-obstruction}; it identifies the cubic-inflection
coefficient but does not produce a tangent-separation theorem.
\end{proof}

4. In В§12.4, replace the endpoint tangent exclusion lemma

It should now only treat the turning-point case.
Find

\begin{lemma}[Endpoint tangent exclusion at an adjacent generic boundary]
\label{lem:endpoint-tangent-exclusion}
...
\end{lemma}

Replace the entire block with

\begin{lemma}[Endpoint tangent exclusion at a generic turning boundary]
\label{lem:endpoint-tangent-exclusion}
Let \(I_1<I_2\) be adjacent ECT intervals meeting at a boundary point \(h_b\),
and assume
\[
M(h_b)=0,\qquad M'(h_b)\neq 0,\qquad N(h_b)\neq 0.
\]
Then the boundary tangent line at \(\Gamma(h_b)\) is vertical and meets
neither adjacent interval in its interior.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The boundary tangent line is
\[
x=\rho_1(h_b),
\qquad
\rho_1(h)=\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)}.
\]
Since \(M(h_b)=0\) is a simple zero,
\[
\rho_1'(h_b)=0,\qquad \rho_1''(h_b)\neq 0,
\]
so \(\rho_1\) has a strict local extremum at \(h_b\). Therefore
\[
\rho_1(h)-\rho_1(h_b)
\]
has fixed sign on each adjacent interval and vanishes only at the boundary
point. Hence the vertical tangent line meets neither adjacent interval in its
interior.
\end{proof}

5. In В§12.4, replace the W=0W=0 obstruction remark with the stronger WвҖІ(hb)WвҖІ(hbвҖӢ) version
Find

\begin{remark}[Failure of the tangent-separator route at generic \(W=0\) boundaries]
\label{rem:w0-inflection-obstruction}
...
\end{remark}

Replace it with

\begin{remark}[The \(W=0\) obstruction and the cubic-inflection coefficient]
\label{rem:w0-inflection-obstruction}
Let
\[
x(h):=\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},
\qquad
y(h):=\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}.
\]
At a boundary point \(h_b\) with
\[
W(h_b)=0,\qquad M(h_b)\neq 0,
\]
the coordinate \(x\) remains a valid local parameter. Since
\[
\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}=\frac{c(h)^3W(h)}{M(h)^3},
\]
one has
\[
\left.\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}\right|_{h_b}=0.
\]
Differentiating once more with respect to \(x\) gives
\[
\left.\frac{d^3y}{dx^3}\right|_{h_b}
=
\frac{c(h_b)^5}{M(h_b)^4}W'(h_b).
\]
Hence, after translating the point to the origin and subtracting the tangent
line, the normalized curve has local expansion
\[
y
=
\frac{c(h_b)^5W'(h_b)}{6M(h_b)^4}\,x^3+O(x^4).
\]

Therefore a generic \(W=0,\ M\neq 0\) boundary is an ordinary affine inflection
unless additional special structure forces
\[
W'(h_b)=0.
\]
In particular, the tangent-separator route is not presently justified across
such boundaries, and the real remaining geometric question is whether the
actual triple \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) imposes any non-generic vanishing of
\(W'(h_b)\).
\end{remark}

\begin{lemma}[Parity elimination of pure same-point septic terms]
\label{lem:parity-elimination-pure-septic}
Let \(t_\pm=h\pm s/2\), and let the odd two-point germ be defined as the odd
part under the involution
\[
s\longmapsto -s
\qquad
(\text{equivalently }t_+\leftrightarrow t_-).
\]
Then any pure same-point septic contribution is even in \(s\), hence vanishes in
the odd two-point germ.

In particular, pure same-point septic terms do not contribute to the weight-one
part \([\Delta_7]^{[1]}\) of the septic residual determinant.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
A pure same-point term depends only on midpoint jet data at \(h\), i.e. on the
local one-point expansion evaluated at the midpoint, and carries no transport
factor coming from the ordered pair \((t_+,t_-)\). Therefore it is invariant
under exchanging the two points:
\[
t_+\leftrightarrow t_-.
\]
Since this exchange is exactly the involution \(s\mapsto -s\), every pure
same-point septic term is an even function of \(s\).

By definition, the odd two-point germ is obtained by projecting to the odd part
under \(s\mapsto -s\). Hence every even contribution vanishes in that
projection. Therefore pure same-point septic terms do not survive into the odd
pair germ.

Finally, \(\Delta_7\) is extracted from the odd pair germ after fixed-sector
projection and quotient normalization. Since pure same-point septic terms are
already zero in the odd germ, they cannot contribute to \([\Delta_7]^{[1]}\).
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Weight-one septic reduction to the direct \(K_1\) slice]
\label{cor:weight-one-septic-reduction}
Let \([\,\cdot\,]^{[1]}\) denote the part linear in the first genuinely new
same-point \(q^{(7)}\)-slice. Then
\[
[\Delta_7]^{[1]}=[\Delta_{7,K_1}]^{[1]}.
\]
Equivalently, the highest genuinely new \(q^{(7)}\)-coefficient of the septic
residual determinant comes only from the direct septic \(K_1\)-transport slice.
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
The first genuinely new \(q^{(7)}\)-slice enters the same-point expansion at
order \(5\), so
\[
X_j^{[1]}=0
\qquad (j<5).
\]
Hence every septic transport sector built only from lower same-point orders is
weight zero; in particular, the \(K_3\)- and \(K_5\)-transport sectors do not
contribute to \([\Delta_7]^{[1]}\).

By Lemma~\ref{lem:parity-elimination-pure-septic}, pure same-point septic terms
also do not contribute to \([\Delta_7]^{[1]}\), because they vanish already in
the odd two-point germ.

Thus the only septic sector that can see the weight-one \(q^{(7)}\)-slice is
the direct \(K_1\)-transport sector, and therefore
\[
[\Delta_7]^{[1]}=[\Delta_{7,K_1}]^{[1]}.
\qedhere
\]
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Exact identities for \(W\) and \(W'\)]
\label{lem:exact-W-and-Wprime-identities}
Let
\[
F(h):=(c(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h)),
\qquad
M(h):=c(h)v_5'(h)-c'(h)v_5(h).
\]
Then
\[
W(h)=\det(F(h),F'(h),F''(h))
\]
admits the exact expansion
\[
W
=
M\,\Delta_7''
+
(v_5c''-cv_5'')\,\Delta_7'
+
(c'v_5''-v_5'c'')\,\Delta_7,
\]
and
\[
W'(h)=\det(F(h),F'(h),F'''(h))
\]
admits the exact expansion
\[
W'
=
M\,\Delta_7'''
+
(v_5c'''-cv_5''')\,\Delta_7'
+
(c'v_5'''-v_5'c''')\,\Delta_7.
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Expand the determinant \(\det(F,F',F'')\) along the third column. This gives
\[
W
=
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
c & v_5\\ c' & v_5'
\end{pmatrix}\Delta_7''
-
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
c & v_5\\ c'' & v_5''
\end{pmatrix}\Delta_7'
+
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
c' & v_5'\\ c'' & v_5''
\end{pmatrix}\Delta_7,
\]
which is exactly the first identity.

For \(W'\), differentiate \(W=\det(F,F',F'')\). The terms
\(\det(F',F',F'')\) and \(\det(F,F'',F'')\) vanish by repeated columns, so
\[
W'=\det(F,F',F''').
\]
Expanding along the third column gives the second formula.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[The \(W=0\) obstruction and the cubic-inflection coefficient]
\label{rem:w0-inflection-obstruction}

or something close.
Make sure its body contains exactly this mathematical core

Let
\[
x(h):=\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},
\qquad
y(h):=\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}.
\]
At a boundary point \(h_b\) with
\[
W(h_b)=0,\qquad M(h_b)\neq 0,
\]
the coordinate \(x\) remains a valid local parameter. Since
\[
\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}=\frac{c(h)^3W(h)}{M(h)^3},
\]
one has
\[
\left.\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}\right|_{h_b}=0.
\]
Differentiating once more with respect to \(x\) gives
\[
\left.\frac{d^3y}{dx^3}\right|_{h_b}
=
\frac{c(h_b)^5}{M(h_b)^4}W'(h_b).
\]
Hence, after translating the point to the origin and subtracting the tangent
line, the normalized curve has local expansion
\[
y
=
\frac{c(h_b)^5W'(h_b)}{6M(h_b)^4}\,x^3+O(x^4).
\]

Therefore a generic \(W=0,\ M\neq 0\) boundary is an ordinary affine inflection
unless additional special structure forces
\[
W'(h_b)=0.
\]

1. In В§12.3, insert the parity lemma and weight-one reduction corollary
Where to insert

\begin{remark}[Audit: full septic fixed-sector coefficient]

This is the septic audit remark currently near the end of В§12.3.
Insert this exact LaTeX

\begin{lemma}[Parity elimination of pure same-point septic terms]
\label{lem:parity-elimination-pure-septic}
Let \(t_\pm=h\pm s/2\), and let the odd two-point germ be defined as the odd
part under the involution
\[
s\longmapsto -s
\qquad
(\text{equivalently }t_+\leftrightarrow t_-).
\]
Then any pure same-point septic contribution is even in \(s\), hence vanishes in
the odd two-point germ.

In particular, pure same-point septic terms do not contribute to the weight-one
part \([\Delta_7]^{[1]}\) of the septic residual determinant.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
A pure same-point term depends only on midpoint jet data at \(h\), i.e. on the
local one-point expansion evaluated at the midpoint, and carries no transport
factor coming from the ordered pair \((t_+,t_-)\). Therefore it is invariant
under exchanging the two points:
\[
t_+\leftrightarrow t_-.
\]
Since this exchange is exactly the involution \(s\mapsto -s\), every pure
same-point septic term is an even function of \(s\).

By definition, the odd two-point germ is obtained by projecting to the odd part
under \(s\mapsto -s\). Hence every even contribution vanishes in that
projection. Therefore pure same-point septic terms do not survive into the odd
pair germ.

Finally, \(\Delta_7\) is extracted from the odd pair germ after fixed-sector
projection and quotient normalization. Since pure same-point septic terms are
already zero in the odd germ, they cannot contribute to \([\Delta_7]^{[1]}\).
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Weight-one septic reduction to the direct \(K_1\) slice]
\label{cor:weight-one-septic-reduction}
Let \([\,\cdot\,]^{[1]}\) denote the part linear in the first genuinely new
same-point \(q^{(7)}\)-slice. Then
\[
[\Delta_7]^{[1]}=[\Delta_{7,K_1}]^{[1]}.
\]
Equivalently, the highest genuinely new \(q^{(7)}\)-coefficient of the septic
residual determinant comes only from the direct septic \(K_1\)-transport slice.
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
The first genuinely new \(q^{(7)}\)-slice enters the same-point expansion at
order \(5\), so
\[
X_j^{[1]}=0
\qquad (j<5).
\]
Hence every septic transport sector built only from lower same-point orders is
weight zero; in particular, the \(K_3\)- and \(K_5\)-transport sectors do not
contribute to \([\Delta_7]^{[1]}\).

By Lemma~\ref{lem:parity-elimination-pure-septic}, pure same-point septic terms
also do not contribute to \([\Delta_7]^{[1]}\), because they vanish already in
the odd two-point germ.

Thus the only septic sector that can see the weight-one \(q^{(7)}\)-slice is
the direct \(K_1\)-transport sector, and therefore
\[
[\Delta_7]^{[1]}=[\Delta_{7,K_1}]^{[1]}.
\qedhere
\]
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Exact identities for \(W\) and \(W'\)]
\label{lem:exact-W-and-Wprime-identities}
Let
\[
F(h):=(c(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h)),
\qquad
M(h):=c(h)v_5'(h)-c'(h)v_5(h).
\]
Then
\[
W(h)=\det(F(h),F'(h),F''(h))
\]
admits the exact expansion
\[
W
=
M\,\Delta_7''
+
(v_5c''-cv_5'')\,\Delta_7'
+
(c'v_5''-v_5'c'')\,\Delta_7,
\]
and
\[
W'(h)=\det(F(h),F'(h),F'''(h))
\]
admits the exact expansion
\[
W'
=
M\,\Delta_7'''
+
(v_5c'''-cv_5''')\,\Delta_7'
+
(c'v_5'''-v_5'c''')\,\Delta_7.
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Expanding \(\det(F,F',F'')\) along the third column gives
\[
W
=
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
c & v_5\\ c' & v_5'
\end{pmatrix}\Delta_7''
-
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
c & v_5\\ c'' & v_5''
\end{pmatrix}\Delta_7'
+
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
c' & v_5'\\ c'' & v_5''
\end{pmatrix}\Delta_7,
\]
which is exactly the first identity.

For \(W'\), differentiate \(W=\det(F,F',F'')\). The terms
\(\det(F',F',F'')\) and \(\det(F,F'',F'')\) vanish by repeated columns, so
\[
W'=\det(F,F',F''').
\]
Expanding along the third column gives the second formula.
\end{proof}

This is one of the biggest missing вҖңgoodiesвҖқ from the chats. It is the precise bridge from the W=0W=0 obstruction back to the septic package. It is not visibly present in the current draft.

jet contribution to \(W'\) is already governed by the weight-one septic

\begin{remark}[Audit: zero-capture in the turning-boundary adjacent theorem]
\label{rem:audit-zero-capture-turning-boundary}
The proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:adjacent-tangent-separator-sign-regularity}
invokes Lemma~\ref{lem:compact-first-zero-reduction} after choosing a compact
rectangle \(J_2\times J_1\subset I_2\times I_1\) containing one zero and
avoiding the boundary-near nonzero region. What remains to be justified fully is
a genuine zero-capture statement on the whole adjacent pair, i.e. a theorem
that all zeros of the tangent-separator kernel \(K(s,t)\) on \(I_2\times I_1\)
are captured in such a compact region under the stated turning-boundary
hypotheses. This is a proof-organizational gap in the current turning-boundary
argument, not an issue specific to the \(W=0\) case.
\end{remark}

This captures the remaining theorem-chain vulnerability without weakening the current theorem statement. It is a вҖңdonвҖҷt forgetвҖқ item and absolutely worth keeping visible.

ok check that everything is here, including all the goodies

IвҖҷm checking the final TeX against the specific late-stage additions: parity/weight-one reduction, exact W/WвҖІW/WвҖІ identities, the order-7-not-order-9 wording, and the zero-capture audit note.

I checked the latest TeX holistically, and almost all of the important later вҖңgoodiesвҖқ are now in the paper.

The big wins are there:

    the turning-boundary vs. W=0W=0 split is explicit,

    the W=0W=0 obstruction is tied to the cubic coefficient via WвҖІ(hb)WвҖІ(hbвҖӢ),

    the parity lemma and weight-one septic reduction are in,

    the exact identities for WW and WвҖІWвҖІ are in,

    the bottleneck/state remarks now say this still looks like an order-7 issue rather than a genuinely new primitive order-9 one,

    the optional septic K1K1вҖӢ trace-target remark is also present,

    and the zero-capture issue is now explicitly surfaced as an audit remark.

So the later chat results have been captured well.

There is, however, one important correction still to make.
The one thing that is still inconsistent

You made Theorem 12.47 conditional on the zero-capture hypothesis. Good. But Theorem 12.51 (вҖңPartial global no-secant theorem on turning-boundary generic blocksвҖқ) is still stated unconditionally, even though its proof invokes Theorem 12.47. In the current file:

    Theorem 12.47 is explicitly conditional on zero-capture,

    Remark 12.48 says zero-capture is still an audit issue,

    but Theorem 12.51 does not carry that same condition forward.

That is the one real remaining mismatch I see.
Exact fix

Replace the statement of Theorem 12.51 with:

\begin{theorem}[Partial global no-secant theorem on turning-boundary generic blocks]
\label{thm:partial-global-no-secant}
Assume every adjacent pair of ECT intervals is separated by a generic turning
boundary
\[
M(h_b)=0,\qquad M'(h_b)\neq 0,\qquad N(h_b)\neq 0,
\]
and assume moreover that the zero-capture hypothesis required in
Theorem~\ref{thm:adjacent-tangent-separator-sign-regularity} holds for every
adjacent turning-boundary pair.
Then no affine line in the chart
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},
\qquad
y=\frac{\Delta_7}{c}
\]
is \(2\)-secant to two distinct ECT intervals. In particular, there is no
global four-secant line for the normalized planar curve \(\Gamma(h)\) on such
a turning-boundary generic block.
\end{theorem}

And then replace the first line of the proof with:

\begin{proof}
Under the stated hypotheses, every adjacent boundary is of turning type and
Theorem~\ref{thm:adjacent-tangent-separator-sign-regularity} applies to each
adjacent pair.

That is the main correction.

You do not need to change the corollary after that, since it already depends on Theorem~\ref{thm:partial-global-no-secant}.
